**Practical Note (1 mark):** If neither 0.50 nor 0.51 mm is available, a stacked combination of 0.30 mm + 0.20 mm = 0.50 mm may be used (knowing the doubled uncertainty).

---

**(b) Stacked-Blade Measurement (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
When the required intermediate gap value is not present as a single blade in the set, two or more blades may be **stacked (piggy-backed)** on top of each other and inserted as a single unit. The resulting measurement equals the arithmetic sum of individual blade thicknesses.

**Derivation of Uncertainty (4 marks):**

Let the two blades have true thicknesses $t_1$ and $t_2$ with individual uncertainties $\Delta t_1$ and $\Delta t_2$.

The combined clearance:
$$C = t_1 + t_2$$

By the principle of **uncertainty propagation for additive quantities**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \frac{\partial C}{\partial t_1} \Delta t_1 + \frac{\partial C}{\partial t_2} \Delta t_2 \\
&= (1) \cdot \Delta t_1 + (1) \cdot \Delta t_2 \\
&= \Delta t_1 + \Delta t_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Substituting values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_1 &= \pm\,0.002 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta t_2 &= \pm\,0.002 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta C &= \pm\,0.002 + \pm\,0.002 \\
&= \pm\,0.004 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The stacked measurement has **twice** the uncertainty of a single-blade measurement. Hence, stacking is justified only when the OEM tolerance band exceeds $\pm\,0.004$ mm. For tighter specs (e.g., fuel injection shim settings), a single blade is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT state the range as 0.01 mm – 1.00 mm.** The lower limit is **0.02 mm** for standard sets. Students commonly lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Always mention the material** (hardened spring steel) when describing construction — examiners allocate marks for material science linkage.
> 3. **For stacked blades, write out the uncertainty derivation** explicitly; simply stating "error doubles" without the propagation equation earns only partial credit.
> 4. **Valve clearance procedure must include the re-verification step after lock-nut tightening** — most students forget this and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Conversion factor is exactly 25.4 mm/inch**, not 25 mm or 2.54 cm. Precision matters.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A feeler gauge is a set of calibrated steel leaves used to measure small clearances.
- **Standard metric range:** **0.02 mm to 1.00 mm** in 0.01 mm or 0.02 mm increments.
- **Standard imperial range:** **0.001 inch to 0.040 inch** (1 thou to 40 thou).
- **Material:** Hardened and tempered spring steel (AISI 1095 typical); HRC 58–62.
- **Working principle:** Match-by-drag (tactile feedback); light uniform drag = correct blade.
- **Key automotive applications:** Valve clearance, spark plug gap, ignition contact gap, piston ring end-gap, clutch free-play.
- **Conversion factor (must memorise):** $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$.
- **Stacking rule:** Clearance = sum of individual blade thicknesses; **uncertainty also adds**.
- **OEM valve clearance (typical petrol OHV):** Intake **0.15–0.20 mm**, Exhaust **0.25–0.30 mm**.
- **OEM spark plug gap (typical petrol):** **0.7–1.1 mm** depending on ignition system.
- **Always re-verify** clearance after tightening any lock nut — metal-on-metal seating shifts the reading.
- **Preferred over micrometers** for inaccessible gaps where a fixed anvil/spindle cannot reach.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for high-speed rotating gaps or surfaces with burrs/oxidation that distort the drag feel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Service, Repair and Overhauling: Basic Engine Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Engine Service, Repair and Overhauling: Basic Engine Parts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Engine Service, Repair, and Overhauling — Formal Definition In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT635)**, **Engine Service, Repair, and Overhauling** refers to the systematic set of maintenance procedures performed on an internal combustion engine to restore its original performance, reliability, and emission characteristics. These procedures are graded on a maintenance severity pyramid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)** > - **Engine Service (Routine Maintenance):** Scheduled preventive tasks such as oil change, filter replacement, spark plug cleaning, valve clearance adjustment, and timing inspection. Performed at manufacturer-specified intervals (e.g., every **5,000 km** or **6 months**). > - **Engine Repair:** Corrective maintenance targeted at a specific failed component (e.g., replacing a burnt valve, fixing a water pump, or rewiring a sensor). The engine is *not* fully dismantled. > - **Engine Overhauling (Major Repair):** Complete disassembly of the engine down to the bare block. All worn parts are inspected, measured, reconditioned (re-bored, re-ground, or re-machined), or replaced. The engine is reassembled to "like-new" or "zero-hour" condition. ### 1.2 Basic Engine Parts — Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy: "The Engine is a Living Organ"** > Think of the engine as the *heart* of a vehicle. Just like the human heart pumps blood, the engine converts chemical energy (fuel) into mechanical energy (rotation). The **cylinder block** is the *skeleton*, the **pistons** are the *muscles*, the **crankshaft** is the *spine* that converts up-down motion to rotation, and the **valves** are the *lips* that let air-fuel in and exhaust out. The **lubrication system** is the bloodstream, and the **cooling system** is the sweat that prevents overheating. Service keeps it healthy; repair fixes a sickness; overhauling is a complete organ transplant-level restoration. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) range for healthy engines:** **$220 \text{ g/kWh} - 280 \text{ g/kWh}$** - **Standard compression ratio for petrol (SI) engines:** **8:1 to 12:1** - **Standard compression ratio for diesel (CI) engines:** **14:1 to 22:1** - **Standard firing order for 4-cylinder inline engines:** 1-3-4-2 or 1-2-4-3 - **Normal operating coolant temperature:** **$85^{\circ}\text{C} - 95^{\circ}\text{C}$** - **Normal engine oil pressure at idle (warm):** **$1.0 \text{ bar} - 2.5 \text{ bar}$** (varies by engine) ### 1.4 Classification of Basic Engine Parts (KTU Module 1 Framework) | S.No | Sub-System | Constituent Parts | Primary Function | |------|------------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Stationary Parts** | Cylinder block, Cylinder head, Crankcase, Manifolds | Form the structural housing; contain combustion | | 2 | **Reciprocating Parts** | Piston, Piston rings, Wrist pin (Gudgeon pin), Connecting rod | Convert fuel energy to linear motion | | 3 | **Rotating Parts** | Crankshaft, Camshaft, Flywheel, Balancer shaft, Damper pulley | Convert reciprocating motion to rotation; store energy | | 4 | **Valvetrain Parts** | Intake valve, Exhaust valve, Valve springs, Rocker arms, Push rods, Tappets, Cam lobes | Time the opening/closing of intake & exhaust ports | | 5 | **Auxiliary Parts** | Gaskets, Seals, Bearings (main & big-end), Timing belt/chain, Tensioner | Seal, support, synchronize, and protect components | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Engine Maintenance Pyramid (Why Service is Graded) Engine maintenance is *not* a single action. It is a layered discipline, ordered by intervention depth: 1. **Level 1 — Daily/Weekly Checks:** Engine oil level, coolant level, visual leak inspection, belt tension. 2. **Level 2 — Routine Service (Minor):** Oil + filter change, air filter cleaning, spark plug check (every **5,000 - 10,000 km**). 3. **Level 3 — Intermediate Service:** Timing belt inspection, valve clearance adjustment, PCV valve, fuel filter (every **20,000 - 40,000 km**). 4. **Level 4 — Major Service:** Coolant flush, brake fluid change, comprehensive inspection (every **60,000 km+**). 5. **Level 5 — Repair:** Component-specific replacement. 6. **Level 6 — Overhauling:** Full engine teardown, machining, reassembly. > [!TIP] > **The "Why" Behind Grading:** Each level exists because of the engineering trade-off between *vehicle downtime*, *cost to owner*, and *risk of component failure*. The KTU examiner expects students to recognize that overhauling is the *last* resort, not the first. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Engine Nomenclature & Geometry) The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU Part B derivations. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application | |---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume | Determines SI vs CI engine classification | | 2 | **Swept Volume (per cylinder)** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | $D$ = bore diameter, $L$ = stroke length (m) | Engine displacement calculation | | 3 | **Total Displacement Volume** | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N_{cyl}$ | $N_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders | Total engine capacity (cc/litre) | | 4 | **Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)** | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_d}$ | $W_{net}$ = net work per cycle (J), $V_d$ in m³ | Engine performance indicator | | 5 | **Engine Power (Brake)** | $P_b = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m) | Output shaft power | | 6 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | $m_f$ = fuel mass, $CV$ = calorific value (J/kg) | Combustion quality check during service | | 7 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual air mass}}{\text{Theoretical air mass}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Indicates valve/port health | | 8 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | $BP$ = brake power, $IP$ = indicated power | Indicates friction losses in bearings/piston rings | | 9 | **Piston Speed (Mean)** | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | $L$ in m, $N$ in rpm | Limiting factor for high-speed engines | | 10 | **Firing Frequency (4-stroke)** | $f_{fire} = \dfrac{N}{120} \cdot N_{cyl}$ | $N$ in rpm | Determines vibration & smoothness | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitution Note for Tables:** All set notation, absolute values, and conditional bars in the equations above are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table grid during KTU PDF conversion. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compression Ratio (CR)** is the single most important parameter that determines whether an engine is petrol (SI) or diesel (CI). When a KTU examiner asks "Why is CR of SI engine lower than CI engine?", the answer is **knocking (auto-ignition) resistance**. Petrol has a low octane number, so high CR causes uncontrolled detonation. Diesel relies on *controlled* auto-ignition. - **IMEP** is used by service engineers to detect a *loss of compression*. A drop in IMEP with the same fuel input indicates worn piston rings, burnt valves, or head gasket leakage. - **$\eta_{mech}$** is the key parameter for overhauling decisions. If $\eta_{mech}$ falls below **$\sim 0.85$** for a worn engine, overhauling is economically justified. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Engine Displacement from Bore, Stroke, and Number of Cylinders This is a **standard KTU Part B derivation** worth 7 marks. **Given:** - Bore diameter of cylinder, $D$ (in metres) - Stroke length of piston, $L$ (in metres) - Number of cylinders, $N_{cyl}$ **To Prove / Find:** Total displacement volume, $V_d$ (in m³ or cc) **Step 1 — Geometric understanding:** Each cylinder is a hollow circular bore. The piston travels a distance equal to the stroke length. The volume swept by the piston in *one* cylinder is the volume of a cylinder of diameter $D$ and height $L$. $$ V_s = \text{Area} \times \text{Height} $$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of cylinder:** The cross-section is a circle. The area of a circle is $\pi r^2$, where $r$ is the radius. Since $D = 2r$, we substitute: $$ A = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 3 — Volume swept per cylinder:** Multiplying the cross-sectional area by the stroke length $L$: $$ V_s = A \times L = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L $$ **Step 4 — Total displacement for $N_{cyl}$ cylinders:** All cylinders share the *same* bore and stroke (in standard inline engines), so total displacement is simply $N_{cyl}$ times the swept volume of one cylinder: $$ V_d = N_{cyl} \times V_s = N_{cyl} \times \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N_{cyl}} $$ **Conversion to cubic centimetres (cc):** Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^6 \text{ cc}$, multiply the m³ value by $10^6$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Compression Ratio for a 4-Stroke Engine **Given:** Swept volume $V_s$ and clearance volume $V_c$. **Step 1 — Total volume when piston is at BDC (Bottom Dead Centre):** This is the *maximum* volume in the cylinder. It is the sum of swept volume and clearance volume: $$ V_{BDC} = V_s + V_c $$ **Step 2 — Minimum volume when piston is at TDC (Top Dead Centre):** This is *only* the clearance volume, since the piston has compressed all the swept volume out: $$ V_{TDC} = V_c $$ **Step 3 — Definition of compression ratio:** Compression ratio is the ratio of the *maximum* volume to the *minimum* volume: $$ CR = \dfrac{V_{BDC}}{V_{TDC}} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{CR = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently write $CR = V_s / V_c$ which is wrong by a factor of 1. The correct form always includes the $+V_c$ in the numerator. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Engine Parts and Their Failure Modes (Service Reference) This matrix is a **high-yield KTU topic**. The examiner can ask: *"List 6 basic engine parts and their common failure modes during operation."* | S.No | Engine Part | Material of Construction | Common Failure Mode | Service/Repair Action | |------|-------------|--------------------------|---------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Cylinder Block** | Cast iron / Aluminium alloy | Cracking, bore wear, ovality | Re-bore, sleeve insertion, welding | | 2 | **Cylinder Head** | Aluminium alloy | Warping, valve seat recession, cracking | Surface grinding, valve seat cutting | | 3 | **Piston** | Al-Si alloy (eutectic) | Crown carbon deposits, skirt wear, ring groove wear | Decarbonizing, replacement | | 4 | **Piston Rings** | Cast iron, chrome-plated | Wear, breakage, loss of tension | Replacement (always new on overhaul) | | 5 | **Connecting Rod** | Forged steel / forged aluminium | Bending, big-end bore ovality, bolt stretch | Replacement, big-end resizing | | 6 | **Crankshaft** | Forged steel / cast iron | Journal wear, cracking, deflection | Re-grinding to undersize, replacement | | 7 | **Camshaft** | Chilled cast iron / forged steel | Lobes wear, journal wear, cam nose pitting | Replacement (re-grinding uneconomical) | | 8 | **Valves (I/E)** | Heat-resistant steel (intake) / Stellite (exhaust) | Burnt head, stem wear, seat pitting | Refacing, seat cutting, replacement | | 9 | **Valve Springs** | Spring steel | Loss of tension, fatigue breakage | Replacement (mandatory on overhaul) | | 10 | **Timing Belt/Chain** | Reinforced rubber / steel chain | Tooth skip, elongation, breakage | Replacement at prescribed interval | | 11 | **Main & Big-End Bearings** | Steel-backed babbitt / aluminium-tin | Spalling, fatigue, copper exposure | Replacement (always new on overhaul) | | 12 | **Gaskets (Head Gasket)** | Multi-layer steel (MLS) / composite | Blow-by, coolant-oil mixing | Replacement (mandatory on overhaul) | | 13 | **Flywheel** | Cast iron | Ring gear wear, friction surface scoring | Ring gear replacement, re-facing | | 14 | **Tappets / Lifters** | Chilled cast iron | Flattened face, jamming | Replacement or cleaning | | 15 | **Rocker Arms** | Forged steel | Pivot wear, pad wear | Replacement | | 16 | **Push Rods** | Steel tube with hardened ends | Bending, tip wear | Straightening or replacement | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students write "re-grind" for parts that *cannot* be re-ground (e.g., aluminium pistons, polymer timing belts, valve springs). The examiner deducts 1 mark for every such incorrect recommendation. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU 7-Mark Problems) **Question:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **80 mm**, a stroke of **90 mm**, and a compression ratio of **9:1**. Calculate (a) the total displacement in cc, and (b) the clearance volume per cylinder in cc. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert dimensions to metres:** $$ D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate swept volume per cylinder:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 \times 0.090 $$ $$ V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.0064 \times 0.090 $$ $$ V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cylinder} $$ **Step 3 — Total displacement for 4 cylinders:** $$ V_d = 4 \times V_s = 4 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to cc (multiply by $10^6$):** $$ V_d = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 1809.6 \text{ cc} \approx 1.81 \text{ L} $$ **Step 5 — Find clearance volume using CR formula:** $$ CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} \implies 9 = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} $$ $$ 9 V_c = V_s + V_c \implies 8 V_c = V_s $$ $$ V_c = \dfrac{V_s}{8} = \dfrac{4.524 \times 10^{-4}}{8} = 5.655 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 56.55 \text{ cc} $$ **Final Answers:** - (a) Total displacement ≈ **1809.6 cc** (or **1.81 L**) - (b) Clearance volume per cylinder ≈ **56.55 cc** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 4-Stroke SI Engine The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional interactions of the basic engine parts — specifically how the **stationary, reciprocating, rotating, and valvetrain subsystems** work together to convert fuel into rotary motion. This block-level architecture is the KTU-recommended way to represent the engine when a physical cross-section drawing is not feasible. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph STN[STATIONARY SUBSYSTEM] A1[Cylinder Block
Cast Iron or Al Alloy] A2[Cylinder Head
Al Alloy] A3[Intake Manifold] A4[Exhaust Manifold] A5[Crankcase Lower Half
Oil Sump] end subgraph REC[RECIPROCATING SUBSYSTEM] B1[Piston
Al-Si Alloy] B2[Piston Rings
Top Compression, Oil Control] B3[Gudgeon Pin
Wrist Pin] B4[Connecting Rod
Forged Steel] end subgraph ROT[ROTATING SUBSYSTEM] C1[Crankshaft
Forged Steel] C2[Flywheel
Cast Iron] C3[Damper Pulley
Rubber-Steel] end subgraph VAL[VALVETRAIN SUBSYSTEM] D1[Camshaft
Chilled Cast Iron] D2[Tappet or Lifter] D3[Push Rod] D4[Rocker Arm] D5[Intake Valve] D6[Exhaust Valve] D7[Valve Spring] end subgraph AUX[AUXILIARY SUBSYSTEM] E1[Timing Chain or Belt] E2[Tensioner] E3[Main Bearings] E4[Big End Bearing] E5[Head Gasket MLS] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 A2 --> D5 A2 --> D6 D7 --> D5 D7 --> D6 D4 --> D5 D4 --> D6 D3 --> D4 D2 --> D3 D1 --> D2 E1 --> D1 E1 --> C1 E3 --> C1 E4 --> B4 E5 --> A2 A3 --> D5 D6 --> A4 ``` --- ### 4.2 Engine Service Decision Flowchart (Block Diagram) This flowchart captures the *logic* a service engineer follows when an engine is brought for maintenance — mapping the decision tree from inspection to overhauling. ```mermaid graph TD S0[Engine Brought for Service] --> S1{Visual Inspection
Leaks, Smoke, Noise} S1 --> S2[Oil and Coolant Level Check] S2 --> S3{Compression Test
and Leak Down Test} S3 --> S4{Compression Loss
Greater Than 20 Percent} S4 --> S5[No Major Fault
Routine Service Only] S4 --> S6[Suspect Rings Valves
or Head Gasket] S6 --> S7{Remove Engine
Required} S7 --> S8[Repair at Vehicle
Top Side Work] S7 --> S9[Engine Removal
Partial Teardown] S9 --> S10{Bore Wear
Greater Than 0.10 mm} S10 --> S11[Component Level
Repair] S10 --> S12[Full Engine
Overhauling] S12 --> S13[Rebore Regrind
New Bearings and Gaskets] S13 --> S14[Engine Reassembly
and Dyno Test] ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Read These Diagrams in KTU Exams:** The KTU examiner awards 1-2 marks for a *labelled, logical* block diagram. Always include arrows indicating flow direction, use rectangles for processes, and diamonds for decisions. Do not waste time drawing a perfect physical cross-section in a 14-mark question — the block diagram above is academically equivalent and faster. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Engine Overhauling". How is it different from routine engine service?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Engine overhauling is the process of complete disassembly of an engine, followed by inspection, measurement, reconditioning (re-boring, re-grinding, or re-machining), and replacement of worn parts with new or re-manufactured components, finally reassembling the engine to its original "zero-hour" specification. (2 marks) **Difference from routine service:** | Aspect | Routine Service | Overhauling | |--------|----------------|-------------| | Disassembly | None / minor | Complete teardown | | Cost | Low | High (3-5x) | | Frequency | Every 5,000 - 10,000 km | Every 100,000 - 150,000 km | | Parts replaced | Filters, oil, plugs | Bearings, rings, gaskets, seals | (1 mark for the comparative distinction) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any six basic parts of an internal combustion engine and state the function of each in one line.* **Model Answer (6 items × 0.5 mark = 3 marks):** 1. **Cylinder Block** — Houses the cylinders and supports the crankshaft and head. 2. **Piston** — Receives combustion pressure and transmits force to the connecting rod. 3. **Connecting Rod** — Links the piston to the crankshaft, converting linear to rotary motion. 4. **Crankshaft** — Converts reciprocating motion of the piston into rotary motion. 5. **Camshaft** — Operates the intake and exhaust valves at correct timing. 6. **Flywheel** — Stores rotational energy and smooths out power delivery between firing strokes. 7. **Valve Springs** *(optional)* — Close the valves when cam lobe rotates away. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine. List any **8** basic engine parts with their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine has a bore of **85 mm** and a stroke of **100 mm**. The clearance volume per cylinder is **70 cc**. Calculate: (i) Swept volume per cylinder, (ii) Compression ratio, (iii) Total displacement in litres. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** **Construction (3 marks):** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine consists of: - A **cylinder block** (cast iron / aluminium) with 4 parallel bores. - A **cylinder head** mounted on top, containing intake and exhaust ports, valves, spark plug, and camshaft housing. - **Pistons** fitted in each bore, connected via **gudgeon pin** to the **connecting rods**. - The lower end of the connecting rod is bolted to the **crankshaft** through big-end bearings. - A **crankcase** (lower half of block) holds the crankshaft in **main bearings**. - A **camshaft** (overhead or side) drives the **valves** through tappets, push rods, and rocker arms. - A **flywheel** is bolted to the rear end of the crankshaft. - **Intake and exhaust manifolds** are bolted to the head. **Working — 4 strokes (3 marks):** 1. **Intake Stroke:** Piston moves TDC → BDC. Intake valve opens. Air-fuel mixture is drawn in. 2. **Compression Stroke:** Piston moves BDC → TDC. Both valves close. Mixture is compressed (~9:1). 3. **Power Stroke:** Spark plug fires near TDC. Combustion raises pressure to ~30-40 bar. Piston is pushed to BDC, producing work. 4. **Exhaust Stroke:** Piston moves BDC → TDC. Exhaust valve opens. Spent gases are pushed out. **8 Basic Parts with Functions (1 mark — tabulated):** | Part | Function | |------|----------| | Cylinder Block | Structural housing for cylinders | | Cylinder Head | Seals the top; houses valves and spark plug | | Piston | Receives combustion force | | Connecting Rod | Transmits force from piston to crank | | Crankshaft | Converts reciprocating to rotary motion | | Camshaft | Times the valve operation | | Valves (I/E) | Control gas exchange | | Flywheel | Stores energy, smooths rotation | --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $N_{cyl} = 4$, Bore $D = 85 \text{ mm} = 0.085 \text{ m}$, Stroke $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $V_c = 70 \text{ cc} = 70 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$ **Step (i) — Swept volume per cylinder:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.085)^2 \times 0.100 $$ $$ V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.007225 \times 0.100 = 5.674 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 567.4 \text{ cc} $$ **Step (ii) — Compression ratio:** $$ CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{567.4 + 70}{70} = \dfrac{637.4}{70} = 9.105 $$ $$ \boxed{CR \approx 9.1 : 1} $$ **Step (iii) — Total displacement:** $$ V_d = N_{cyl} \times V_s = 4 \times 567.4 = 2269.6 \text{ cc} \approx 2.27 \text{ L} $$ $$ \boxed{V_d \approx 2.27 \text{ L}} $$ --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Differentiate between **engine service, engine repair, and engine overhauling** with suitable examples. State **when** an engine should be overhauled. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a 4-stroke SI engine. List the **stationary, reciprocating, and rotating** parts separately. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** **Service (2 marks):** Scheduled preventive maintenance done at fixed intervals. Example: Oil + filter change every 5,000 km, spark plug cleaning every 10,000 km. No dismantling of major assemblies. **Repair (2 marks):** Corrective maintenance to fix a *specific* failed component. Example: Replacing a leaking water pump, a burnt ignition coil, a cracked manifold. Partial disassembly only. **Overhauling (2 marks):** Complete engine teardown and rebuild. Example: Re-boring worn cylinders, regrinding the crankshaft, replacing piston rings, main bearings, and gaskets. Engine is restored to "zero-hour" condition. **When to overhaul (1 mark):** - Compression loss > **20%** across one or more cylinders - Excessive oil consumption (> **1 L/1000 km**) - Excessive blue/white smoke - $\eta_{mech} < 0.85$ or knocking that cannot be fixed by other means - High crankcase pressure (blow-by) --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** A 4-stroke SI engine block diagram must show: - Air-fuel mixture → intake manifold → intake valve → cylinder (combustion) - Combustion gases → exhaust valve → exhaust manifold → muffler - Piston ↔ connecting rod ↔ crankshaft (mechanical linkage) - Camshaft ↔ valves (valvetrain control) - Lubrication circuit (oil pump → galleries → bearings → sump) - Cooling circuit (water jacket → pump → radiator) *(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the detailed Mermaid block diagram — KTU accepts either a hand-drawn or Mermaid equivalent block diagram.)* **Parts Classification (4 marks):** | Category | Parts | |----------|-------| | **Stationary** | Cylinder block, Cylinder head, Crankcase, Intake manifold, Exhaust manifold | | **Reciprocating** | Piston, Piston rings, Gudgeon pin, Connecting rod | | **Rotating** | Crankshaft, Camshaft, Flywheel, Balancer shaft, Damper pulley | | **Valvetrain** | Valves, Springs, Rocker arms, Push rods, Tappets, Camshaft lobes | --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic (Common Pitfalls):** > 1. **Conflating "Repair" with "Overhauling" (–2 marks):** Repair is component-specific. Overhauling is the *entire* engine. Examiners deduct heavily if the student uses these terms interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in compression ratio (–1 mark):** Writing $CR = V_s / V_c$ instead of $(V_s + V_c)/V_c$ is a classic error. > 3. **Wrong unit conversion (–1 mark):** $1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^6 \text{ cc}$. Students often write $10^3$ and produce a displacement value 1000× too small. > 4. **Omitting valves and springs as separate parts (–1 mark):** Many students list "valve" as one part, but KTU expects *intake* and *exhaust* valves as separate entries, plus valve springs. > 5. **Not stating materials (–0.5 mark per part):** In the analytical matrix question, mentioning that the crankshaft is *forged steel* (not just "metal") earns a bonus mark. > 6. **Drawing poor diagrams (–1 mark):** Use a *labelled block diagram* with arrows. Do not attempt a freehand cross-section unless you have practised it. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Engine Service < Engine Repair < Engine Overhauling** in terms of intervention depth, cost, and time. - **Service** is *preventive*; **Repair** is *corrective*; **Overhauling** is *restorative*. - **Cylinder block** is the main structural element; **head** seals the top; **crankcase** is the lower half. - **Piston + Connecting rod + Crankshaft** form the *reciprocating-to-rotary* conversion chain. - **Camshaft + Valves + Springs** form the *valvetrain* — the "breathing" system of the engine. - **Gaskets and bearings** are *always replaced* during overhauling — never reused. - **Compression Ratio (CR)** for petrol engines is **8:1 to 12:1**; for diesel engines, **14:1 to 22:1**. - **Total Displacement Formula:** $V_d = (\pi / 4) \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N_{cyl}$ - **Compression Ratio Formula:** $CR = (V_s + V_c) / V_c$ — remember the **+1** in the expanded form. - **Standard Firing Order** for 4-cyl inline: 1-3-4-2 (most common) or 1-2-4-3. - **Materials Memory Aid:** Block = cast iron/Al; Head = Al alloy; Piston = Al-Si; Crank = forged steel; Cam = chilled CI; Valves = heat-resistant steel; Bearings = babbitt/Al-Sn. - **Service Intervals Memory Aid:** Oil = 5,000 km; Air filter = 10,000 km; Coolant = 2 years; Timing belt = 60,000-100,000 km; Spark plug = 30,000 km (copper) / 100,000 km (iridium). - **Overhauling Triggers:** Compression loss > 20%, oil consumption > 1 L/1000 km, persistent knocking, excessive blow-by, $\eta_{mech} < 0.85$. - **Always answer with both a definition and an example** for any 3-mark KTU question — definitions alone fetch only 1.5-2 marks. - **Block diagrams with arrows** are accepted and preferred for 14-mark questions where a physical drawing is complex.

topics.title: Engine Disassembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Engine Disassembly # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Engine Disassembly** is the systematic, sequential dismantling of an internal combustion engine into its constituent sub-assemblies and individual components, performed in a controlled workshop environment to facilitate inspection, diagnosis, repair, reconditioning, or complete overhaul of the power unit. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT635)**, engine disassembly forms the **first critical phase of an Engine Overhaul (EOH)** procedure. It is the precursor to engine cleaning, dimensional inspection, component testing, and subsequent reassembly. The operation must follow a **reverse-manufacturing sequence** — opposite to the order in which the engine was originally built — to avoid damaging mating surfaces, hidden fasteners, or precision components such as bearings, pistons, and the cylinder bore. The standard industrial classification recognizes **two levels of disassembly**: - **Partial / Top-End Disassembly:** Removal of cylinder head, valve train, and timing components only. Performed for minor services such as valve-seat recutting, head gasket replacement, or timing belt service. - **Complete / Full Engine Disassembly:** Stripping the engine down to the bare **short block** (cylinder block + crankshaft only). Performed during a full rebuild or when bottom-end damage (e.g., spun bearing, cracked block) is suspected. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *list the sequence of operations*, *identify the special tools used*, and *state the safety precautions* observed during engine disassembly. This topic carries direct weightage in **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** Part A questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Surgeon's Approach" Think of engine disassembly like a **cardiac surgeon opening a patient's chest**: 1. **Pre-operative preparation** — Just as a surgeon reviews the patient's history, diagnoses the problem, sterilizes instruments, and prepares the operating theater, a technician first conducts a **compression test, oil analysis, and borescope inspection**, gathers the right tools, and cleans the work area. 2. **External connections first** — The surgeon disconnects monitoring cables and IV lines before opening the body cavity. Similarly, the mechanic first removes **exterior ancillaries** — battery, air intake, exhaust manifold, cooling hoses, fuel lines, and wiring harness. 3. **Layered opening** — The surgeon never cuts randomly; every incision follows a planned path. Likewise, the engine is opened in a fixed, documented sequence so that no hidden bolt is sheared and no fragile component (e.g., aluminum head, sensor wiring) is cracked. 4. **Labelling & documentation** — Surgeons photograph each step; technicians **tag, bag, and rack** every fastener, gasket, and sub-assembly so reassembly is foolproof. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for remembering disassembly order — "ETBOC":** **E**xterior parts → **T**op end (head, valves, camshaft) → **B**ottom end (oil pan, pump) → **O**ut the pistons & rods → **C**rankshaft out. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **industry-standard values** are referenced throughout engine disassembly procedures: - **Workshop ambient temperature:** **20 °C – 25 °C** (for thermal expansion-controlled operations). - **Recommended disassembly torque-record accuracy:** **± 2 %** of original tightening torque. - **Standard fastener inspection stretch limit (TTY bolts):** **0.2 mm** — bolts stretched beyond this **must be discarded**. - **Maximum allowable oil-pan bolt re-use cycles:** **3 cycles** (industry best practice for non-TTY fasteners). - **Cylinder bore out-of-round limit (typical 4-cyl petrol):** **0.05 mm (50 µm)** — anything beyond requires rebore. - **Cleanliness target (post-cleaning):** ISO **4406 : 99** cleanliness code (residual particle limit < **15 µm**). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix ## 2.1 The Five-Phase Disassembly Logic Every professional engine disassembly, regardless of engine type (inline-4, V6, V8, boxer, or diesel), follows a **universal five-phase logic**. Understanding the *why* behind each phase is what separates a skilled KTU-grade technician from a parts-replacer. ### Phase 1 — Pre-Disassembly Preparation (Diagnostic Phase) **Why it matters:** You cannot fix what you have not measured. - Conduct **pre-disassembly diagnostic tests**: - **Compression test** — identifies worn rings, burnt valves, head gasket failure. - **Leak-down test** — quantifies percentage leakage per cylinder. - **Oil-pressure test** — flags worn bearings or a weak oil pump. - **Cylinder leak-down / borescope inspection** — visualizes internal damage. - **Drain all fluids:** Engine oil, coolant (from radiator + block drain plug), fuel (relieve fuel-system pressure first), and AC refrigerant (by a certified technician only). - **Remove the engine from the vehicle** if a full rebuild is needed; otherwise, perform in-situ partial disassembly. - **Photograph and document** every step; create a "disassembly log." ### Phase 2 — External Accessories Removal (Peripheral Strip-Down) **Why it matters:** These components obstruct access to the main engine and have their own wiring/fittings that can be damaged. - Disconnect the **battery negative terminal first** (always). - Remove: air-cleaner assembly, throttle body / intake manifold, exhaust manifold, alternator, starter motor, A/C compressor (without disconnecting refrigerant lines — leave them attached to the compressor and *set the compressor aside* with lines still connected), power-steering pump, fuel injectors & fuel rail, spark plugs, ignition coils, and the entire **engine wiring harness**. - Tag every connector with **colour-coded tape** and a label. ### Phase 3 — Top-End Disassembly (Cylinder Head & Valve Train) **Why it matters:** The cylinder head is the most precision-machined component; it must be removed without warping. 1. Set engine to **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** of cylinder #1 on the compression stroke — aligns timing marks. 2. Remove **valve cover / rocker cover** (typically 8–10 bolts, replace gasket). 3. Remove **timing belt / chain cover**, then the timing belt or chain (note the cam and crank alignment marks). 4. Loosen **cylinder head bolts** in the **reverse of the tightening sequence** (outside-in, in 3 stages: 50 %, 80 %, 100 % release) — using a calibrated torque wrench. 5. Lift the **cylinder head** straight up with a head-lifting fixture — **never pry**, as the aluminum alloy can warp. 6. Remove **camshaft(s)**, rocker arms / followers, and lifters — store in a **camshaft rack** in firing order. 7. Push out valves using a **valve spring compressor**; keep valves with their springs and retainers in labelled bags. ### Phase 4 — Bottom-End Disassembly (Piston, Rod, Crankshaft) **Why it matters:** Bottom-end wear determines engine life; this is where bearing failures occur. 1. Remove the **oil pan** (lower-end bolts first, work towards centre to avoid cracking the pan flange). 2. Remove the **oil pump assembly** (usually mounted inside the oil pan or behind the front cover). 3. Remove **connecting-rod caps** — note the **cylinder number and orientation** (most rods have a "FRONT" stamp; cap must return to its original rod). 4. Push the **pistons & connecting rods** out through the top of the cylinder bores using a **wooden hammer handle** — *never* a metal rod, which scores the bore wall. 5. Remove the **main bearing caps** in sequence (typically 5 caps on an inline-4) and lift out the **crankshaft**. 6. Remove the **rear main seal housing** and **front timing cover** if required. 7. Remove **piston rings** using a dedicated **ring expander tool**. ### Phase 5 — Cleaning, Inspection & Storage - **Clean** every component using a parts washer with **petroleum-based solvent or aqueous alkaline cleaner** at **60–80 °C**. - **Inspect** with micrometers, dial bore gauges, plastigauge, and magnetic-particle crack detection. - **Store** parts in labelled bins / trays; never pile them on top of each other. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix — Tools & Equipment The following table summarizes every **special tool** a KTU student must know for this topic: | Tool Name | Function / Use | Stage Used | Special Caution | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Torque Wrench (Click-type)** | Controlled tightening / loosening of head & rod bolts | Phase 3 & 4 | Must be **calibrated annually**; never use for breakaway | | **Ring Compressor / Expander** | Installing/removing piston rings without breakage | Phase 4 & 5 | Use only the **correct size** for the bore | | **Valve Spring Compressor** | Compress valve spring to remove collets | Phase 3 | Use **overhead-type** for OHV engines | | **Piston Ring Groove Cleaner** | Removes carbon from piston ring lands | Phase 5 | Use **plastic or bronze** tools only — never steel | | **Cylinder Hone (Flexible-Ball Type)** | De-glazes / cross-hatches bore surface | Reassembly phase | Produces **60° cross-hatch** at **0.025–0.05 mm** depth | | **Bearing-Shell Puller** | Removes main & rod bearings without damage | Phase 4 | **Never reuse** shell-type bearings | | **Crankshaft Turning Tool** | Rotates crankshaft for piston installation | Phase 4 | Use the **front pulley bolt** with a long bar | | **Micrometer (0–25 mm)** | Measures journals, pistons, pins | Phase 5 | Read **to 0.01 mm** accuracy | | **Dial Bore Gauge** | Measures cylinder bore taper & ovality | Phase 5 | Zero-set on a **ring gauge** | | **Plastigauge (Plastigage)** | Measures bearing-to-journal oil clearance | Phase 5 | Do **not rotate crankshaft** after pressing plastigauge | | **Engine Hoist / Crane (≥ 1 Tonne)** | Lifts engine in/out of vehicle bay | Phase 1 | Inspect **chain & sling** for wear before every lift | | **Head-Lifting Fixture** | Removes cylinder head straight up | Phase 3 | Mandatory for **aluminum heads** | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Engine disassembly skill is the foundation of every **Engine R&D activity**, **automotive forensic engineering** (failure analysis), and **remanufacturing (reman) industry**. India's organized **engine-reman sector** is valued at over **₹ 8,000 crore (2024 estimate)** and employs thousands of KTU-trained engineers. Mastery of disassembly sequence directly reduces **mean-time-to-repair (MTTR)** and improves workshop **first-time-fix (FTF)** rate — both key **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in any modern automotive service centre. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Always remember that **disassembly is the inverse of the assembly sequence**. The assembly torque sequence for the cylinder head is typically **centre-outwards in 3 stages**; therefore, disassembly must follow the **exact reverse — outside-inwards in 3 stages**. Missing this single detail is the **#1 cause of warped cylinder heads** in Indian workshops. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Engine disassembly is a **procedural engineering topic** rather than a formula-driven one. However, the **torque-release sequence** and the **bearing-clearance calculation** are mathematical procedures that KTU examiners frequently test. The full analytical walkthrough is given below. ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Three-Stage Bolt Release Procedure When releasing cylinder head bolts (or any critical fastener under elastic preload), KTU workshop practice and every major OEM service manual (Toyota, Hyundai, Tata, Maruti) mandate a **three-stage release** rather than a single full-release. This prevents **elastic rebound**, **bolt stretching**, and **head warpage**. Let the original tightening torque be **$T_{final}$**. The release stages are computed as: $$ T_1 = 0.50 \times T_{final} $$ $$ T_2 = 0.80 \times T_{final} $$ $$ T_3 = 1.00 \times T_{final} \quad \text{(Full release)} $$ **Worked Example (KTU-style):** *Given:* A 4-cylinder petrol engine's cylinder head bolts are tightened to a final torque of **$T_{final} = 90 \text{ N·m}$**. Calculate the three-stage release torque values. **Step 1 — Identify the final torque:** $$T_{final} = 90 \text{ N·m} \quad \text{(given, from OEM spec)}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the first release stage (50 %):** $$T_1 = 0.50 \times T_{final} = 0.50 \times 90 = 45 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the second release stage (80 %):** $$T_2 = 0.80 \times T_{final} = 0.80 \times 90 = 72 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 4 — The third (final) release — fully loosen:** $$T_3 = 1.00 \times T_{final} = 90 \text{ N·m} \rightarrow \text{full release}$$ **Step 5 — Sequence:** The bolts must be loosened in this sequence — **{$T_1$ → $T_2$ → $T_3$ → full}** — applied across the cylinder head in the **outside-inward direction** (reverse of tightening). ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Bearing Oil-Clearance (Plastigauge Method) After disassembly, the **main bearing and connecting-rod bearing** oil-clearance is measured using **plastigauge**. The measurement procedure and its mathematical interpretation are as follows. **Step 1 — Plastigauge Reading.** Lay a strip of plastigauge across the bearing journal, install the cap, and torque to spec. Remove the cap; the flattened strip width is read against the **plastigauge envelope** (printed on the packet). **Step 2 — Clearance Lookup.** Match the widest flattened width (in inches or mm) to the envelope scale to obtain the **oil clearance** $C_{oil}$. The **clearance classification** for a typical passenger-car engine (journal diameter $D_j = 50 \text{ mm}$) is: $$ C_{oil} = 0.025 \text{ mm} \;\text{to}\; 0.076 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Compare against OEM Spec.** For a Maruti Suzuki K-series engine, OEM main-bearing clearance is: $$ C_{spec} = 0.020 \text{ mm} \;\text{to}\; 0.044 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Decision Rule:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } C_{oil} &< C_{spec,\,min} \rightarrow \text{Interference fit (too tight, overheating)} \\ \text{If } C_{spec,\,min} \leq C_{oil} &\leq C_{spec,\,max} \rightarrow \text{Serviceable} \\ \text{If } C_{oil} &> C_{spec,\,max} \rightarrow \text{Replace bearings / grind crank} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Comprehensive Disassembly Decision Matrix | Stage | Action | Tool | Torque / Spec | Common Fault if Skipped | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Disconnect battery **negative** terminal | Spanner (10 mm) | Hand-tight | Electrical short / ECU damage | | 2 | Drain engine oil | Drain pan + plug spanner (14/17 mm) | Plug torque **25–30 N·m** | Oil spillage, fire hazard | | 3 | Drain coolant | Drain petcock | Hand-operated | Coolant skin irritation | | 4 | Relieve fuel pressure | Fuel-pressure gauge / schrader tool | **350 kPa** max residual | Fuel spray, fire | | 5 | Remove intake & exhaust manifolds | Socket set (10, 12, 14 mm) | M8: **22 N·m**, M10: **45 N·m** | Intake leak, exhaust gas ingress | | 6 | Remove timing cover & belt/chain | Holding tool + spanner | Belt tension: deflection **5–7 mm** | Timing misalignment, valve-piston contact | | 7 | Loosen head bolts (3-stage) | Calibrated torque wrench | 90 N·m in 3 stages | Bolt stretch / head warp | | 8 | Lift cylinder head | Head-lifting fixture | N/A | Head bolt hole thread damage | | 9 | Remove camshaft(s) | Camshaft puller | Bearing-cap bolts **12 N·m** | Cam journal scoring | | 10 | Remove valves | Valve-spring compressor | N/A | Valve drop into cylinder | | 11 | Remove oil pan | Socket (10 mm) | **10 N·m** in criss-cross | Pan flange crack | | 12 | Remove oil pump | Socket (12 mm) | **22 N·m** | Pump gear damage | | 13 | Remove rod caps | Socket (14 mm) | **25 N·m** + 90° angle | Rod bolt stretch | | 14 | Push out pistons & rods | Wooden handle | N/A | Bore wall scoring | | 15 | Remove main caps & crank | Socket (17 mm) | **55 N·m** + 90° angle | Crankshaft journal damage | | 16 | Remove rings | Ring expander | N/A | Ring breakage, finger injury | ## 3.4 Safety & Precautionary Mathematical Limits The **maximum safe single-component lift weight** for an average 2-person workshop team, accounting for the human-factors safety factor of **$SF = 2.0$**, is: $$ W_{max,\,safe} = \frac{W_{rated}}{SF} $$ For a 1-tonne (1000 kg) rated engine hoist: $$ W_{max,\,safe} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{2.0} = 500 \text{ kg} $$ A typical 4-cylinder passenger-car engine weighs **120–180 kg**, well within the safe limit. However, a **V8 diesel engine** can weigh **220–280 kg**, requiring the rated **$W_{rated} \geq 560 \text{ kg}$** hoist to maintain the safety factor. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Engine Disassembly Master Flow The following **functional architecture flowchart** maps every step of the engine disassembly process, from the moment the vehicle enters the bay until the bare short-block remains on the workshop stand. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Vehicle Arrives at Bay]) --> nodeDiag{Pre-Disassembly
