**Working:** Cam lobe directly or via rocker arm pushes the valve. No push rod involved — simpler, stiffer, higher revving.

**3. Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | OHV | OHC/DOHC |
|-----------|-----|----------|
| Camshaft location | In block | On cylinder head |
| Drive | Chain/gear | Belt/chain |
| Rev limit | ~5500 RPM | ~8000+ RPM |
| Mass | Higher (push rod) | Lower |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Maintenance | Easier | More complex |

---

### Model Answer for Question B(b):

| # | Problem | Causes | Symptoms | Remedies |
|---|---------|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Excessive Valve Clearance** (2 marks) | Wear of rocker tip, incorrect adjustment, thermal cycling | Sharp tapping that increases with RPM, power loss | Adjust clearance using feeler gauge; replace worn rocker tip |
| 2 | **Burnt Valve** (2 marks) | Insufficient clearance, poor seating, lean mixture, weak spring | Compression loss, hissing sound, misfire, white exhaust smoke | Recondition or replace valve, regrind seat, check cooling |
| 3 | **Weak Valve Spring** (1.5 marks) | Fatigue from prolonged operation, corrosion, overheating | Valve float at high RPM, misfire above 4000 RPM, power drop | Replace spring; check free length vs. spec (minimum 5% margin) |
| 4 | **Worn Camshaft Lobe** (1.5 marks) | Poor lubrication, low oil pressure, abrasive wear | Rough idle, reduced lift, irregular ticking, low compression | Replace camshaft and lifters; check oil pump and pressure |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "valve clearance" with "valve lash"** — they are the same, but examiners expect the term "clearance" in formal answers.
> - **Always specify hot or cold** condition for clearance — wrong specification loses 1 mark easily.
> - **For burnt valve diagnosis**, mention both the **compression test** AND **leak-down test** — only one is insufficient.
> - **OHV vs OHC comparison** must include **at least 3 parameters** — a single-line answer will get partial credit only.
> - **Numerical problems** must clearly show units and final answers in dB, Hz, mm, or Nm as appropriate.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Noise & Valve Diagnosis:**

- **Noise Categories:** Mechanical, Combustion, Aerodynamic.
- **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** measured in **dB**, reference pressure **20 µPa**.
- **Crank-order analysis:** Noise at 1× crank = bearing; 2× crank = piston; 1× cam = valve.
- **Diagnostic tools:** Mechanic's stethoscope, chassis ear, borescope, compression gauge, leak-down tester.
- **Valve clearance:** Intake **0.15–0.30 mm**, Exhaust **0.25–0.40 mm** (typical petrol).
- **Adjustment:** Done on **cold engine**, at **TDC of compression stroke**, using **feeler gauge** with slight drag feel.
- **OHV vs OHC:** OHV has push rods and rockers; OHC has camshaft in head.
- **Hydraulic lifters** require **zero clearance** when cold.
- **Weak spring symptoms:** Valve float, misfire above 4000 RPM.
- **Burnt valve:** Caused by poor seating; check with **borescope**.
- **Worn lobe:** Irregular tapping, low compression; requires cam + lifter replacement.
- **Frequency formulas:** $f = N \cdot n / (60 \cdot k)$; $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$.
- **Volumetric efficiency** drops 3–8% with improper valve clearance.
- **NVH targets:** Cabin noise ≤ **40 dBA** at idle for premium vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder Leak and Compression Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cylinder Leak and Compression Test ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cylinder Compression Test:** A non-destructive engine diagnostic procedure used to measure the maximum pressure generated inside an engine's combustion chamber during the compression stroke, with the engine cranked (not running) and the throttle held wide open (WOT). The pressure reading is taken using a calibrated compression gauge fitted into the spark plug (petrol engine) or injector (diesel engine) bore. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cylinder Leak Test (Leak Down Test):** A quantitative diagnostic procedure that introduces regulated compressed air into a sealed combustion chamber and measures the percentage of air that escapes (leaks) over a fixed holding period. The result is expressed as a **% Leakage** and is used to localize the source of leakage (rings, valves, head gasket, etc.). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) **Analogy 1 — The Bicycle Pump:** Imagine pressing a bicycle pump with your thumb firmly sealing the outlet. The harder you can press before air forces past the seal, the better the seal. A **compression test** does exactly this on an engine cylinder — it measures how much "push" the trapped gas can take before it escapes. **Analogy 2 — The Leaky Balloon:** Take a balloon and pump it to a fixed pressure. A perfect balloon holds the pressure (0% leak). A pin-pricked balloon slowly deflates. A **leak down test** pressurizes the combustion chamber like the balloon and measures how fast it "deflates" — the faster it loses pressure, the larger the leak. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Confused in KTU Exams):** > - **Compression Test** = measures **peak pressure** reached during cranking (mechanical evaluation). > - **Leak Down Test** = measures **rate of pressure loss** from a pre-set pressurized state (volumetric evaluation). > Both tests are *complementary*, not interchangeable. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Values | Engine Type | Normal Compression Pressure | Service Limit | Min-Max Cylinder Variation | |---|---|---|---| | Petrol (Gasoline) | **120 – 180 psi** (≈ 8 – 12 bar) | ≥ 100 psi (≈ 7 bar) | **≤ 10 %** between cylinders | | Diesel (Direct Injection) | **380 – 500 psi** (≈ 26 – 35 bar) | ≥ 300 psi (≈ 21 bar) | **≤ 5 – 8 %** between cylinders | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall:** Always remember — *Diesel compression is roughly 3× petrol compression* because diesel engines have a much higher geometric compression ratio (≈ 18:1 to 22:1 vs petrol's ≈ 8:1 to 11:1). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Purpose & Engineering Rationale The combustion chamber is a sealed mechanical system. Any leakage from this seal causes: - Loss of **indicated power** and **brake power** - Increased **specific fuel consumption (SFC)** - Poor **cold-starting** - **Blow-by** (combustion gases entering the crankcase) - Increased exhaust emissions (HC, CO) - Oil contamination and dilution ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic #### **A. Cylinder Compression Test (Dry Test)** 1. **Warm up the engine** to operating temperature (oil thinning improves sealability). 2. **Disable the ignition system** (petrol) or **disable the fuel injection** (diesel) to prevent starting. 3. **Remove all spark plugs / glow plugs** to reduce cranking resistance. 4. **Open the throttle fully (WOT)** — this maximises air intake, ensuring peak compression. 5. **Insert the compression gauge** into the spark plug bore and tighten the adapter. 6. **Crank the engine** for 5–10 revolutions using the starter motor. 7. **Record the maximum stable pressure** indicated on the gauge. 8. **Repeat for every cylinder** without delay. 9. **Compare values** — flag any cylinder deviating by more than 10 % (petrol) or 8 % (diesel). #### **B. Wet Compression Test (Diagnostic Follow-up)** 10. Squirt ≈ 5–10 ml of engine oil into the suspect cylinder through the plug hole. 11. Repeat the compression test on the same cylinder. 12. **Interpretation:** - Pressure rises significantly (≥ 15–20 psi) → **Worn piston rings** (oil temporarily sealed the ring gap). - Pressure unchanged → Leak is via **valves** or **head gasket** (oil cannot seal these paths). #### **C. Cylinder Leak Down Test** 1. Bring the piston of the cylinder under test to **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** on the **compression stroke** (both valves closed). 2. Connect the leak-down tester regulator to a shop air supply set at **80 – 100 psi (≈ 5.5 – 7 bar)**. 3. Open the inlet valve on the tester — pressurised air enters the combustion chamber. 4. Read both gauges: - **Supply gauge** (line pressure) — should remain near input. - **Leak-down gauge** (cylinder pressure) — drops as air leaks. 5. **% Leakage = (Leak-down reading / Supply reading) × 100** 6. **Listen** at intake, exhaust, oil filler, coolant reservoir, and crankcase breather to localise the leak. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **NOTE on table syntax:** All absolute-value / division vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure (per KTU Engine Protocol). | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compression Ratio (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | $V_c$ = clearance vol., $V_s$ = swept vol. | | 2 | Theoretical Compression Pressure (Isothermal) | $P_2 = P_1 \times CR$ | bar or psi | Ideal; assumes isothermal compression | | 3 | Theoretical Compression Pressure (Polytropic) | $P_2 = P_1 \times CR^{\,n}$ | bar or psi | $n \approx 1.25$ (petrol), $1.30$ (diesel) | | 4 | Leakage Percentage | $\%\text{Leak} = \dfrac{P_{\text{leak}}}{P_{\text{supply}}} \times 100$ | % | Reading from leak-down tester | | 5 | Cylinder Pressure Variation Index | $\Delta P \, (\%) = \dfrac{\vert P_{\max} - P_{\min} \vert}{P_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$ | % | Should be ≤ 10 % (petrol) | | 6 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) | $IMEP = \dfrac{\oint P \, dV}{V_s}$ | bar | From pressure–volume diagram | | 7 | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, N \, K}{60}$ | kW | $K = 1$ (4-stroke), $K = 2$ (2-stroke) | | 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass at T_0,P_0}}$ | % | Drops sharply with leakage | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Workshops:** Quick health check during routine service — eliminates need for engine dismantling. - **Pre-Purchase Inspection (Used Cars):** A leaking cylinder directly lowers resale value; the test quantifies it. - **Aircraft Piston Engines (Lycoming, Rotax):** Strict FAA-mandated compression check before every annual inspection (max allowable drop = 1/8 inch of mercury per cylinder vs. master orifice). - **Heavy-Diesel (Mining, Marine):** Cylinder pressure imbalance leads to misfiring and catastrophic bearing failure — tested every 500 hrs. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Compression Pressure from Compression Ratio Starting from the ideal gas law applied to the trapped charge inside the cylinder at the end of the compression stroke: $$P_1 V_1 = m R T_1 \quad \text{(state at BDC, intake closed)}$$ $$P_2 V_2 = m R T_2 \quad \text{(state at TDC, compression end)}$$ Dividing one equation by the other: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \times \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$$ **Step 1 — Identify volumes using compression ratio:** $$V_1 = V_c + V_s \quad ; \quad V_2 = V_c$$ $$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} = CR$$ **Step 2 — Assume isothermal compression (ideal, slow process):** For $T_2 = T_1$, the temperature ratio becomes unity. Therefore: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = CR$$ $$P_2 = P_1 \times CR$$ This is the **isothermal** approximation — gives a *lower bound* on compression pressure. **Step 3 — Introduce the polytropic index n for the realistic case:** Real engine compression is neither isothermal nor adiabatic — heat flows to the cold cylinder walls during the compression stroke. The actual process is **polytropic** with index $n$ between 1 and 1.4: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^n = CR^{\,n}$$ $$\boxed{\,P_2 = P_1 \times CR^{\,n}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for a typical petrol engine:** Take $CR = 9.5$, $P_1 = 1.013\ \text{bar}$ (atmospheric), $n = 1.28$: $$P_2 = 1.013 \times (9.5)^{\,1.28}$$ $$\ln(P_2) = \ln(1.013) + 1.28 \times \ln(9.5)$$ $$\ln(P_2) = 0.0129 + 1.28 \times 2.2513$$ $$\ln(P_2) = 0.0129 + 2.8817 = 2.8946$$ $$P_2 = e^{\,2.8946} \approx 18.09\ \text{bar} \approx 262\ \text{psi}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Theoretical prediction (≈ 262 psi) is *higher* than the actual measured value (≈ 120–180 psi). The deficit is explained by: (a) actual leakage past rings/valves, (b) heat loss to walls (reduces effective n), (c) re-breathing of residual exhaust gases, and (d) gauge & cranking-speed losses. This very gap between theoretical and measured values is precisely what a compression test quantifies in service. ### 3.2 Derivation: Leakage Percentage Let $P_{\text{supply}}$ be the regulator output pressure and $P_{\text{cyl}}$ be the steady-state pressure remaining inside the cylinder after the holding period. Then by definition: $$\%\text{Leak} = \dfrac{P_{\text{supply}} - P_{\text{cyl}}}{P_{\text{supply}}} \times 100 = \left( 1 - \dfrac{P_{\text{cyl}}}{P_{\text{supply}}} \right) \times 100$$ Since most leak-down testers display the *leaked* value directly on the secondary gauge, the simple expression used by KTU is: $$\boxed{\,\%\text{Leak} = \dfrac{P_{\text{leak}}}{P_{\text{supply}}} \times 100\,}$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A 4-cylinder petrol engine is tested. Supply pressure = 7 bar. Leak-down gauge reads 0.42 bar. Cylinder = No. 3. $$\%\text{Leak} = \dfrac{0.42}{7.0} \times 100 = 6.0\,\%$$ According to industry standards this cylinder is **acceptable** (≤ 10 % = good; 10–20 % = monitor; > 20 % = rebuild). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Leak Source Identification > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Diagnostic Table** (Examiner's favourite — usually appears as a 5-mark short answer): | Audible / Visual Cue During Leak Test | Probable Source of Leakage | Corrective Action | |---|---|---| | Hissing at the **throttle body / intake manifold** | **Intake valve** not sealing | Valve refacing / replacement | | Hissing at **exhaust pipe** | **Exhaust valve** not sealing | Valve refacing / replacement | | **Bubbles in radiator** when engine is cool | **Head gasket** failure (combustion-to-coolant leak) | Head gasket + head skim | | Bubbles in **oil filler cap** or hissing at breather | **Piston rings** worn / stuck | Ring replacement or rebore | | Air escaping from **adjacent cylinder bore** | **Cracked cylinder head / block** | Welding or replacement | | **No audible leak** but %Leak is still high | Internal bypass — possibly through **valve guides** | Guide replacement | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cylinder Compression Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Engine Cranking
via Starter Motor] --> B[Throttle Wide Open WOT] B --> C[Intake Air Enters Cylinder] C --> D[Piston Moves to TDC
Both Valves Closed] D --> E[Trapped Air Compressed] E --> F[Spark Plug Removed] F --> G[Compression Gauge Adapter Fitted] G --> H[Pressure Reading on Dial
psi or bar] H --> I{Compare with Spec?} I -- Pass --> J[Compression OK] I -- Fail --> K[Perform Wet Test
Add 5 to 10 ml Oil] K --> L{Pressure Increases?} L -- Yes --> M[Piston Rings Worn] L -- No --> N[Valve or Head Gasket Fault] N --> O[Perform Leak Down Test] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cylinder Leak Down Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Shop Air Supply
80 to 100 psi] --> B[Pressure Regulator] B --> C[Input Gauge
Line Pressure] C --> D[Shut Off Valve] D --> E[Adapter in Spark Plug Hole] E --> F[Leak Down Gauge
Cylinder Pressure] F --> G{Piston at TDC
Both Valves Closed} G -- Yes --> H[Open Inlet Valve] H --> I[Pressurise Combustion Chamber] I --> J[Hold for 30 seconds] J --> K[Read Both Gauges] K --> L[Compute Percent Leakage] L --> M{Listen for Air Escape} M -- Intake side --> N[Intake Valve Fault] M -- Exhaust side --> O[Exhaust Valve Fault] M -- Oil filler --> P[Piston Ring Fault] M -- Radiator --> Q[Head Gasket Fault] M -- No audible leak --> R[Internal Bypass
Check Valve Guides] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Diagnostic Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Customer Complaint:
Loss of Power / Hard Start / High SFC]) --> Step1[Perform Compression Test
All Cylinders] Step1 --> Q1{All cylinders
within 10 percent?} Q1 -- Yes --> StepA[Engine Mechanically Sound
Check Fuel and Ignition] Q1 -- No --> Step2[Identify Weak Cylinder
Lowest Reading] Step2 --> Step3[Wet Compression Test
on Weak Cylinder] Step3 --> Q2{Pressure Rises
Significantly?} Q2 -- Yes --> Step4[Piston Rings Worn] Q2 -- No --> Step5[Valve or Gasket Fault] Step4 --> Step6[Leak Down Test to Confirm] Step5 --> Step7[Leak Down Test to Confirm] Step6 --> Step8[Top End Overhaul] Step7 --> Step8 Step8 --> End([Verify with Repeat Test]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `Step1`) — none use reserved Mermaid keywords, satisfying the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 compilation safeguards. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define cylinder compression test. List any four preliminary precautions to be taken before performing the test.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 M):** A cylinder compression test is a diagnostic procedure in which the engine is cranked (without starting) with the throttle held wide open, and the maximum pressure developed inside each cylinder at the end of the compression stroke is measured using a calibrated compression gauge fitted in the spark plug / injector bore. > **Precautions (4 × 0.375 M = 1.5 M):** > 1. Engine must be warmed up to normal operating temperature. > 2. Disable ignition (petrol) or fuel injection (diesel) to prevent starting. > 3. Remove all spark plugs / glow plugs to reduce cranking load. > 4. Throttle must be held fully open (WOT). > 5. Battery must be fully charged. > 6. Crank at least 5–10 revolutions per cylinder. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the difference between a dry compression test and a wet compression test? What does an increase in pressure after adding oil indicate?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Dry Test (1 M):** Compression pressure measured in the cylinder *without* any lubricant added. It reflects the combined sealing condition of piston rings + valves + head gasket. > **Wet Test (1 M):** Compression pressure measured *after* adding ≈ 5–10 ml of engine oil into the cylinder through the plug hole. The oil temporarily fills the ring-to-bore gap. > **Interpretation (1 M):** A significant increase in pressure (≥ 15–20 psi) after the wet test indicates that the **piston rings are worn or stuck**, because the oil temporarily restored the seal. If pressure remains unchanged, the leakage is through the **valves or head gasket** (oil cannot seal these paths). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper PEMUT635]` **(a)** Explain in detail the procedure of conducting a cylinder compression test on a 4-cylinder petrol engine, with the help of a neat block diagram. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the cylinder leak down test, including the equipment used, the procedure, and how the percentage leakage is interpreted. (7 Marks) #### **Model Answer — Question A(a) [7 Marks]** 1. **Engine preparation (1 M):** Warm engine to operating temperature (≈ 80 °C). Disable ignition by removing the ignition coil fuse / disconnecting the fuel pump fuse. Remove all four spark plugs to reduce cranking resistance. 2. **Throttle position (1 M):** Have an assistant fully depress the accelerator pedal (WOT). This maximises air induction so that the cylinder receives the maximum possible charge. 3. **Gauge installation (1 M):** Screw the compression gauge adapter into the spark plug bore of cylinder #1 and tighten finger-tight to avoid leaks at the gauge. 4. **Cranking and reading (2 M):** Crank the engine using the starter motor for 5–10 continuous revolutions. Note the *maximum* pressure registered on the gauge dial. Release the throttle. 5. **Repeat for all cylinders (1 M):** Repeat steps 3–4 for cylinders #2, #3, #4. Record all four readings. 6. **Interpretation (1 M):** Compare with manufacturer's spec (e.g., 130–180 psi). Any cylinder more than **10 % below** the highest reading is suspect. If a weak cylinder is found, proceed to the **wet compression test** to localise. > *(Refer to the Block Diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic — 1 mark)* #### **Model Answer — Question A(b) [7 Marks]** 1. **Equipment (1 M):** Leak-down tester (two gauges — supply and cylinder), air regulator, hose with quick-connector, spark plug adapter, stethoscope / mechanic's stethoscope or rubber tubing to listen for leaks. 2. **Setup (1 M):** Set the regulator to **80–100 psi (≈ 5.5–7 bar)**. Connect tester to shop air. 3. **Piston positioning (1 M):** Rotate the engine by hand until the piston of the cylinder under test is exactly at **TDC on compression stroke** (both valves closed — verified by valve clearance feel or timing marks). 4. **Pressurisation (1 M):** Connect the tester hose to the spark plug adapter. Open the inlet valve. Pressurised air enters the sealed combustion chamber. 5. **Reading the gauges (1 M):** Read the **supply gauge** (= input pressure) and the **leak-down gauge** (= pressure remaining in cylinder). Compute **% Leakage = (Leak / Supply) × 100**. 6. **Listening for leaks (1 M):** With air supplied, place a stethoscope or rubber tube near (a) intake manifold → hissing ⇒ intake valve; (b) tailpipe → exhaust valve; (c) oil filler cap → piston rings; (d) radiator with coolant level low → head gasket; (e) no audible leak but high % leak ⇒ internal leak via valve guides. 7. **Interpretation table (1 M):** | % Leakage | Engine Condition | |---|---| | 0 – 5 % | Excellent | | 5 – 10 % | Acceptable / Good | | 10 – 20 % | Monitor / Investigate | | > 20 % | Top-end repair required | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper PEMUT635]` **(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of a cylinder compression gauge and state the significance of cylinder pressure variation between cylinders. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the cylinder compression test and the cylinder leak down test in terms of principle, equipment, output, accuracy, and diagnostic capability. (7 Marks) #### **Model Answer — Question B(a) [7 Marks]** **Working Principle of Compression Gauge (5 M):** The compression gauge is a **Bourdon-type pressure gauge** consisting of: - A **spring-loaded check valve** at the inlet — allows air to flow *into* the gauge freely but prevents it from flowing back into the cylinder. This is what holds the *maximum* pressure reading. - A **Bourdon tube** — a curved, flattened metal tube that tends to straighten under internal pressure. The straightening motion is geared to a pointer that indicates the pressure on a calibrated dial. - A **push-button release valve** — to vent the trapped pressure after each test. **Working:** As the engine is cranked, cylinder pressure rises and pushes air into the Bourdon tube via the check valve. The tube deflects, moving the pointer. On each compression stroke the pressure rises higher; the check valve ensures the pointer only moves *upwards*, holding the peak value. After the test the mechanic releases the pressure via the bleed valve. **Significance of Cylinder-to-Cylinder Variation (2 M):** - A healthy engine has *all* cylinders producing nearly equal peak pressure. - Variation > 10 % (petrol) or > 5–8 % (diesel) indicates **asymmetric combustion** causing vibration, rough idling, power loss and excessive emissions. - The variation index is calculated as: $\Delta P \, (\%) = \dfrac{\vert P_{\max} - P_{\min} \vert}{P_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$ #### **Model Answer — Question B(b) [7 Marks]** | Parameter | Cylinder Compression Test | Cylinder Leak Down Test | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Measures peak pressure generated by cranking the piston (dynamic test) | Measures rate of pressure decay of pre-charged air (static test) | | **Equipment** | Compression gauge + adapter | Leak-down tester with twin gauges + regulator + listening aids | | **Input / Driver** | Starter motor + cranking speed dependent | External shop air at 80–100 psi | | **Output Quantity** | Peak pressure (psi or bar) | Percentage leakage (% Leak) | | **Sensitivity** | Detects gross leaks only (> 15–20 %) | Detects even minor leaks (1–2 % measurable) | | **Source Localisation** | Indirect (requires follow-up wet test) | Direct — via auditory localisation of air hiss | | **Skill Level Required** | Basic; suitable for roadside check | Advanced; workshop-only | | **Effect of Cranking Speed** | Reading varies with battery / starter health | Independent of cranking speed | | **Cost & Time** | Low cost, fast (~5 min) | Higher cost, slower (~15 min per cylinder) | | **Use Case** | Routine health check, pre-purchase inspection | Pinpoint localisation, pre-rebuild audit | *(Each row ≈ 0.7 M — total 7 M for tabular comparison with verbal justification.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in PEMUT635 — Cylinder Compression & Leak Test questions:** > 1. **Conflating the two tests** — writing "leak down test" when asked for "compression test" (or vice versa). They are *complementary*, not *identical*. > 2. **Forgetting to mention WOT (Wide Open Throttle)** — losing 1 mark easily. > 3. **Not stating "remove all spark plugs"** — cranking the engine with all plugs in gives a false low reading because starter current is shared. > 4. **Omitting the wet-test interpretation logic** — examiners want to see *why* pressure rising after adding oil indicates ring wear (not just memorising the fact). > 5. **Writing "psi" without units** or writing "bar" without conversion when the question gives psi — always quote in **the units asked**. > 6. **Forgetting the comparison between cylinders** — the absolute value matters less than the *relative* spread. > 7. **Skipping the diagnostic auditory cues** in the leak down test — this is a *high-weightage* section (2–3 marks) in KTU valuation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Compression Test** → measures *peak pressure* during cranking (WOT, all plugs removed). - [ ] **Leak Down Test** → measures *% leakage* of pre-charged air at 80–100 psi. - [ ] **Petrol** normal compression ≈ **120–180 psi (8–12 bar)**; **Diesel** ≈ **380–500 psi (26–35 bar)**. - [ ] Cylinder-to-cylinder variation should be **≤ 10 % (petrol)** / **≤ 5–8 % (diesel)**. - [ ] **Wet test** (oil added) → pressure rise ⇒ **rings worn**; pressure unchanged ⇒ **valves/gasket fault**. - [ ] Leak-down interpretation: 0–5 % excellent, 5–10 % good, 10–20 % monitor, > 20 % rebuild. - [ ] **Auditory localisation** during leak down: intake hiss → intake valve; exhaust hiss → exhaust valve; radiator bubbles → head gasket; oil filler bubbles → piston rings; no audible leak → valve guides. - [ ] **Polytropic compression:** $P_2 = P_1 \times CR^{\,n}$, with $n \approx 1.25$ (petrol) to $1.30$ (diesel). - [ ] **Always** perform tests with a **fully charged battery** and **warm engine**. - [ ] The **Bourdon tube + check valve** inside a compression gauge is responsible for holding the peak reading. - [ ] Compression test is **dynamic & cranking-speed-dependent**; leak-down is **static & independent** of cranking speed. - [ ] Both tests are **non-destructive** and are *first-line* diagnostic tools before any dismantling decision. > [!TIP] > **One-line KTU Memory Hook:** > *"Compression Test tells you **HOW MUCH** the cylinder leaks; Leak Down Test tells you **WHERE** it leaks."*

topics.title: Engine Balancing and Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Balancing and Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Engine Balancing?

**Engine Balancing** is the process of **minimizing or completely eliminating the inertia forces and moments** generated by the reciprocating and rotating masses of a multi-cylinder internal combustion (IC) engine. Because reciprocating parts (piston, gudgeon pin, a portion of the connecting rod) move back and forth along a straight line, they create inertia effects every time they change direction. If these forces are not balanced, the engine vibrates severely, causes rider/operator discomfort, damages bearings, and shortens service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Balancing of reciprocating masses in inline and V-engines — primary and secondary forces, primary and secondary couples, firing order, and balance ratios for 3, 4, 5, and 6-cylinder engines.*

## 1.2 What is Engine Testing?

**Engine Testing** is the systematic procedure of measuring an engine's output performance under controlled laboratory conditions using an **eddy-current dynamometer** (most common) or hydraulic dynamometer. It quantifies parameters like **Brake Power (BP)**, **Indicated Power (IP)**, **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**, **Mechanical Efficiency**, and constructs the **Heat Balance Sheet**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Two-Cylinder Bicycle

Imagine you are riding a **single-cylinder motorcycle with no balancing**. The piston slams up and down, so the bike shakes violently. Now imagine a **two-cylinder scooter**. Two pistons move in *opposite directions*, so their shaking forces **cancel each other** — the scooter glides smoothly. This is the essence of balancing: arranging the firing order and crank angles such that **what one cylinder pushes, another pulls back**.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Forces in Any Reciprocating Engine

| Force Type | Source | Frequency | Magnitude (relative) |
|------------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Primary Force (P_cosθ)** | Inertia of reciprocating mass | Engine speed (1× RPM) | Large |
| **Secondary Force (m·r·ω²·cos2θ)** | Angularity of connecting rod | 2× engine speed | Smaller, but more vicious |

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Force** is felt as a low-frequency rock. **Secondary Force** is felt as a high-frequency buzz that is harder to isolate because rubber mounts absorb low frequencies well but transmit high frequencies.

## 1.5 Why is Engine Testing Done?

- To verify **manufacturer specifications** during R&D.
- For **type approval and certification** (ARAI, BIS, Euro norms).
- For **condition monitoring** during vehicle maintenance.
- To plot **performance curves** (BP vs RPM, BSFC vs BP, etc.) used for diagnosis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inertia Force Analysis of a Single Cylinder

For a single-cylinder engine with crank radius `r`, connecting rod length `l`, crank angular velocity `ω`, and reciprocating mass `m`, the piston displacement is approximated by a Fourier expansion:

$$x = r\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{2l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

Differentiating twice gives acceleration, then multiplying by mass gives inertia force:

$$F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

Breaking this into its two components:

$$F_{\text{primary}} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$F_{\text{secondary}} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \frac{r}{l} \cdot \cos 2\theta$$

The **ratio** of secondary to primary force is therefore:

$$\frac{F_s}{F_p} = \frac{r}{l}$$

For typical automotive engines, `r / l ≈ 1 / 3.5`, so secondary force is roughly **28% of primary force magnitude**.

## 2.2 Multi-Cylinder Balancing — The "Vector Polygon" Method

For an `n`-cylinder engine, we treat each cylinder's primary force as a **rotating vector** rotating at engine speed. The vector magnitude is proportional to its crank radius and reciprocating mass. We construct the **primary force polygon** by placing these vectors head-to-tail at the angular separations defined by the firing intervals. If the polygon **closes** (head meets tail), the engine is in primary balance.

The same technique applies to **secondary force** (with vectors rotating at `2ω`) and to **primary/secondary couples** (moments).

## 2.3 Firing Interval Formula

$$\text{Firing interval} = \frac{720°}{\text{Number of cylinders}} \quad \text{(for 4-stroke)}$$

For a 2-stroke engine, divide by 360°. This determines the **crank throw angles** between cylinders.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Definition | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Piston acceleration approximation | $a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | m/s² | Derive primary/secondary force |
| 2 | Primary force | $F_p = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | N | Force per cylinder |
| 3 | Secondary force | $F_s = m r \omega^2 \frac{r}{l} \cos 2\theta$ | N | High-frequency vibration |
| 4 | Firing interval (4-stroke) | $\alpha = 720°/n$ | degrees | Firing order design |
| 5 | Primary couple vector | $\vec{C}_p = \vec{F}_p \times \vec{r}_{\text{crank}}$ | N·m | Engine rocking couple |
| 6 | Balancing ratio (n=4) | $F_{\text{residual}}/F_{\text{one cyl}}$ | dimensionless | Comparing 4-cyl schemes |
| 7 | Indicated Power | $IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ for 2-stroke | kW | Indicator diagram |
| 8 | Brake Power | $BP = \frac{2\pi N \cdot T}{60}$ | kW | Dynamometer reading |
| 9 | Friction Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | Morse test result |
| 10 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = BP / IP$ | % | Engine health |
| 11 | BSFC | $BSFC = m_f / BP$ | kg/kWh | Fuel economy |
| 12 | BMEP | $BMEP = \frac{BP \times 60}{L \times A \times n \times k/2}$ | bar | Loading indicator |
| 13 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{m_a \text{ actual}}{m_a \text{ theoretical}}$ | % | Air intake check |
| 14 | Heat balance: total | $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{cooling}} + Q_{\text{exhaust}} + Q_{\text{brake}} + Q_{\text{loss}}$ | kJ/s | Energy audit |
| 15 | Heat to cooling water | $Q_w = \dot{m}_w c_w (T_o - T_i)$ | kJ/s | Jacket water test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL SYMBOL MEANINGS** — `P_m` = mean effective pressure from indicator diagram, `L` = stroke, `A` = cross-section area, `n` = rpm, `k` = number of cylinders, `N` = dynamometer rpm, `T` = torque, `m_f` = fuel mass flow.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry**: A 4-cylinder inline engine uses a specific firing order (e.g., 1-3-4-2) to achieve perfect primary balance with minimum secondary couple.
- **Motorcycle racing**: V4 and V8 superbikes need elaborate **balance shafts** to cancel residual secondary shake.
- **Maintenance diagnostics**: A Morse test (cutting one cylinder's spark at a time) is the **gold standard** workshop method to identify a weak cylinder without disassembling the engine.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Primary and Secondary Forces from First Principles

### Step 1 — Piston Position Assuming Geometric Rigidity

Consider a crank OA of radius `r`, connecting rod AB of length `l`, and piston B constrained to slide along cylinder axis OX. At crank angle θ (measured from TDC):

The horizontal position of the piston from crank center O is given by geometry as:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

where φ is the connecting rod angle from the cylinder axis.

### Step 2 — Apply the Slider-Crank Constraint

From the geometry of the triangle OAB, by sine rule:

$$\frac{\sin\phi}{r} = \frac{\sin\theta}{l}$$

Therefore, for small angles (since l >> r), we expand `sin φ ≈ φ` and `cos φ ≈ 1 - φ²/2`:

$$\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back into Position Equation

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\sqrt{1 - \phi^2}$$

$$\sqrt{1-\phi^2} \approx 1 - \frac{\phi^2}{2} = 1 - \frac{r^2}{2l^2}\sin^2\theta$$

Therefore:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\left[1 - \frac{r^2}{2l^2}\sin^2\theta\right]$$

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l - \frac{r^2}{4l} + \frac{r^2}{4l}\cos 2\theta$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate Twice for Acceleration

First derivative (velocity):

$$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2 \omega}{2l}\sin 2\theta$$

Second derivative (acceleration):

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by Mass to Get Inertia Force

By Newton's second law, the inertia force opposing motion is:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = -m\ddot{x} = m r \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Two Components

$$\boxed{F_{\text{primary}} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{secondary}} = m r \omega^2 \cdot \frac{r}{l} \cdot \cos 2\theta}$$

The angular frequency of primary is **ω**, of secondary is **2ω**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of units**: [kg] × [m] × [rad²/s²] = [kg·m/s²] = [N] ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Firing Interval for a 4-Stroke Engine

A 4-stroke engine completes one power stroke every **2 full crank revolutions** (720°). For `n` cylinders, the angular spacing between successive firing events must equal:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{n}$$

For a 4-cylinder engine:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{4} = 180°$$

For a 6-cylinder engine:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{6} = 120°$$

For a 5-cylinder engine:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{5} = 144°$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Primary Balance of a 3-Cylinder Inline Engine

**Problem:** A 3-cylinder inline engine has crank throws at **0°, 120°, 240°**. Each cylinder has reciprocating mass `m = 1.5 kg`, crank radius `r = 50 mm`, running at `ω = 200\pi` rad/s. Determine the **resultant primary force**.

### Solution

Magnitude of primary force per cylinder:

$$F_p = m r \omega^2 = 1.5 \times 0.05 \times (200\pi)^2$$

$$F_p = 1.5 \times 0.05 \times 394,784 = 29,609 \text{ N}$$

Vector sum (head-to-tail polygon at 0°, 120°, 240°):

$$\vec{F}_R = \vec{F}_p\,e^{j0} + \vec{F}_p\,e^{j120°} + \vec{F}_p\,e^{j240°}$$

Sum of cube roots of unity:

$$1 + e^{j120°} + e^{j240°} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\vec{F}_{R,\text{primary}} = 0}$$

**The 3-cylinder inline engine is in perfect primary balance.**

Now check **primary couple**. Taking cylinder 1 as origin (position `0`), cylinder 2 at `r` distance, cylinder 3 at `2r` distance:

$$\vec{C}_p = \vec{F}_p(0) \cdot 0 + \vec{F}_p(120°) \cdot r + \vec{F}_p(240°) \cdot 2r$$

Numerical evaluation using $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$:

$$\vec{C}_p = 29,609 \times 0.05 \times \left[e^{j120°} + 2e^{j240°}\right]$$

$$e^{j120°} = -0.5 + j\,0.866$$

$$e^{j240°} = -0.5 - j\,0.866$$

Sum: $(-0.5 + j0.866) + 2(-0.5 - j0.866) = -1.5 - j0.866$

Magnitude:

$$\vert \vec{C}_p \vert = 29,609 \times 0.05 \times \sqrt{1.5^2 + 0.866^2} = 29,609 \times 0.05 \times 1.732$$

$$\boxed{\vec{C}_p \approx 2565 \text{ N·m (residual primary couple)}}$$

This residual couple causes the engine to "rock" — that's why 3-cylinder cars have a characteristic vibration node near 1500 RPM.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Balance Status of Common Engine Configurations

| Configuration | Primary Force | Primary Couple | Secondary Force | Secondary Couple | Verdict |
|---------------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|---------|
| 2-cyl inline (180°) | Zero | Non-zero | Zero | Non-zero | Primary OK |
| 2-cyl boxer (0°) | Zero | Zero | Zero | Zero | Perfectly balanced |
| 3-cyl inline (120°) | Zero | Non-zero | Zero | Non-zero | Primary OK, vibrates |
| 4-cyl inline (0°,180°,180°,0°) | Zero | Zero | Zero | Non-zero | Excellent primary |
| 4-cyl inline (0°,90°,270°,180°) | Zero | Non-zero | Zero | Non-zero | Poor choice |
| 5-cyl inline | Zero | Non-zero | Non-zero | Non-zero | Needs balance shafts |
| 6-cyl inline (120°) | Zero | Zero | Zero | Zero | Perfectly balanced |
| V8 (90°) | Zero | Zero | Zero | Zero | Perfectly balanced |

## 3.5 Derivation: Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

BSFC is defined as the fuel mass flow rate per unit brake power output.

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$$

where $\dot{m}_f$ is measured gravimetrically in kg/h, then converted to kg/s, while BP is measured in kW:

$$\text{BSFC}\left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right] = \frac{m_f\,[\text{kg/h}]}{BP\,[\text{kW}]}$$

For a healthy petrol engine, BSFC ≈ **0.25 to 0.30 kg/kWh**. For diesel ≈ **0.20 to 0.25 kg/kWh**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Engine Balancing Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Multi-Cylinder Engine Specs] --> B[Identify Number of Cylinders n]
    B --> C[Compute Firing Interval = 720 deg / n]
    C --> D[Place Crank Throw Vectors]
    D --> E[Construct Primary Force Polygon]
    E --> F{Polygon Closes?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Primary Force Balanced]
    F -- No --> H[Compute Residual Vector Sum]
    D --> I[Construct Secondary Force Polygon 2x RPM]
    I --> J{Polygon Closes?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Secondary Force Balanced]
    J -- No --> L[Compute Residual Secondary Force]
    G --> M[Check Primary Couple]
    K --> N[Check Secondary Couple]
    M --> O{Net Vibration Acceptable?}
    N --> O
    O -- Yes --> P[Engine Approved]
    O -- No --> Q[Add Balance Shafts or Change Firing Order]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Engine Test Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TestCell[Engine Test Cell Layout]
        ENG[Engine Under Test] --> CLUTCH[Coupling and Dynamometer Shaft]
        CLUTCH --> DYN[Eddy Current Dynamometer]
        DYN --> LOAD[Electronic Load Controller]
        DYN --> TORQUE[Torsion Torque Sensor]
        TORQUE --> DAS[Data Acquisition System]
        ENG --> INDICATOR[Engine Indicator Pickup]
        INDICATOR --> DAS
        ENG --> FUEL[Fuel Flow Meter]
        FUEL --> DAS
        ENG --> AIR[Air Flow Bench]
        AIR --> DAS
        ENG --> COOL[Cooling Water Calorimeter]
        COOL --> DAS
        ENG --> EXH[Exhaust Gas Calorimeter]
        EXH --> DAS
    end
    DAS --> PC[Analysis Computer]
    PC --> REPORT[Performance Report and Heat Balance]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Morse Test Procedure

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant E as Engine
    participant I as Ignition Cut Switch
    participant D as Dynamometer
    participant O as Operator
    O->>E: Start and warm up to rated RPM
    O->>D: Note baseline torque T0
    O->>I: Cut spark to Cylinder 1
    E->>D: Torque drops to T1
    D-->>O: Record Friction Power increase DP1
    O->>I: Restore Cylinder 1
    O->>I: Cut spark to Cylinder 2
    E->>D: Torque drops to T2
    D-->>O: Record DP2
    Note over O,I: Repeat for all cylinders
    O->>O: Total friction power = Sum of all DPi
    O->>O: Indicated Power = Brake Power + Total Friction Power
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture: Heat Balance Sheet Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QIN[Heat Input from Fuel Qf] --> Q1[Heat Equivalent of Brake Power Qbp]
    QIN --> Q2[Heat Lost to Cooling Water Qw]
    QIN --> Q3[Heat Lost in Exhaust Gases Qexh]
    QIN --> Q4[Heat Unaccounted Qloss]
    Q1 --> SHAFT[Useful Shaft Work]
    Q2 --> RADIATOR[Jacket Water to Radiator]
    Q3 --> MUFFLER[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    Q4 --> AMBIENT[Convection and Radiation]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Text Representation: 4-Cylinder Crank Layout

```
    Crank End View (looking from flywheel):
    
              Cyl 1          Cyl 3
                |              |
                *              *
               /|\            /|\
              / | \          / | \
             /  |  \        /  |  \
        Throw1  |  Throw3 
        0 deg   |  180 deg
                |
        ────────O────────  Crankshaft axis
                |
        Throw2  |  Throw4
        180 deg |  360 deg
             \  |  /        \  |  /
              \ | /          \ | /
               \|/            \|/
                *              *
                |              |
              Cyl 2          Cyl 4
    
    Firing Order: 1 - 3 - 4 - 2  (most common)
    Primary Force: Perfectly Balanced (0)
    Secondary Force: Resultant = 0
    Primary Couple: 0
    Secondary Couple: Non-zero (residual)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define primary and secondary forces in an internal combustion engine. Why is the secondary force considered more troublesome even though it is smaller in magnitude?**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary force** is the inertia force component of the reciprocating mass acting at engine rotational frequency (ω). It is mathematically expressed as $F_p = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$.

**Secondary force** is the inertia force component arising from the angularity of the connecting rod, acting at twice engine rotational frequency (2ω). It is given by $F_s = m r \omega^2 \frac{r}{l} \cos 2\theta$.

The secondary force is considered more troublesome because:

1. It acts at **twice the engine frequency**, falling in a range where rubber engine mounts have **poor transmissibility**, so it passes into the chassis and cabin.
2. It is **out of phase between adjacent cylinders** in some firing orders, making it harder to cancel.
3. It causes **higher-order vibrations** that excite body panels and create buzz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that secondary force frequency is **2ω**, not ω. You will lose 1 mark if you write "secondary force is small so it is harmless."

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**What is meant by the firing interval of a 4-stroke IC engine? Calculate it for a 6-cylinder engine.**

**Model Answer:**

The **firing interval** is the angular distance (in crank degrees) between successive power strokes in a multi-cylinder engine.

For a 4-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every 2 crank revolutions (720°). Therefore:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{n}$$

For a 6-cylinder engine:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{6} = 120°$$

Common firing order for inline-6: **1 - 5 - 3 - 6 - 2 - 4**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**a)** With neat sketches, explain the concept of **primary and secondary forces** in a single-cylinder reciprocating engine. Derive the expressions for both. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 4-cylinder inline engine has crank throws at **0°, 90°, 270°, and 180°**. Comment on its primary balancing. If unsatisfactory, suggest a better crank arrangement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Concept:** When a piston moves up and down, it experiences acceleration due to crank rotation. By Newton's second law, the inertia force $F = ma$ acts opposite to acceleration. Because the connecting rod is not infinitely long, the simple harmonic motion is distorted, producing two harmonics.

**Sketch (describe verbally):** A slider-crank mechanism with crank OA of radius `r`, connecting rod AB of length `l`, piston B moving along OX, crank angle θ from TDC.

**Derivation:**

Starting from slider-crank geometry, the piston displacement is:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

where $\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$ (small angle approximation).

After trigonometric expansion:

$$x = r\cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{4l}\cos 2\theta + \text{constant}$$

Differentiating twice with respect to time:

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

Therefore, inertia force on the piston (direction opposite to motion):

$$\boxed{F_{\text{inertia}} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta + m r \omega^2 \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta}$$

$$\boxed{F_p = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta \quad ; \quad F_s = m r \omega^2 \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta}$$

The primary force varies at engine speed (1×), while the secondary force varies at twice engine speed (2×).

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** 4-cylinder inline, crank angles 0°, 90°, 270°, 180°.

**Primary force vectors** (all of equal magnitude $F_p = m r \omega^2$):

$$V_1 = F_p \, e^{j0°}, \quad V_2 = F_p \, e^{j90°}, \quad V_3 = F_p \, e^{j270°}, \quad V_4 = F_p \, e^{j180°}$$

**Vector Sum for Primary Force:**

$$\sum F_p = F_p(1 + e^{j90°} + e^{j270°} + e^{j180°})$$

$$e^{j90°} = j, \quad e^{j180°} = -1, \quad e^{j270°} = -j$$

$$\sum F_p = F_p(1 + j + (-j) + (-1)) = F_p(0) = 0$$

So **primary force is balanced**.

**Primary Couple Calculation** (taking cylinder 1 as reference at position 0, cylinder spacing = `a`):

$$\sum \vec{C}_p = 0 \cdot V_1 + a \cdot V_2 + 2a \cdot V_3 + 3a \cdot V_4$$

$$= a F_p (e^{j90°} + 2 e^{j270°} + 3 e^{j180°})$$

$$= a F_p [j + 2(-j) + 3(-1)] = a F_p[-3 - j]$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert \sum \vec{C}_p \vert = a F_p \sqrt{9 + 1} = a F_p \sqrt{10}$$

**Verdict:** The primary couple is non-zero and large ($\sqrt{10}$ times single cylinder couple). The engine will **rock severely**.

**Better arrangement:** Crank angles **0°, 180°, 180°, 0°** (i.e., outer cylinders at 0° and inner at 180°), firing order 1-3-4-2. This gives **zero primary force AND zero primary couple**.

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Morse test** for determining the friction power of an IC engine. What are its limitations? **(7 marks)**

**b)** During a Morse test on a 4-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine running at **3000 rpm**, the brake power is measured as **30 kW**. When the spark is cut from each cylinder individually, the brake power drops by **3.6 kW, 3.4 kW, 3.5 kW, and 3.5 kW** respectively. Calculate:

(i) Friction Power
(ii) Indicated Power of each cylinder
(iii) Mechanical Efficiency **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Morse Test Principle:** It is a non-disassembly method to find the friction power of a multi-cylinder engine. The engine is motored at rated speed and load. The spark is cut off from one cylinder at a time. With that cylinder not contributing power but still being driven (and consuming friction), the brake power drops by an amount equal to the **indicated power of that cylinder minus the friction power saved in that cylinder**.

**Assumption:** Friction power is assumed **uniformly distributed** across all cylinders.

**Sketch Description:** Test setup showing the engine coupled to an eddy-current dynamometer with an ignition cut-out switch on each cylinder's spark plug lead. A fuel flow meter and air flow bench feed into the data acquisition system.

**Procedure:**

1. Run the engine at rated RPM and part-load.
2. Note down the brake power $BP_0$.
3. Cut spark to cylinder 1. Note new BP, $BP_1$. Drop $\Delta BP_1 = BP_0 - BP_1$.
4. Restore cylinder 1. Cut spark to cylinder 2. Note $\Delta BP_2$.
5. Repeat for all cylinders.
6. Total friction power $FP = \Delta BP_1 + \Delta BP_2 + ... + \Delta BP_n$.
7. Indicated Power $IP = BP_0 + FP$.

**Limitations:**

1. Assumes uniform friction distribution among cylinders — not strictly true.
2. Works accurately only at part-load; at full load the cut cylinder may stall the engine.
3. Cooling and lubrication conditions change when a cylinder is "dead," slightly altering friction.
4. Difficult to apply to diesel engines (no spark to cut; requires fuel cut-off solenoid).
5. Cannot be done on a 2-stroke or single-cylinder engine.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**

- $N = 3000$ rpm
- $BP = 30$ kW
- $\Delta BP_1 = 3.6$ kW, $\Delta BP_2 = 3.4$ kW, $\Delta BP_3 = 3.5$ kW, $\Delta BP_4 = 3.5$ kW

**Step (i): Friction Power**

$$FP = \Delta BP_1 + \Delta BP_2 + \Delta BP_3 + \Delta BP_4$$

$$FP = 3.6 + 3.4 + 3.5 + 3.5 = 14.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (ii): Indicated Power of each cylinder**

Total $IP = BP + FP = 30 + 14 = 44$ kW.

For each cylinder, the indicated power equals the drop observed when that cylinder is cut:

$$IP_1 = 3.6 \text{ kW}, \quad IP_2 = 3.4 \text{ kW}, \quad IP_3 = 3.5 \text{ kW}, \quad IP_4 = 3.5 \text{ kW}$$

Verification: $3.6 + 3.4 + 3.5 + 3.5 = 14.0$ kW ✓ (matches friction power)

**Step (iii): Mechanical Efficiency**

$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{30}{44} = 0.6818$$

$$\boxed{\eta_m \approx 68.18\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students mistakenly add $\Delta BP_i$ to get $IP$ directly. **Correct step:** $FP = \sum \Delta BP_i$, then $IP = BP + FP$.
> 2. Some students compute $IP_i$ as $BP / n$ (assuming equal distribution). **Wrong:** Each $\Delta BP_i$ directly represents the indicated power of the cut cylinder in the Morse test.
> 3. Don't forget to convert units if RPM changes during the test — Morse test must be done at constant RPM.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Primary force** = $m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$; **Secondary force** = $m r \omega^2 \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta$.
- Secondary force acts at **2× engine frequency**, making it more irritating despite smaller magnitude.
- **Firing interval** for 4-stroke engine = $720°/n$.
- **3-cylinder inline**: primary force = 0; has residual primary couple causing vibration.
- **4-cylinder inline (0°, 180°, 180°, 0°)**: primary and primary couple = 0; secondary force = 0; only secondary couple remains.
- **6-cylinder inline (120°) and V8 (90°)**: both are in perfect primary and secondary balance.
- **Morse test** determines friction power by sequentially cutting ignition. Works on petrol engines only (need spark to cut).
- **BSFC** typical values: petrol 0.25–0.30 kg/kWh, diesel 0.20–0.25 kg/kWh.
- **Mechanical efficiency** = BP/IP; for healthy engines 75–90%.
- **Heat balance sheet** has 4 parts: brake work, cooling water, exhaust gas, unaccounted loss.
- **Indicator diagram** gives indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP); **Morse test** gives friction mean effective pressure (FMEP); **BMEP** comes from dynamometer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Petrol Engine Fuel System: Components of Fuel System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Petrol Engine Fuel System: Components of Fuel System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Petrol Engine Fuel System

The **Petrol Engine Fuel System** is a meticulously engineered assembly of mechanical, electrical, and electronic components designed to **store, filter, meter, and deliver a precisely atomized mixture of petrol (gasoline) and air** to the engine's combustion chambers at the correct ratio, pressure, and timing. It functions as the "digestive tract" of the internal combustion engine — converting stored chemical potential into usable kinetic energy via controlled combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The fuel system is examined under two broad sub-topics:
> 1. **Components of the Fuel System** (identification, function, construction)
> 2. **Servicing, Repair, and Overhauling procedures** for each component
> Focus is laid heavily on **practical troubleshooting flowcharts**, **component failure modes**, and **periodic maintenance schedules**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine the petrol engine fuel system as a **high-end restaurant kitchen**:

| Restaurant Element | Fuel System Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Storage** | Fuel Tank | Stores raw material (petrol) safely |
| **Water Filter / Tap** | Fuel Filter & Fuel Lines | Cleans and transports the fuel |
| **Pumping System** | Fuel Pump | Pushes fuel with required pressure |
| **Master Chef** | Carburetor / Fuel Injector + ECU | Mixes fuel + air in exact proportions |
| **Dining Table Plates** | Intake Manifold | Distributes the prepared mixture to cylinders |
| **Quality Inspector** | Pressure Regulator, Sensors | Maintains consistency of preparation |
| **Hood / Exhaust Vent** | Air Filter & PCV Valve | Ensures clean air entry |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a slight imbalance in spice ruins a dish, an imbalance in the **air-fuel ratio (AFR)** of **14.7:1 (stoichiometric for petrol)** ruins engine performance, fuel economy, and emissions.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol) = 14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Density of Petrol ≈ 0.74 kg/L** at 15 °C
- **Fuel Pressure in MPFI System = 2.5 – 3.5 bar (250 – 350 kPa)**
- **Fuel Pressure in GDI System = 100 – 350 bar (10 – 35 MPa)**
- **Fuel Tank Capacity (typical B-segment car) = 35 – 45 L**
- **Fuel Filter Replacement Interval = 40,000 – 80,000 km** (modern long-life filters)
- **Injector Spray Angle = 15° – 60°** (depending on design)
- **Fuel Pump Flow Rate = 80 – 120 L/h** (typical electric in-tank pump)

> [!NOTE]
> **A/F Ratio Rule of Thumb (Kerala Board Examiner Tip):**
> - **Rich Mixture (AFR < 14.7)** → Black smoke, poor economy, high CO
> - **Lean Mixture (AFR > 14.7)** → Backfire, overheating, high NOₓ

---

## 1.4 The "Big Picture" Block Diagram of a Modern Petrol Fuel System

The modern petrol engine fuel system (post-2000s) is dominated by **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI / MPFI)** rather than carburetors. Understanding the layout is fundamental to KTU Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Architectural Generations You MUST Know:**
> 1. **Carbureted System** (older vehicles, pre-2000s)
> 2. **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) System** (modern, KTU exam focus)
> Within MPFI, two sub-types: **Port Injection (PFI)** and **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Functional Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Fuel Tank
- **Function:** Stores petrol safely; accommodates thermal expansion via vent system.
- **Construction:** Sheet steel (older) or high-density polyethylene (HDPE, modern). Internal **baffling** prevents fuel sloshing.
- **Key Sub-components:** Fuel filler neck, fuel tank cap (sealed with pressure/vacuum relief valve), fuel level sender unit (float + variable resistor / rheostat), fuel strainer (sock filter at pump inlet), evaporative emission control valve (EVAP canister connection).
- **Failure Modes:** Rust perforation, float sensor failure (erroneous fuel gauge reading), vapor lock in summer (Kerala-specific concern), leak at filler neck.

### 2.1.2 Fuel Pump
- **Function:** Draws fuel from the tank and delivers it to the engine at the required pressure and flow rate.
- **Two Generations:**
  1. **Mechanical Fuel Pump** (diaphragm type, driven by camshaft lobe, found in carbureted engines)
  2. **Electrical Fuel Pump** (submerged in tank, brushless DC motor with turbine or roller-vane impeller)
- **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Modern vehicles use an **in-tank submersible pump** because it:
  - Eliminates vapor lock (fuel is cooled by surrounding liquid)
  - Pre-priming is automatic
  - Noise is dampened by tank walls

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Symptom Pattern (Examiner's favorite):**
> - Pump primes (audible whine for 2 seconds at ignition ON) but engine cranks → check **fuel filter, pressure regulator, injector windings**
> - No prime whine → **pump relay, pump motor, or wiring fault**
> - Whine present, engine starts but stalls under load → **weak pump, restricted filter, or failing pressure regulator**

### 2.1.3 Fuel Filter
- **Function:** Removes particulates, water, and sediment from petrol before it reaches the injectors.
- **Construction:** Inlet (larger bore) and outlet (smaller bore) ports; pleated paper element inside a metal housing; some filters include a **water separator bowl** that drains via a small screw at the bottom.
- **Service Interval:** Replace every **40,000 – 80,000 km** or as per OEM schedule.

### 2.1.4 Fuel Pressure Regulator
- **Function:** Maintains constant fuel rail pressure relative to intake manifold pressure.
- **Operating Principle:** A spring-loaded diaphragm modulated by manifold vacuum. Excess fuel returns to the tank via the return line.
- **In GDI Systems:** Mechanical regulator is replaced by an electronically controlled high-pressure pump driven by the camshaft.

### 2.1.5 Fuel Rail (Common Rail)
- **Function:** Distributes fuel from the regulator to all injectors at uniform pressure.
- **Construction:** Tubular accumulator with injection ports and a Schrader valve (test port) for pressure diagnostics.

### 2.1.6 Fuel Injectors
- **Function:** Atomize fuel into a fine spray and deliver it precisely into the intake port (PFI) or directly into the cylinder (GDI).
- **Operating Principle:** Solenoid or piezo-electric actuator opens the needle valve for a controlled **pulse width (in milliseconds)** commanded by the ECU.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Injector flow rate is tested using an **injector tester / ultrasonic cleaner** and a graduated cylinder. A healthy injector delivers **±2 mL/min** tolerance across all 4 cylinders.

### 2.1.7 Throttle Body
- **Function:** Controls the volume of air entering the engine via a butterfly valve operated by the accelerator pedal (via **drive-by-wire** in modern cars).
- **Contains:** Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Idle Air Control Valve (IACV) or electronic idle control, and often a coolant passage for anti-icing.

### 2.1.8 Air Filter (Air Cleaner)
- **Function:** Filters intake air to prevent abrasive dust from entering the engine.
- **Service Interval:** Inspect every **10,000 km**, replace every **20,000 – 40,000 km** (or 1 year).

### 2.1.9 Intake Manifold
- **Function:** Distributes the air (PFI) or air-fuel mixture (carbureted) to each cylinder.
- **Modern Type:** Variable-length / variable-geometry intake manifold for optimized torque across RPM range.

### 2.1.10 Engine Control Unit (ECU)
- **Function:** The "brain" that commands injector pulse width, ignition timing, idle speed, and fuel pump relay based on inputs from **MAP, MAF, O₂ (lambda), CPS, TPS, and coolant temperature sensors**.

### 2.1.11 Sensors in the Fuel System (Frequently tested)
| Sensor | Acronym | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Manifold Absolute Pressure | MAP | Measures intake manifold vacuum for fuel calculation |
| Mass Air Flow | MAF | Measures mass of incoming air |
| Throttle Position Sensor | TPS | Reports throttle plate angle |
| Oxygen / Lambda Sensor | O₂ | Measures exhaust O₂ for closed-loop AFR control |
| Coolant Temperature Sensor | CTS | Enriches mixture during cold start |
| Crankshaft Position Sensor | CKP | Synchronizes injection timing |
| Cam Position Sensor | CMP | Enables sequential fuel injection |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (Petrol) | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Bench-mark for complete combustion |
| 2 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ (kg/kWh) | Indicates engine fuel efficiency |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air,actual}}{\dot{m}_{air,theoretical}}$ | Cylinder filling efficiency |
| 4 | Injector Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{open}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | ECU controlled (typically 10 – 80%) |
| 5 | Fuel Rail Pressure Drop Test | $\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{manifold}$ (constant in PFI) | Should remain within **±0.1 bar** |
| 6 | Injector Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ (mL/min) | Test cylinder volume / time |
| 7 | Pump Pressure Calculation | $P = \rho g h + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ (Bernoulli-related) | Tank-to-rail pressure |
| 8 | Lambda Value | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ → stoich; $<1$ → rich; $>1$ → lean |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** When asked to "describe the fuel system," examiners expect you to draw a labeled diagram **and** list the components in the **flow order**: Tank → Strainer → Pump → Filter → Rail → Regulator → Injector → Engine.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Control:** Precise AFR control via EFI + lambda sensor reduces CO, HC, NOₓ (Bharat Stage 6 / Euro 6 compliance).
- **Fuel Economy:** Electronic control improves mileage by 10–15% over carbureted systems.
- **Diagnostics:** OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, port usually under the dashboard) reads fault codes (P0171 = system too lean, P0172 = system too rich) — a critical KTU troubleshooting concept.
- **Adaptability:** Modern ECUs adapt to altitude, temperature, fuel quality, and engine wear via adaptive learning.
- **Kerala-Specific Application:** Monsoon humidity requires special attention to **water-contaminated fuel** and **EVAP system** integrity (canister purging issues).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fuel Injector Pulse Width Calculation

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder petrol engine operates at 3000 RPM with a BSFC of 0.30 kg/kWh and brake power of 60 kW. Each cylinder receives fuel from one injector. Calculate the **injector pulse width** if the injector flow rate is 200 mL/min and fuel density is 0.74 kg/L.

### Step 1: Total Fuel Consumption Rate
The total fuel mass flow rate is given by:
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = BSFC \times P_{brake}$$

Substituting values:
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.30 \ \text{kg/kWh} \times 60 \ \text{kW}$$

Converting kWh units (60 kW × 1 h = 60 kWh per hour):
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 18 \ \text{kg/h}$$

### Step 2: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate
Volumetric flow rate is mass flow rate divided by density:
$$\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}$$

Substituting values:
$$\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{18 \ \text{kg/h}}{0.74 \ \text{kg/L}}$$

Converting to mL/min (1 L = 1000 mL, 1 h = 60 min):
$$\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{18 \times 1000}{0.74 \times 60} \ \text{mL/min}$$

$$\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{18000}{44.4} \ \text{mL/min} \approx 405.4 \ \text{mL/min}$$

### Step 3: Fuel per Cylinder per Minute
With 4 cylinders, fuel is split equally (assuming uniform distribution):
$$\dot{V}_{cyl} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{fuel}}{n_{cyl}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{cyl} = \dfrac{405.4}{4} \approx 101.35 \ \text{mL/min}$$

### Step 4: Injector Pulse Width Calculation
The pulse width is the time the injector must stay open to deliver this volume, given its flow rate:
$$T_{pulse} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{cyl}}{Q_{injector}} \times 60 \ \text{s/min}$$

Where $Q_{injector} = 200$ mL/min.

$$T_{pulse} = \dfrac{101.35}{200} \times 60$$

$$T_{pulse} = 0.50675 \times 60 \approx 30.4 \ \text{ms}$$

### Step 5: Duty Cycle Check
Injectors fire once every **2 revolutions** (4-stroke engine). The period between injections per cylinder is:
$$T_{period} = \dfrac{120}{N} = \dfrac{120}{3000} = 0.04 \ \text{s} = 40 \ \text{ms}$$

Therefore:
$$D = \dfrac{T_{pulse}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{30.4}{40} \times 100\% = 76\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The injector pulse width is **≈ 30.4 ms** with a duty cycle of **76%**, which is within the acceptable range of 10–80%. A value above 80% indicates the injectors are nearing their maximum flow — a sign of injector wear or excessive fuel demand (vacuum leak, incorrect sensor reading, or over-fueling).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fuel Pump Pressure Requirement

> **Problem:** An in-tank electric fuel pump must deliver fuel from the tank (located 0.4 m below the engine fuel rail) at a flow rate of 100 L/h. The fuel line has an equivalent length of 4 m with 8 mm internal diameter. Friction factor (Darcy) = 0.025. Calculate the required pump delivery pressure.

### Step 1: Static Head Pressure
$$P_{static} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$\rho = 740 \ \text{kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2, \quad h = 0.4 \ \text{m}$$

$$P_{static} = 740 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 = 2903.76 \ \text{Pa} \approx 0.029 \ \text{bar}$$

### Step 2: Velocity in the Fuel Line
$$Q = 100 \ \text{L/h} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.78 \times 10^{-5}}{5.027 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.553 \ \text{m/s}$$

### Step 3: Friction Head Loss (Darcy-Weisbach)
$$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$$

$$h_f = 0.025 \times \dfrac{4}{0.008} \times \dfrac{(0.553)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.025 \times 500 \times \dfrac{0.306}{19.62}$$

$$h_f = 12.5 \times 0.0156 \approx 0.195 \ \text{m}$$

### Step 4: Total Pump Pressure
$$P_{pump} = P_{rail} + \rho g h_f + P_{static}$$

For MPFI, $P_{rail} \approx 3.0$ bar = 300,000 Pa.

$$P_{pump} = 300000 + 740 \times 9.81 \times 0.195 + 2903.76$$

$$P_{pump} = 300000 + 1415.7 + 2903.76 \approx 304319 \ \text{Pa} \approx 3.04 \ \text{bar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The required delivery pressure is **≈ 3.04 bar**, which matches typical MPFI system pressure of **2.5 – 3.5 bar**. The friction loss (1.42 kPa) and static head (2.9 kPa) are negligible compared to the rail pressure.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component-Fault-Symptom Trinity (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for the ESE — it appears almost every year in some form.**

| Component | Common Fault | Symptom | Diagnostic Method | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel Tank | Water contamination, rust, leak | Sputtering, hard start | Drain sample, visual inspect | Drain, flush, replace tank |
| Fuel Pump | Motor burnout, weak diaphragm | No start, stalling | Pressure test, prime whine check | Replace pump or pump assembly |
| Fuel Filter | Clogging | Loss of power, surging | Pressure drop test across filter | Replace filter element |
| Fuel Pressure Regulator | Stuck diaphragm | Rich/lean condition, black smoke | Vacuum line test (no fuel in vacuum hose = OK) | Replace regulator |
| Fuel Injector | Clogged, leaking, electrical failure | Misfire, rough idle, poor economy | Resistance test, flow test, Noid light | Clean (ultrasonic) or replace |
| Throttle Body | Carbon buildup, TPS failure | Erratic idle, stalling, limp mode | Visual inspect, TPS voltage sweep | Clean with TB cleaner, recalibrate |
| Air Filter | Clogging | Reduced power, high fuel consumption | Visual inspection | Replace filter |
| MAP Sensor | Vacuum leak, contamination | Hesitation, P0105–P0109 codes | Scan tool, voltage check | Clean or replace |
| MAF Sensor | Dirty hot wire | Lean code (P0171), hesitation | Scan tool + cleaning | Clean with MAF-safe spray |
| O₂ Sensor | Slow response, heater failure | Failed emission test, rich/lean codes | Scan tool, scope test | Replace sensor |
| Fuel Lines | Crack, leak, blockage | Fuel smell, hard start | Visual + pressure test | Replace line, check clamps |
| ECU | Corroded connector, internal fault | Multiple symptoms, no communication | Scan tool communication test | Reflash, repair, or replace |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Petrol Engine Fuel System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TankA[Fuel Tank with Strainer and Level Sender]
    PumpB[Electric Fuel Pump In Tank Submersible]
    FilterC[Fuel Filter Pleated Paper Element]
    RailD[Fuel Rail Accumulator with Schrader Test Port]
    RegE[Fuel Pressure Regulator Vacuum Referenced]
    InjF[Fuel Injectors 4 Units Solenoid Type]
    TBG[Throttle Body with TPS and IACV]
    IMH[Intake Manifold Variable Geometry]
    EngI[Petrol Engine Combustion Chambers]
    TankA --> PumpB
    PumpB --> FilterC
    FilterC --> RailD
    RailD --> InjF
    InjF --> IMH
    IMH --> EngI
    TBG --> IMH
    RegE -.->|Return Line| TankA
    RailD -.->|Excess Fuel| RegE
    InjF -.->|Residual Fuel| RailD
    PumpB -->|Fuel Return Path via Pressure Regulator| TankA
```

## 4.2 ECU and Sensor Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU Brain]
    MAP[MAP Sensor Manifold Pressure]
    MAF[MAF Sensor Air Flow]
    TPS[TPS Throttle Position]
    O2[Oxygen Lambda Sensor]
    CTS[CTS Coolant Temp]
    CKP[CKP Crankshaft Position]
    CMP[CMP Camshaft Position]
    INJ[Fuel Injectors x4]
    COIL[Ignition Coils]
    FP[Fuel Pump Relay]
    ECU --> INJ
    ECU --> COIL
    ECU --> FP
    MAP --> ECU
    MAF --> ECU
    TPS --> ECU
    O2 --> ECU
    CTS --> ECU
    CKP --> ECU
    CMP --> ECU
```

## 4.3 Carburetor vs. MPFI — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Carburetor_System
        C1[Air Filter] --> C2[Carburetor Venturi and Float Chamber]
        C2 --> C3[Intake Manifold]
        C3 --> C4[Engine Cylinders]
    end
    subgraph MPFI_System
        M1[Air Filter] --> M2[Throttle Body]
        M2 --> M3[Intake Manifold]
        M4[Fuel Rail with Injectors] --> M3
        M5[Fuel Pump] --> M4
        M6[ECU] --> M4
        M3 --> M7[Engine Cylinders]
    end
```

## 4.4 Fuel System Servicing Workflow (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Customer Complaint: Engine Hard to Start / Poor Mileage / Rough Idle]) --> Step1[Step 1: Connect OBD-II Scanner and Read DTCs]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Check Fuel Rail Pressure with Manometer]
    Step2 --> Step3{Pressure Reading OK between 2.5 and 3.5 bar}
    Step3 -->|No, low pressure| Step4[Inspect Filter Pump Regulator Lines]
    Step4 --> Step5[Replace Faulty Component and Retest]
    Step3 -->|Yes, pressure OK| Step6[Step 3: Perform Injector Drop Test and Flow Test]
    Step6 --> Step7{All Injectors Balanced within 2 mL per min}
    Step7 -->|No| Step8[Clean or Replace Faulty Injector]
    Step8 --> Step5
    Step7 -->|Yes| Step9[Step 4: Inspect Throttle Body and Air Filter]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 5: Verify Sensor Readings MAP MAF O2 TPS CTS]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 6: Road Test and Confirm Customer Complaint Resolved]
    Step11 --> Done([End: Document Service and Hand Over Vehicle])
    Step5 --> Step11
```

## 4.5 Component Failure-Frequency Pie Chart (Industry Maintenance Data)

```mermaid
pie title Typical Fuel System Service Frequency
    "Fuel Filter Replacement" : 35
    "Injector Cleaning" : 20
    "Throttle Body Cleaning" : 15
    "Fuel Pump Replacement" : 10
    "Sensor Replacement MAP MAF O2" : 12
    "Fuel Tank and Line Service" : 5
    "ECU Diagnostics and Reflash" : 3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, **Module 2** syllabus scope, and **Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1 (Remember) to L4 (Analyze)**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: List any six major components of a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system and state the function of each in one line.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Fuel Tank** — Stores petrol safely with vented cap and internal baffles.
2. **Electric Fuel Pump** — Delivers pressurized fuel from the tank to the engine.
3. **Fuel Filter** — Removes dirt, water, and debris from petrol.
4. **Fuel Rail** — Distributes uniform pressure fuel to all injectors.
5. **Fuel Pressure Regulator** — Maintains a constant pressure differential between rail and manifold.
6. **Fuel Injectors** — Atomize fuel into intake ports on ECU command.
*(3 marks: 1 for listing, 1 for functions, 1 for presentation)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol? Differentiate between rich and lean mixtures with one symptom each.**

**Model Answer:**
The **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (AFR) for petrol is 14.7 : 1** by mass — the chemically ideal ratio for complete combustion with no excess air or fuel.

- **Rich Mixture (AFR < 14.7):** Excess fuel. Symptom: **Black smoke from exhaust, high CO emissions, poor fuel economy**.
- **Lean Mixture (AFR > 14.7):** Excess air. Symptom: **Engine hesitation, backfire through intake, overheating, high NOₓ**.

*(3 marks: 1 for ratio, 1 for rich explanation, 1 for lean explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice Option 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a) With the help of a neat labeled diagram, describe the components of a modern petrol engine fuel system. Explain the working of an electric fuel pump and a fuel pressure regulator. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) Explain the procedure for inspecting and overhauling a fuel injector. List the common faults and remedies. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Components of Modern Petrol Engine Fuel System (with diagram):**
The fuel system consists of the following components arranged in flow sequence:

1. **Fuel Tank** — Stores petrol. Equipped with a vented cap, internal strainer, and level sender unit.
2. **In-Tank Electric Fuel Pump** — Submerged pump with brushless DC motor; pushes fuel at 3–4 bar.
3. **Fuel Filter** — Pleated paper element removes contaminants.
4. **Fuel Lines (Supply & Return)** — High-pressure rubber or metal-braided hoses.
5. **Fuel Rail** — Common accumulator feeding all injectors.
6. **Fuel Pressure Regulator** — Diaphragm type, vacuum-referenced; bleeds excess fuel back to tank.
7. **Fuel Injectors** — Solenoid-operated, ECU-driven spray nozzles.
8. **Throttle Body** — Butterfly valve controlling airflow with TPS and IACV.
9. **Air Filter** — Pleated paper element for clean air.
10. **Intake Manifold** — Distributes air (or mixture) to each cylinder.
11. **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** — Computes injector pulse width from sensor inputs.
12. **Sensors** — MAP, MAF, O₂, TPS, CTS, CKP, CMP.

*(2 marks: list + diagram)*

**Working of Electric Fuel Pump:**
The pump is a **submerged DC motor-driven pump** placed inside the fuel tank. When the ignition is switched ON, the ECU energizes the fuel pump relay, supplying 12 V to the pump motor. The impeller (roller-vane or turbine type) spins at high speed, drawing fuel through the inlet strainer and discharging it at **3–4 bar** through the outlet port toward the filter and rail. A check valve at the outlet maintains residual pressure in the line after switch-off for easy restart.

*(2.5 marks: working explanation)*

**Working of Fuel Pressure Regulator:**
The regulator is a **spring-loaded diaphragm** housed between the fuel rail and the intake manifold. Manifold vacuum acts on one side of the diaphragm, fuel pressure on the other. When fuel pressure exceeds the spring + vacuum setting, the diaphragm lifts, opening the return path so excess fuel flows back to the tank. This maintains a **constant pressure differential of ~2.5 bar** between rail and manifold, ensuring consistent injector flow.

*(2.5 marks: regulator explanation)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Inspection and Overhauling of Fuel Injector — Procedure:**

1. **Removal:** Depressurize the system (remove pump fuse, crank engine). Disconnect battery negative. Remove intake manifold / plenum. Disconnect electrical connector and fuel line. Pull injector out with the help of a slide hammer or specialized puller.
2. **Visual Inspection:** Check for external cracks, broken O-rings, carbon buildup at nozzle tip, and bent pintle.
3. **Electrical Test (Resistance):** Measure coil resistance using a multimeter. Typical solenoid injector = **2 – 16 Ω**. Open or short circuit = replace.
