# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks typical):** Conflating *symptom* with *issue*. Always state both explicitly. Example: "**Symptom:** Blue exhaust smoke. **Possible Issues:** Worn piston rings, valve guide seal failure, or turbo seal leak."
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks typical):** Listing possible issues without ranking them. Examiners reward **Pareto-ordered** answers (most likely first).
> - **Pitfall 3 (–3 marks typical):** Skipping the **verification step**. A symptom that cannot be reproduced is *intermittent* — your diagnosis path must differ.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–2 marks typical):** Recommending part replacement before **confirming root cause**. Always pair "possible issue" with a **diagnostic test** that confirms it.
> - **Pitfall 5 (–1 mark typical):** Not mentioning **OBD-II/DTC scanning** in modern vehicle questions. For post-2010 vehicles, it is the first diagnostic step.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between a *symptom* and a *possible issue* in vehicle diagnostics. Give **two examples** to illustrate the difference.

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**

A **symptom** is the *external, observable abnormal behavior* of a vehicle, perceived by the operator or measured by instruments. It is the *effect*. A **possible issue** is the *internal mechanical, electrical, or electronic defect* that *causes* the symptom. It is the *defect* itself.

- **Example 1:** *Symptom:* Engine oil level drops rapidly. *Possible Issues:* Worn piston rings, leaking valve guide seals, external oil pan gasket leak, or failed turbo oil seal.
- **Example 2:** *Symptom:* Steering wheel vibrates at highway speeds. *Possible Issues:* Unbalanced wheel, bent rim, worn tie-rod end, or failing wheel bearing.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for examples, 1 for clarity. Examiners award full marks when both symptom and issue are correctly identified as cause-effect.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> List the **five categories of vehicle symptoms** and state **one diagnostic instrument or method** used to detect each.

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**

1. **Auditory symptoms** — detected by the human ear, mechanic's mechanical stethoscope, or electronic stethoscope.
2. **Visual symptoms** — detected by direct visual inspection, borescope, or thermal imaging camera.
3. **Performance symptoms** — detected by OBD-II scanner, dynamometer, or exhaust gas analyzer.
4. **Olfactory symptoms** — detected by the trained technician's sense of smell.
5. **Tactile/kinesthetic symptoms** — detected by operator road-test feedback or hand-feel inspection.

(3 marks: 0.5 per category, 0.1 bonus for naming an instrument.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 2 offers an internal choice. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**

> (a) Explain the **systematic six-step methodology** for troubleshooting vehicle symptoms. Why is hypothesis ranking by probability important? (7 marks)
>
> (b) A customer reports: *"My car has poor fuel economy and the engine warning light is on."* List the **possible issues**, the **diagnostic tests** to confirm each, and the **expected OBD-II DTCs**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The six-step systematic troubleshooting methodology is:

1. **Symptom Verification (1 mark):** Reproduce the reported symptom under controlled workshop or road-test conditions. This eliminates operator misinterpretation and confirms the fault is real, not intermittent.
2. **Information Gathering (1 mark):** Connect OBD-II scanner, read all DTCs, gather service history, and document operator description accurately.
3. **Symptom Classification (1 mark):** Categorize the symptom into one or more of the five sensory buckets (auditory, visual, performance, olfactory, tactile).
4. **Hypothesis Generation (1 mark):** Brainstorm all components whose failure could produce the observed symptom. Do not pre-filter at this stage.
5. **Testing & Elimination (2 marks):** Test hypotheses in order of **simplicity and cost** — from visual checks, to scan data, to instrumented tests (compression, pressure, resistance), to mechanical disassembly. Eliminate ruled-out causes systematically.
6. **Root Cause Confirmation & Repair (1 mark):** Once the failed component is identified, perform the repair using OEM specifications, clear DTCs, and conduct a post-repair verification (road test) to confirm symptom elimination.

**Why ranking by probability matters:** Workshop data shows that **80% of recurring symptoms are caused by 20% of common failures** (Pareto principle). Ranking hypotheses by probability saves diagnostic time, reduces customer cost, and avoids replacing functional parts. It also aligns with industry-standard FMEA risk-prioritization thinking.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Possible Issue | Diagnostic Test | Expected OBD-II DTC |
|---|---|---|
| Faulty oxygen sensor (O2) | Live data: O2 voltage stuck at 0.45 V or slow switching | P0131, P0132, P0133, P0171 |
| Dirty or failed MAF sensor | Live data: MAF grams/sec out of range; clean and retest | P0100, P0101, P0171 |
| Leaking fuel injector(s) | Injector balance test, fuel pressure drop test | P0172 (rich), P0300 (misfire) |
| Stuck-open thermostat (cold engine running rich) | IR temp scan at thermostat housing | P0128 |
| Low tire pressure (increased rolling resistance) | Tire pressure gauge | (No DTC, visual) |
| Clogged air filter | Visual inspection of filter element | P0101 (possible) |
| Faulty coolant temperature sensor (ECTS) | Resistance vs. temperature table check | P0115, P0117, P0125 |
| Dragging brake caliper (engine load) | Infrared temperature of wheel hubs | (No DTC) |

(7 marks: ~0.5 per correct issue, 0.5 for test, 0.5 for DTC = 1.5 per row, capped at 7.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**

> (a) Describe the **symptom-interpretation principles** for **exhaust smoke color**. Cover black, blue, white, and gray smoke. For each, list **two possible issues**. (7 marks)
>
> (b) A vehicle exhibits **excessive steering wheel vibration at 80 km/h but not at lower speeds**. List the **possible issues**, the **diagnostic tests**, and the **expected findings**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (≈1.75 per smoke color):**

- **Black Smoke (rich air-fuel mixture):** Excess fuel, insufficient air. *Possible issues:* (1) Faulty MAF sensor reading low, (2) Leaking fuel injector or high fuel pressure, (3) Stuck-open coolant temperature sensor (engine thinks cold, enriches mixture). Diagnostic: OBD-II fuel trim data; short-term fuel trim should be near $\pm 5\%$.
- **Blue Smoke (oil burning in combustion chamber):** Oil entering combustion. *Possible issues:* (1) Worn piston rings (compression loss + oil consumption), (2) Worn valve guide seals (oil sipping down guides). Diagnostic: Compression test + borescope.
- **White Sweet-Smelling Smoke (coolant in combustion chamber):** Coolant leaking into cylinder. *Possible issues:* (1) Blown head gasket, (2) Cracked cylinder head or block. Diagnostic: Combustion leak detector ("block test") — blue fluid turns yellow in exhaust gases.
- **Gray Smoke (often normal on cold start, or transmission fluid in diesels):** Can indicate *possible issues* of (1) Valve stem seal wear (intermittent blue-gray), (2) Transmission fluid siphoned into intake on certain diesel applications (e.g., Ford Powerstroke). Diagnostic: ATF level check; cold-start observation.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Possible Issue | Diagnostic Test | Expected Finding |
|---|---|---|
| Unbalanced wheel/tire | Road-force balancer | Imbalance $\geq 25$ g on one or more planes |
| Bent wheel rim | Dial indicator on rim flange | Runout $\geq 1.5$ mm |
| Worn tie-rod end | Grab tie-rod, attempt shake | Visible vertical/horizontal play |
| Worn wheel bearing | Wheel spin, listen for hum | Growl that changes with vehicle lean |
| Tire flat-spotting (hard use) | Visual + balance | Hard spot visible; imbalance |
| Driveshaft imbalance (RWD) | Driveshaft runout gauge | Runout $\geq 0.5$ mm at U-joint |
| Brake rotor warpage | Dial indicator on rotor | Lateral runout $\geq 0.05$ mm |

(7 marks: 0.5 issue, 0.5 test, 0.5 finding per row, capped.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Symptoms and Possible Issues**

- **Definition Trio:** Always distinguish **Symptom** (effect) → **Possible Issue** (cause) → **Root Cause** (verified defect).
- **Five Sensory Categories:** Auditory, Visual, Performance, Olfactory, Tactile. Memorize one diagnostic instrument per category.
- **Six-Step Method:** Verify → Gather → Classify → Hypothesize → Test → Confirm. **Do not skip verification.**
- **Pareto Principle:** 80% of symptoms are caused by 20% of common faults. Rank hypotheses by probability.
- **Three Combustion Requirements:** Fuel + Spark + Compression. A no-start engine has lost at least one — test all three.
- **Exhaust Smoke Color Code:** Black = rich (fuel), Blue = oil burning, White sweet = coolant (head gasket), Gray = often normal/cold-start.
- **First Diagnostic Test:** Always OBD-II scan on post-2010 vehicles. DTCs dramatically narrow the hypothesis set.
- **Cost-Effective Test Order:** Visual → Listen → Scan → Measure (pressure, voltage, compression) → Disassemble. Never start with disassembly.
- **Engine Overheat Equation Intuition:** Heat generated $\approx$ coolant flow $\times$ temperature rise. If gauge climbs, one of the three terms is wrong.
- **Steering Vibration at Speed:** Wheel balance is rank 1; rim, tie-rod, bearing, and rotor warpage follow.
- **Brake Pull Diagnosis:** Stuck caliper or restricted brake hose is rank 1; uneven pad wear is a *consequence* not a cause.
- **Battery Drain:** Parasitic draw should be $\leq 50$ mA on a healthy vehicle. Higher = a relay, light, or module is staying awake.
- **Intermittent vs Hard Fault:** Cannot reproduce = check connectors, grounds, and wiring first. A DTC stored but not active is a *history* code.
- **Board Answer Structure:** For 14-mark questions, always present in **table format** with columns for Symptom, Issue, Test, and Expected Finding. Examiners scan for tabular structure.
- **Avoid:** (1) Confusing symptom with issue; (2) Replacing parts before testing; (3) Omitting DTC references on modern vehicles; (4) Skipping post-repair verification.
- **Reference Standards to Mention (Board Favorites):** SAE J2012 (DTC definitions), ISO 9001 (workshop quality), BIS 14654 (Indian automotive service standard), OEM service manuals.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Transmission System: Basic Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Transmission System: Basic Parts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Transmission System** of an automobile is the powertrain assembly that transmits the engine's mechanical power — torque and rotational speed — from the engine flywheel to the driving wheels, while simultaneously allowing the driver to vary the **torque-speed relationship**, reverse the direction of travel, and disconnect the engine from the drivetrain as required. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT635)** classification, the transmission system is broadly divided into the following sub-assemblies: 1. **Clutch Assembly** – Disengages/engages engine from gearbox. 2. **Gear Box (Transmission Box)** – Provides multiple gear ratios. 3. **Propeller / Drive Shaft** – Transmits power from gearbox to differential. 4. **Universal Joint (U-Joint) / Constant Velocity (CV) Joint** – Allows angular movement of drive shaft. 5. **Differential Unit** – Splits torque between two drive wheels and allows different wheel speeds during cornering. 6. **Axles (Half / Full / Live)** – Final drive members delivering power to the wheels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The "Basic Parts" of the transmission system constitute the foundational components that every maintenance engineer must identify, inspect, service, and overhaul. Mastery of these parts is mandatory for Part-A and Part-B examination questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **bicycle** 🚲 — the chain connects the pedals to the rear wheel, the chainring and sprocket change the "effort" vs "speed," and the rear hub lets the wheel spin freely. Now, scale this idea up to a heavy truck or car. The **transmission system is essentially the "muscle-and-bone" between the engine (the heart) and the wheels (the feet).** | Human Body Part | Vehicle Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Heart | Engine | Produces power | | Shoulder Joint | Clutch | Connects/disconnects power | | Spine | Propeller Shaft | Transmits power along length | | Hip Joint | Universal/CV Joint | Allows angular flexibility | | Hips | Differential | Splits power left/right | | Legs | Axles & Wheels | Deliver power to ground | > [!NOTE] > **Quick Memory Hook:** *"Engage – Reduce – Transmit – Steer – Deliver"* — these five verbs describe the sequential role of Clutch → Gearbox → Prop-Shaft → Differential → Axle in the transmission chain. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold Standards) - **Gear Ratio (i):** Dimensionless, typically ranges from **3:1 to 4:1** in top gear and up to **1:1 in direct drive**. - **Final Drive Ratio (FDR):** For a passenger car, usually between **3.5:1 and 4.5:1**. - **Torque Capacity of Clutch:** Modern single-plate clutches handle **150 Nm to 600 Nm**. - **Universal Joint Operating Angle:** Limited to a maximum of **15° to 20°** to prevent severe vibration. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Breakdown of Each Basic Part #### A. Clutch Assembly - **Purpose:** Disengages the engine from the gearbox during gear shifts and at standstill. - **Basic Parts:** Flywheel, Clutch Disc (with friction lining), Pressure Plate, Release/Throw-out Bearing, Clutch Pedal, Clutch Master & Slave Cylinder. - **Operating Principle:** Friction — the friction lining grips the flywheel and pressure plate face. - **Common Faults:** Slipping, grabbing, rattling, juddering, hard pedal, and noise on release. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note:** A *single-plate dry clutch* is the most common type tested in university examinations. #### B. Gear Box - **Purpose:** Varies torque-speed ratio as per load and speed demand. - **Basic Parts:** Input shaft, output shaft, countershaft (layshaft), synchronizer hubs, gear wheels (constant-mesh type), selector forks, and shift rails. - **Operating Principle:** Two meshing gears of different diameters exchange torque and speed inversely proportional to their teeth count. - **Common Faults:** Hard shifting, grinding noise, jumping out of gear, oil leaks. #### C. Propeller / Drive Shaft - **Purpose:** Carries rotational power from gearbox output to the differential input. - **Basic Parts:** Tubular shaft, yoke ends, slip yoke (spline). - **Operating Principle:** Pure torsional transmission; the shaft is dynamically balanced. - **Common Faults:** Whining, vibration at speed, U-joint wear, yoke cracks. #### D. Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint) and CV Joint - **Purpose:** Transmits torque even when shafts are at an angle. - **Basic Parts:** Cross/spider, needle bearings, bearing cups, seals, grease boots. - **Operating Principle:** A four-bar linkage that keeps angular velocity varying cyclically — minimized by using *two* U-joints in series (phase-corrected). - **Common Faults:** Clicking on turns (CV joint), vibration during acceleration, grease leakage. #### E. Differential Unit - **Purpose:** Permits the two driving wheels to rotate at different speeds (essential during cornering) while splitting engine torque equally. - **Basic Parts:** Crown wheel (ring gear), pinion gear, differential case, side gears, spider/cross pin, sun gears (also called planet gears). - **Operating Principle:** When the vehicle moves straight, both side gears rotate with the case. When turning, the planet gears walk around the slower side gear, allowing differential action. - **Common Faults:** Whining, howling, chatter on turns, oil leak, binding. #### F. Axles - **Live Axle (Rear Wheel Drive):** Two half-axles inside a single housing. - **Dead/Independent Axle (FWD):** Stub axles connected via CV joints. - **Basic Parts:** Axle shaft, axle housing, wheel bearings, hub. - **Common Faults:** Bearing noise, oil leak, bent shaft. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Law | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1}$ | Gear Ratio (Speed/Torque) | Dimensionless | Clutch & Gearbox calculations | | 2 | $T = F \times r$ | Torque = Force × Radius | N·m | Clutch torque capacity | | 3 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power (Rotational) | Watts (W) | All driveline members | | 4 | $v = \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{60} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Linear velocity of wheel | m/s | Vehicle speed calculation | | 5 | $N_w = \dfrac{N_e}{i_g \times i_f}$ | Wheel RPM from engine RPM | rpm | Final drive speed | | 6 | $T_w = T_e \times i_g \times i_f \times \eta$ | Wheel Torque | N·m | Tractive effort at wheel | | 7 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\tan^2 \phi - 1}{\tan^2 \phi + 1}\right)$ | U-Joint velocity condition | degrees | Universal joint design | | 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{clutch} \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{prop} \times \eta_{diff}$ | Overall driveline efficiency | Decimal | Total power loss | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Rule:** In the formula table, **never use the symbol `|` (vertical bar) inside table cells** — it breaks Markdown table parsing. Always write `Divides` or use `\vert` / `\mid` in LaTeX. ### 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Logic - **Why does a vehicle need a clutch?** Because the engine cannot be stopped and started for every gear change, and it cannot be "stalled" — the clutch absorbs the inertia mismatch. - **Why a gear box?** An engine has a narrow useful RPM range (~1000–6000 rpm), but vehicle demand varies from **0 km/h (hill start) to 200 km/h (highway)** — the gearbox bridges this gap. - **Why a differential?** A car turning on a curve has the *outer wheel* traveling a longer arc than the *inner wheel* — without a differential, the inner wheel would skid and the tyres would wear out. - **Why a U-joint?** The engine sits on rubber mounts, the rear axle bounces over bumps — the driveline angle is not fixed; the U-joint absorbs this angular variation. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The transmission is the *second most expensive assembly* in a vehicle (after the engine). Overhauling a transmission, rather than replacing it, saves **40–60% of cost** — hence the importance of skilled servicing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Overall Vehicle Speed and Tractive Effort The KTU 2024 Scheme commonly asks students to derive vehicle performance parameters starting from engine output. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation**. **Given:** - Engine torque, $T_e$ (N·m) - Engine speed, $N_e$ (rpm) - Gearbox ratio, $i_g$ - Final drive (differential) ratio, $i_f$ - Overall efficiency, $\eta_{overall}$ - Wheel rolling radius, $r$ (m) **Step 1 — Engine Power Output** Engine power is given by the standard rotational power equation: $$P_e = \frac{2 \pi N_e T_e}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$ > *Logic:* Power in watts is torque in N·m multiplied by angular velocity in rad/s. The factor $2\pi/60$ converts rpm into rad/s. **Step 2 — Torque Available at the Wheel** The gearbox multiplies torque by $i_g$ and the differential multiplies torque by $i_f$. However, each transfer incurs frictional losses accounted for by $\eta_{overall}$. Therefore: $$T_w = T_e \times i_g \times i_f \times \eta_{overall} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ > *Logic:* The gear train is a *torque transformer*. A smaller driven gear driven by a larger driver gear delivers more torque but at a lower speed. Efficiency $\eta < 1$ is multiplied at each stage. **Step 3 — Wheel Rotational Speed** The rotational speed reduces at each gear stage: $$N_w = \frac{N_e}{i_g \times i_f} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$ > *Logic:* Since gear ratio is the inverse of speed ratio, an increase in torque by factor $i$ implies a decrease in speed by the same factor $i$. **Step 4 — Linear Vehicle Speed** Linear vehicle speed is the wheel's peripheral velocity: $$v = \frac{\pi D_w N_w}{60} = \frac{2 \pi r N_w}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$ Substituting $N_w$: $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{2 \pi r}{60} \times \frac{N_e}{i_g \times i_f} \\ &= \frac{\pi r N_e}{30 \times i_g \times i_f} \quad \text{[m/s]} \end{aligned}$$ To convert to km/h, multiply by 3.6: $$v = 0.377 \times \frac{r N_e}{i_g \times i_f} \quad \text{[km/h]}$$ > *Logic:* The constant $0.377$ comes from $\dfrac{2 \pi \times 60 \times 60}{1000 \times 1000}$ rounding off. **Step 5 — Tractive Effort at Wheel** Tractive effort is the force pushing the vehicle forward at the tyre–road contact patch: $$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{T_e \times i_g \times i_f \times \eta_{overall}}{r} \quad \text{[N]}$$ > *Logic:* A lever arm of length $r$ converts wheel torque into a linear force. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component-Level Maintenance Decision This matrix maps each basic transmission part to its typical fault, the inspection method, and the KTU-recommended servicing action. | Component | Typical Fault | Inspection Method | Service / Overhaul Action | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | Clutch Disc | Slipping, judder | Rev engine in high gear; smell | Replace friction lining; check flywheel | Every 60,000 km | | Pressure Plate | Diaphragm spring weak | Measure free length with vernier | Re-grind or replace | As needed | | Throw-out Bearing | Noisy on depress | Listen with stethoscope | Replace (sealed unit) | Every clutch job | | Gearbox | Hard shift, oil leak | Drain & inspect oil, road test | Drain, flush, refill; sync ring check | Oil change 40,000 km | | Propeller Shaft | Vibration at speed | Mark and rotate 180°; recheck | Re-balance; replace U-joints | Every 20,000 km | | U-Joint | Click, grease leak | Jack up, rotate by hand | Repack grease; replace if seized | Every 30,000 km | | CV Joint | Click on full lock turns | Drive slowly in tight circle | Replace boot; repack or replace joint | Every 50,000 km | | Differential | Whine on deceleration | Coast in neutral at speed | Drain, refill gear oil; check backlash | Every 60,000 km | | Axle Bearing | Hum that varies with speed | Lift and spin wheel | Repack grease; replace bearing | Every 80,000 km | ### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A vehicle has the following data: $T_e = 200 \text{ N·m}$ at $N_e = 3000$ rpm, $i_g = 2.5$, $i_f = 4.1$, $\eta = 0.85$, $r = 0.32$ m. Find (a) vehicle speed in km/h, and (b) tractive effort. **Solution:** (a) Compute $N_w$: $$N_w = \frac{3000}{2.5 \times 4.1} = \frac{3000}{10.25} = 292.68 \text{ rpm}$$ Compute $v$: $$v = 0.377 \times \frac{0.32 \times 3000}{10.25} = 0.377 \times 93.66 = 35.31 \text{ km/h}$$ (b) Compute $T_w$: $$T_w = 200 \times 2.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.85 = 1742.5 \text{ N·m}$$ Compute $F_t$: $$F_t = \frac{1742.5}{0.32} = 5445.3 \text{ N} \approx 5.45 \text{ kN}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** Always retain at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps, then round only the final answer. Examiners deduct marks for premature rounding. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Flow Through the Transmission System This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the sequential power flow from the engine to the driving wheels, with the function of each component labelled explicitly. ```mermaid flowchart LR engNodeA[Engine
Produces torque and RPM] flyNodeB[Flywheel
Stores rotational inertia] cluNodeC[Clutch
Engage or Disengage power] gbNodeD[Gearbox
Changes torque and speed ratio] propNodeE[Propeller Shaft
Transmits torque over distance] ujointNodeF[Universal Joint
Permits angular misalignment] diffNodeG[Differential
Splits torque left and right] axleNodeH[Axle Shafts
Deliver power to wheels] wheelNodeI[Driving Wheels
Apply tractive force to road] engNodeA --> flyNodeB flyNodeB --> cluNodeC cluNodeC --> gbNodeD gbNodeD --> propNodeE propNodeE --> ujointNodeF ujointNodeF --> diffNodeG diffNodeG --> axleNodeH axleNodeH --> wheelNodeI style engNodeA fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style flyNodeB fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style cluNodeC fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style gbNodeD fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style propNodeE fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style ujointNodeF fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style diffNodeG fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style axleNodeH fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style wheelNodeI fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Servicing & Overhauling Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD step1[Step 1: Symptom Diagnosis
Road test and customer complaint] step2[Step 2: Visual and Auditory Inspection
Check for leaks, noise, vibration] step3[Step 3: Oil Analysis
Drain, inspect for metal particles] step4[Step 4: Disassembly
Mark parts; use pullers and fixtures] step5[Step 5: Component Cleaning
Solvent bath and ultrasonic cleaning] step6[Step 6: Inspection of Wear Parts
Measure with micrometer and dial gauge] step7[Step 7: Replacement Decision
Within tolerance reuse or replace] step8[Step 8: Reassembly with New Seals
Use torque wrench and thread locker] step9[Step 9: Road Test Verification
Recheck noise, leak, and shift quality] step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6 step6 --> step7 step7 --> step8 step8 --> step9 style step1 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#000 style step4 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000 style step6 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000 style step8 fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#000 style step9 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Component Cross-Section Reference — Differential Unit ```mermaid flowchart TB pinionA[Pinion Gear
Drives the ring gear] ringB[Crown Wheel
Ring gear bolted to differential case] caseC[Differential Case
Houses side and planet gears] sideD[Side Gear Left
Connects to left axle shaft] sideE[Side Gear Right
Connects to right axle shaft] planetF[Planet Gear 1
Spins on cross pin during turn] planetG[Planet Gear 2
Opposite to planet 1] pinH[Cross Pin Spider
Carries planet gears] pinionA --> ringB ringB --> caseC caseC --> sideD caseC --> sideE caseC --> planetF caseC --> planetG caseC --> pinH style pinionA fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000 style ringB fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000 style caseC fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style sideD fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#fff style sideE fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#fff style planetF fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#000 style planetG fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#000 style pinH fill:#8338ec,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs use purely alphanumeric identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `engNodeA`, `step1`, `pinionA`). Reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, and `style` are NOT used as node names. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italics, or HTML tags inside quotes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List any **six basic parts** of an automobile transmission system and state the function of each in one line. **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** | # | Part | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | Clutch | Engages/disengages engine from gearbox | | 2 | Gear Box | Provides multiple torque-speed ratios | | 3 | Propeller Shaft | Transmits torque from gearbox to differential | | 4 | Universal Joint | Allows angular misalignment between shafts | | 5 | Differential | Splits torque and permits wheel-speed difference | | 6 | Axle Shafts | Deliver final drive torque to wheels | *(1 mark for correctly listing six parts; 1 mark for the function of clutch; 1 mark for the function of differential or any other two parts.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the difference between a **single-plate clutch** and a **multi-plate clutch**? Mention one advantage of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A single-plate clutch has **one friction disc** between the flywheel and pressure plate; a multi-plate clutch has **two or more friction discs** stacked alternately with steel plates. (2 marks) - Advantage: Single-plate — *simple, easy to service, good heat dissipation*; Multi-plate — *higher torque capacity in compact space, used in heavy vehicles and racing*. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Provided > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question contains two sub-parts — (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is provided **between two main questions** (not within sub-parts). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper PEMUT635]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **single-plate clutch**. State the **clutch torque capacity equation** and define each term. (7 marks) **(b)** A single-plate clutch has a friction disc with **outer radius $R_o = 150$ mm** and **inner radius $R_i = 100$ mm**. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.3$, and the axial clamping force is $W = 4000$ N assuming **uniform pressure theory**. Calculate the **torque capacity** of the clutch. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working — Single Plate Clutch (7 marks)** The single-plate clutch consists of the following parts: 1. **Flywheel** — bolted to the engine crankshaft; provides one friction surface. 2. **Clutch Plate/Disc** — carries the friction lining on both sides; splined to the gearbox input shaft. 3. **Pressure Plate** — bolted to the flywheel via spring tabs; provides the clamping force. 4. **Clutch Springs** — typically diaphragm-type in modern cars; provide axial clamping force. 5. **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** — pushes the pressure plate fingers/springs when the pedal is depressed. 6. **Clutch Pedal & Linkage** — mechanical or hydraulic actuation. **Working Principle:** - When the pedal is **released**, the diaphragm spring clamps the clutch plate between the flywheel and pressure plate. Friction transmits torque from engine to gearbox. - When the pedal is **pressed**, the release bearing pushes the diaphragm spring fingers inward, the pressure plate moves back, the clutch plate is released, and engine-gearbox connection is **broken**. **Torque Capacity Equation:** $$T_c = \mu \times W \times R_m$$ where $R_m$ is the **mean friction radius**: $$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$ For uniform pressure assumption: $$T_c = \frac{2 \mu W}{3} \times \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$ (1 mark for diagram description, 2 marks for parts list, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definition of terms.) --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $R_o = 0.150$ m, $R_i = 0.100$ m - $\mu = 0.3$, $W = 4000$ N - Uniform pressure theory. **Step 1 — Compute $R_o^3$ and $R_i^3$:** $$R_o^3 = 0.150^3 = 0.003375 \text{ m}^3$$ $$R_i^3 = 0.100^3 = 0.001000 \text{ m}^3$$ $$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 0.002375 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_o^2$ and $R_i^2$:** $$R_o^2 = 0.150^2 = 0.0225 \text{ m}^2$$ $$R_i^2 = 0.100^2 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2$$ $$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.0125 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute $R_m$:** $$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{0.002375}{0.0125} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.190 = 0.1267 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $T_c$:** $$T_c = \mu \times W \times R_m = 0.3 \times 4000 \times 0.1267$$ $$T_c = 1520 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.52 \text{ kN·m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification using the integrated form:** $T_c = \dfrac{2 \mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.3 \times 4000 \times 0.002375}{3 \times 0.0125} = \dfrac{5.7}{0.0375} = 1520$ N·m. **Both methods agree.** ✓ (2 marks for substituting values, 2 marks for computing $R_m$, 2 marks for final torque, 1 mark for unit and final answer.) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PEMUT635]** **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a constant-mesh gearbox** with a neat diagram. State any **two advantages** over a sliding-mesh gearbox. (7 marks) **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure for overhauling a propeller shaft assembly** including inspection of the universal joint. (7 marks) **Model Solution Outline (for self-study):** **(a) Constant-Mesh Gearbox (7 marks):** - All gears on the countershaft are in **constant mesh** with corresponding gears on the main shaft. - Power transmission is achieved by **synchronizer units** (synchro-rings) that lock a particular gear to the main shaft. - Synchronizer parts: hub, sleeve, baulk rings, and shift fork. - **Advantages over sliding mesh:** (i) Gear teeth are not damaged by sliding contact → longer life; (ii) Easier and smoother gear changes; (iii) Quieter operation; (iv) Less driver skill required. **(b) Propeller Shaft Overhauling Procedure (7 marks):** 1. **Removal:** Mark the shaft and U-joint yokes to preserve balance phasing. Drop the shaft. 2. **Disassembly:** Remove snap rings, press out bearing cups from yoke ears. 3. **Cleaning:** Solvent wash, inspect needle bearings, and check spider trunnions for brinelling. 4. **Inspection:** Check shaft runout (max 0.05 mm), yoke ears for cracks (dye-penetrant test), and spline condition. 5. **Replacement:** Replace bearings and seals; repack with chassis grease. 6. **Reassembly:** Press new cups, install new snap rings, ensure free movement without binding. 7. **Refitting & Balancing:** Reinstall in original phasing; recheck for vibration on road test. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Failing to write the formula before substituting values** — Examiners deduct **1 mark** if the equation is not stated. > 2. **Unit mismatch** — Writing torque in kgf·m instead of N·m is a common error; **−0.5 to −1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., uniform pressure vs uniform wear) — **−1 mark**. > 4. **Confusing gear ratio with speed ratio sign convention** — gear ratio $> 1$ for reduction; remember: small driver, large driven. > 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — In (a) parts, an unlabeled sketch forfeits **2 of 7 marks** in KTU 2024 valuation. > 6. **Skipping the "why"** — Examiners award 1 mark in 7-mark answers for explaining the *physical reason* behind the formula, not just the math. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **transmission system** consists of **clutch, gearbox, propeller shaft, U-joint/CV joint, differential, and axles**. - ✅ **Clutch torque capacity** (uniform pressure): $T_c = \dfrac{2 \mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ - ✅ **Clutch torque capacity** (uniform wear): $T_c = \dfrac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2}$ - ✅ **Vehicle speed:** $v = 0.377 \times \dfrac{r N_e}{i_g \times i_f}$ km/h - ✅ **Tractive effort:** $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \times i_g \times i_f \times \eta}{r}$ - ✅ **Constant-mesh gearbox** is the standard in modern cars — uses **synchronizers** for smooth shifting. - ✅ **Differential** splits torque 50:50 in straight motion; allows speed difference during turning via planet gears. - ✅ **Propeller shaft** must be dynamically balanced and re-phased correctly after reassembly. - ✅ **U-joint** angle should be **less than 20°** to avoid severe torsional vibration. - ✅ Overhauling process follows: **Diagnose → Inspect → Disassemble → Clean → Measure → Replace → Reassemble → Test**. - ✅ **Most common KTU exam topic:** single-plate clutch numerical + gearbox function theory. - ✅ Always retain the **original phasing marks** on the propeller shaft and U-joints during dismantling. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the **power-flow sequence** (Engine → Flywheel → Clutch → Gearbox → Prop-Shaft → U-Joint → Differential → Axle → Wheel). The examiner often gives this as a 3-mark "list and explain" question.

topics.title: Preventive Maintenance and Repairs of Automotive Transmission Components: Clutch, Gearbox, Differential, Drive Shafts and Final Drive
Symptoms and Possible Faults in Clutch, Manual Transmission, Automatic Transmission and Drive Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Symptoms and Possible Faults in Clutch, Manual Transmission, Automatic Transmission and Drive Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition: Drivetrain Fault Symptomatology

In the context of **Vehicle Maintenance and Troubleshooting (PEMUT635)**, the term *"Symptoms and Possible Faults"* refers to the **systematic diagnostic methodology** used by automotive service technicians and KTU-trained engineers to identify the root cause of mechanical, hydraulic, or electronic malfunctions within a vehicle's power transmission system.

The drivetrain fault chain typically follows this hierarchy:

$$ \text{Symptom (Driver Observation)} \rightarrow \text{Diagnostic Test (Workshop)} \rightarrow \text{Fault (Root Cause)} \rightarrow \text{Remedial Action (Repair)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** A "symptom" is the observable consequence (noise, vibration, slippage, fluid leak) that the driver or technician notices. A "fault" is the underlying mechanical failure (worn friction plate, cracked synchronizer ring, burnt torque converter, seized U-joint) responsible for that symptom.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Vehicle as the Human Body

Imagine the vehicle's powertrain as a human body:

| Vehicle Drivetrain Component | Human Body Analogy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Clutch | The hand that gently disconnects a fan's plug | Disengages engine from wheels |
| Manual Transmission | A gearbox that changes walking speed | Provides torque multiplication |
| Automatic Transmission | An automatic heart rate adjuster | Self-selects optimal gear ratio |
| Drive Shaft | The spine connecting brain to legs | Transmits rotational torque |

When your "back" (drive shaft) hurts, your "legs" (wheels) cannot rotate properly — exactly like a fractured spine crippling leg movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse "symptoms" with "faults." Symptom = what you observe (e.g., noise). Fault = what is physically broken (e.g., worn gear teeth). Always distinguish these two clearly in 14-mark answers.

### 1.3 The Four Sub-Systems Covered in This Module

1. **Clutch Assembly** — Friction-based torque interrupter
2. **Manual Transmission (MT)** — Synchromesh gearbox with manual gear selection
3. **Automatic Transmission (AT)** — Hydraulic/electronic self-shifting gearbox
4. **Drive Shafts** — Torque-transmitting rotating shafts (propeller & half shafts)

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 maps directly to **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Diagnose common drivetrain faults and recommend appropriate remedial procedures."* Every symptom-fault pair tested in exams directly evaluates this CO at **Bloom's Level Apply / Analyze (Level 3 & 4).**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Clutch — Symptoms and Possible Faults

The clutch is a **friction coupling device** that transmits engine torque to the gearbox input shaft on engagement and disconnects it on pedal depression.

#### 2.1.1 Symptom-Fault Matrix for Clutch

| # | Symptom (Driver Observation) | Possible Fault (Root Cause) | Diagnostic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clutch Slippage** — Engine RPM rises but vehicle speed does not | Worn friction disc, weak pressure plate springs, oil contamination on disc | Run stall test, inspect disc thickness |
| 2 | **Clutch Drag** — Gear crunch when shifting from neutral to 1st | Misadjusted free-play, warped pressure plate, seized piston in hydraulic system | Measure pedal free-play |
| 3 | **Judder / Vibration** — Car shudders during take-off | Warped flywheel, worn pilot bearing, oil on friction surface | Inspect flywheel with dial gauge |
| 4 | **Noise on Pedal Press** — Squeal or rattle when clutch pedal depressed | Worn release bearing, dry pivot points, broken tension springs | Listen with stethoscope at bell housing |
| 5 | **Hard Pedal Effort** — Excessive foot force required | Faulty master/slave cylinder, worn hydraulic seals, misaligned linkage | Pressure test hydraulic system |
| 6 | **Clutch Pedal Sticking to Floor** | Master cylinder failure, air in hydraulic line | Bleed hydraulic system |
| 7 | **Burning Smell** | Severely worn friction material, overheated pressure plate | Inspect for glazing |
| 8 | **No Power Transfer** | Broken friction disc, broken pressure plate fingers | Bench-test assembly |

#### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet for Clutch Diagnostics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Formulas** — frequently tested in university numerical/derivation questions.

$$ T_{\text{clutch}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n $$

Where:
- $T_{\text{clutch}}$ = Torque transmitted by the clutch (**N·m**)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (**dimensionless**, typically **0.25 to 0.45** for organic linings)
- $F_{\text{axial}}$ = Axial clamping force from pressure plate (**N**)
- $R_{\text{mean}}$ = Mean effective radius of friction surface (**m**)
- $n$ = Number of friction surfaces (single plate clutch $n = 2$)

**Free-play relationship:**

$$ F_{\text{axial}} = k \cdot (x_0 - x) $$

Where $x_0$ = original spring compression, $x$ = current compression, $k$ = spring stiffness.

**Slip condition threshold:**

$$ \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n \geq T_{\text{engine,max}} $$

If this inequality fails, **clutch slippage** occurs.

### 2.2 Manual Transmission (MT) — Symptoms and Possible Faults

#### 2.2.1 Symptom-Fault Matrix for Manual Transmission

| # | Symptom | Possible Fault | Diagnostic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Grinding noise when shifting** | Worn synchronizer rings, worn dog clutches, low gear oil | Check lubricant level/viscosity |
| 2 | **Difficulty engaging gear** | Worn shift fork, bent selector rod, clutch drag | Inspect shift mechanism |
| 3 | **Gear pops out of mesh** | Worn shift fork groove, weak detent spring, stretched selector | Replace detent spring |
| 4 | **Whining / Humming noise** | Worn bearings, low oil, incorrect gear oil grade | Drain & refill with correct grade |
| 5 | **Oil leak from gearbox** | Damaged oil seals, cracked housing, loose bolts | Pressure wash & trace leak |
| 6 | **Noisy in neutral, quiet in gear** | Input shaft bearing failure | Bench inspect input shaft |
| 7 | **Restricted movement of shift lever** | Worn bushes, damaged linkage, misaligned cables | Lubricate shift linkage |
| 8 | **Oil frothy / Milky** | Water ingress, failed input shaft seal | Drain, flush, replace seal |

#### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet for Manual Transmission

$$ \text{Gear Ratio } (i) = \frac{N_{\text{input}}}{N_{\text{output}}} = \frac{Z_{\text{driven}}}{Z_{\text{driver}}} $$

$$ v_{\text{vehicle}} = \frac{0.377 \cdot N_{\text{engine}} \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}}{i_{\text{gear}} \cdot i_{\text{final}}} $$

Where:
- $v_{\text{vehicle}}$ = Vehicle speed (**km/h**)
- $N_{\text{engine}}$ = Engine RPM
- $r_{\text{wheel}}$ = Effective wheel radius (**m**)
- $i_{\text{gear}}$ = Transmission gear ratio
- $i_{\text{final}}$ = Final drive ratio
- The constant **0.377** converts m/s to km/h

**Synchronizer torque balance:**

$$ T_{\text{sync}} = I_{\text{shaft}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{sync}} $$

$$ \alpha_{\text{sync}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{input}} - \omega_{\text{output}}}{t_{\text{sync}}} $$

If synchronization time $t_{\text{sync}}$ becomes too long (worn baulk rings), gear grinding results.

### 2.3 Automatic Transmission (AT) — Symptoms and Possible Faults

#### 2.3.1 Symptom-Fault Matrix for Automatic Transmission

| # | Symptom | Possible Fault | Diagnostic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Delayed engagement (Drive/Reverse)** | Low ATF, worn front clutch, faulty pressure regulator | Check ATF level & condition |
| 2 | **Slippage during shifts** | Worn clutch packs, burnt friction discs, weak servo | Pressure test |
| 3 | **Harsh / Hard shifts** | Sticky valve body, worn accumulator, faulty shift solenoid | Scan ECU for codes |
| 4 | **No Reverse** | Failed reverse band, broken reverse idler gear | Inspect band servo |
| 5 | **Torque converter shudder** | Worn TCC clutch lining, contaminated ATF | Drain & flush ATF |
| 6 | **Overheating transmission** | Cooler failure, low ATF, blocked cooler lines | Test cooler flow rate |
| 7 | **Fluid leak (red/brown puddle)** | Pan gasket, cooler line seal, pump seal | Dye-test |
| 8 | **Transmission warning light ON** | Sensor fault (speed, pressure, temperature) | OBD-II scan |
| 9 | **Limp mode activation** | Major internal failure, ECU detected anomaly | Read DTCs |
| 10 | **Noisy in all gears** | Pump failure, planetary gear damage | Disassemble & inspect |

#### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet for Automatic Transmission

**Torque converter stall torque relationship:**

$$ T_{\text{stall}} = T_{\text{engine,max}} \cdot \text{SR} $$

Where SR = **Stall Ratio** (typically **1.8 to 2.5** for passenger cars).

**Converter slip speed:**

$$ \text{Slip}_{\text{converter}} = N_{\text{engine}} - N_{\text{turbine}} $$

Typical slip = **50 to 200 RPM** at cruising. Higher slip → worn lock-up clutch.

**Hydraulic line pressure (governor equation):**

$$ P_{\text{line}} = P_{\text{regulator}} + \Delta P_{\text{governor}} $$

**ATF temperature fault threshold:**

$$ T_{\text{ATF,critical}} = \mathbf{130^{\circ}\text{C}} $$

Above this, ATF oxidation accelerates and clutch pack life reduces dramatically.

### 2.4 Drive Shafts — Symptoms and Possible Faults

#### 2.4.1 Symptom-Fault Matrix for Drive Shafts

| # | Symptom | Possible Fault | Diagnostic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vibration during acceleration** | Worn U-joints, bent shaft,不平衡 (imbalance) | Measure run-out with dial gauge |
| 2 | **Clunking on gear engagement** | Worn U-joint needle bearings, loose slip yoke splines | Inspect U-joints |
| 3 | **Squeaking / Squealing** | Dry U-joints, lack of grease | Repack U-joints |
| 4 | **Clicking on turns (CV joints)** | Torn CV boot, worn CV joint | Inspect boot & joint |
| 5 | **Grease splatter on wheel arch** | Torn CV boot | Replace boot & repack grease |
| 6 | **Vibration at highway speeds** | Imbalanced propeller shaft, worn center bearing | Measure balance & run-out |
| 7 | **Growling noise at constant speed** | Worn center support bearing | Replace center bearing |

#### 2.4.2 KTU Formula Sheet for Drive Shafts

**Critical speed equation (whirling vibration):**

$$ N_{\text{critical}} = \frac{9.55}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{\rho \cdot A}} $$

Where:
- $N_{\text{critical}}$ = Critical speed (**RPM**)
- $L$ = Shaft length between supports (**m**)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (**N/m²**)
- $I$ = Area moment of inertia (**m⁴**)
- $\rho$ = Material density (**kg/m³**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (**m²**)
- The constant **9.55** = $30/\pi$ (converts rad/s to RPM)

**Operating safety condition:**

$$ N_{\text{operating}} < 0.8 \cdot N_{\text{critical}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Drive shafts must always operate below **80% of their critical speed** to avoid destructive whirling vibrations. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU university exams.

**U-joint operating angle limits:**

$$ \phi_{\text{max}} \leq 7^{\circ} \text{ (per joint)} $$

$$ \phi_1 - \phi_2 \leq 1^{\circ} \text{ (U-joint phasing)} $$

If the angle exceeds these limits, **vibration and U-joint failure** occur rapidly.

**Power transmission in drive shaft:**

$$ P = \frac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} $$

Where:
- $P$ = Power transmitted (**W**)
- $N$ = Shaft RPM
- $T$ = Torque (**N·m**)
- The constant $\pi$ = **3.14159...**

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Clutch Slippage Threshold Condition

**Objective:** Derive the engineering condition under which a clutch begins to slip — frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

**Given:**
- Single plate clutch with $n = 2$ friction surfaces
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$
- Uniform pressure distribution assumption (elementary theory)
- Mean radius $R_{\text{mean}} = (R_o + R_i)/2$ where $R_o$ = outer radius, $R_i$ = inner radius

**Step 1 — Frictional force on an elemental ring:**

Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ on the friction plate. The normal force on this ring (with uniform pressure $p$) is:

$$ dF_{\text{normal}} = p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr $$

**Step 2 — Frictional force on the elemental ring:**

The frictional (tangential) force opposing rotation is:

$$ dF_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot dF_{\text{normal}} = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr $$

**Step 3 — Elemental torque about clutch axis:**

The elemental torque $dT$ contributed by this ring is frictional force multiplied by the lever arm $r$:

$$ dT = r \cdot dF_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \cdot dr $$

**Step 4 — Integrate across the friction surface:**

Integrate $dT$ from inner radius $R_i$ to outer radius $R_o$:

$$ T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr $$

$$ T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr $$

$$ T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} $$

$$ T = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \cdot (R_o^3 - R_i^3) $$

**Step 5 — Convert uniform pressure to total axial force:**

Total axial clamping force:

$$ F_{\text{axial}} = p \cdot \pi \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2) $$

Solving for $p$:

$$ p = \frac{F_{\text{axial}}}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} $$

**Step 6 — Substitute p back into torque equation:**

$$ T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \frac{F_{\text{axial}}}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} \cdot (R_o^3 - R_i^3) $$

$$ T = \frac{2\mu F_{\text{axial}}}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} $$

**Step 7 — Apply the mean radius substitution:**

Using the identity:

$$ \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} = \frac{R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2}{R_o + R_i} $$

And defining mean radius:

$$ R_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} $$

We obtain the canonical KTU formula:

$$ \boxed{T_{\text{clutch}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n} $$

**Step 8 — Slippage threshold condition:**

The clutch begins to slip when transmitted torque exceeds available friction torque:

$$ \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n < T_{\text{engine,max}} \rightarrow \text{SLIPPAGE OCCURS} $$

This is the **engineering diagnosis criterion** used by mechanics worldwide to determine clutch wear.

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Speed of a Drive Shaft

**Objective:** Derive the whirling critical speed formula tested in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Model the drive shaft as a simply supported beam:**

The differential equation for transverse vibration of a uniform rotating shaft:

$$ EI \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Assume harmonic motion:**

Let $y(x,t) = Y(x) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ where $\omega$ = angular frequency in rad/s.

Substituting:

$$ EI \frac{d^4 Y}{dx^4} - \rho A \omega^2 Y = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Solve the ODE:**

The general solution for a simply supported beam with classical boundary conditions $Y(0) = Y(L) = 0$ and $Y''(0) = Y''(L) = 0$ yields:

$$ Y(x) = C \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right) $$

where $m$ = mode number (1, 2, 3, ...).

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find natural frequencies:**

$$ \omega_m^2 = \frac{m^4 \pi^4 EI}{\rho A L^4} $$

$$ \omega_m = \frac{m^2 \pi^2}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} $$

**Step 5 — Convert rad/s to RPM:**

Using $N = \omega \cdot 60 / (2\pi) = \omega \cdot 9.549$:

$$ N_{\text{critical}} = \frac{9.55 \cdot m^2 \pi^2}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} $$

For first mode ($m = 1$):

$$ \boxed{N_{\text{critical}} = \frac{9.55}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 EI}{\rho A}}} $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation for KTU answer:**

If a vehicle's propeller shaft is **1500 mm long** with steel construction ($E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³), the designer must ensure engine RPM never reaches the first critical speed during normal operation. The 80% safety rule applies.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Fault Isolation Tree

| Observed Symptom | First Test | If Positive → | If Negative → |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clutch slip | Stall test | Worn friction disc → replace | Proceed to next test |
| Clutch slip | Check free-play | If less than 10mm → adjust | Pressure plate fault |
| MT grinding | Check gear oil | Low/wrong grade → refill | Synchronizer worn |
| MT grinding | Check shift linkage | Worn bushes → replace | Internal gear fault |
| AT delayed engagement | Check ATF level | Low → top up | Worn front clutch |
| AT delayed engagement | Pressure test | Low pressure → pump fault | Seal leak |
| Drive shaft vibration | Measure run-out | $> 0.5$ mm → balance/swap | U-joint test |
| Drive shaft vibration | Rotate U-joints | Stiff/dry → replace | Center bearing test |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] List any four symptoms of a slipping clutch and state the possible fault for each.**

**Model Answer (4 symptoms × 0.75 marks each):**

1. **Engine RPM rises rapidly without proportional vehicle acceleration** → Worn friction disc lining (below minimum thickness).
2. **Burning smell during high-load driving** → Glazed or oil-contaminated friction surface.
3. **Loss of power at peak torque conditions (e.g., uphill)** → Weak or fatigued pressure plate springs.
4. **Vehicle creeps forward even with clutch fully depressed** → Friction disc not fully releasing (clutch drag).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Mention three symptoms indicating failure of CV joints in a front-wheel-drive vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 symptoms × 1 mark each):**

1. **Clicking noise during sharp turns** → Worn outer CV joint, typically due to torn CV boot and grease leakage.
2. **Grease splatter on inner wheel arch or undercarriage** → Torn CV boot allowing grease to escape.
3. **Vibration during acceleration in straight line** → Excessive play in inner CV joint or worn tripod bearing.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 3 carries internal choice. Choose **ONE** of the two questions. Each has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a single plate clutch. List any five symptoms of clutch failure with their possible faults. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A single plate clutch consists of the following components:

1. **Flywheel** — Bolted to engine crankshaft; provides one friction surface.
2. **Friction Disc (Clutch Plate)** — Carries the friction lining on both faces; splined to gearbox input shaft.
3. **Pressure Plate** — Bolted to flywheel via cover; exerts axial clamping force.
4. **Diaphragm Spring** — Conical spring providing the clamping force.
5. **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** — Pushes diaphragm fingers when clutch pedal is depressed.
6. **Clutch Fork & Pivot** — Mechanical linkage from pedal to release bearing.

**Working Principle:**
- When the clutch pedal is released, the diaphragm spring pushes the pressure plate against the friction disc, which is clamped between the pressure plate and flywheel. Engine torque is transmitted by friction.
- When the pedal is depressed, the release bearing pushes the diaphragm spring fingers inward, the pressure plate retracts, and the friction disc is released — disconnecting engine from gearbox.

**Five Symptoms of Clutch Failure:**

| # | Symptom | Possible Fault |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clutch slippage | Worn friction lining, weak spring |
| 2 | Clutch drag (gear grind) | Misadjusted free-play, seized slave cylinder |
| 3 | Judder during engagement | Warped flywheel, oil on friction surface |
| 4 | Squealing noise on pedal press | Worn release bearing |
| 5 | Hard pedal | Faulty master cylinder, air in hydraulic line |

**(b) Derive the expression for torque transmitted by a single plate clutch assuming uniform pressure theory. A single plate clutch has friction surfaces of 300 mm outer diameter and 200 mm inner diameter. If the coefficient of friction is 0.3 and axial force is 5000 N, find the torque transmitted. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

The torque transmitted by a single plate clutch under uniform pressure theory is derived by integrating the elemental friction torque over the friction surface area:

$$ T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr $$

$$ T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{3} $$

Total axial clamping force $F = p \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$, hence $p = \dfrac{F}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$.

Substituting:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi \mu F}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} $$

For uniform pressure theory, the effective mean radius is:

$$ R_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{T = \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n} $$

For single plate clutch, $n = 2$ (two friction surfaces).

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $R_o = 300/2 = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- $R_i = 200/2 = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $\mu = 0.3$
- $F_{\text{axial}} = 5000$ N
- $n = 2$

Compute $R_{\text{mean}}$:

$$ R_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} $$

$$ R_o^3 = (0.150)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$
$$ R_i^3 = (0.100)^3 = 1.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$
$$ R_o^2 = (0.150)^2 = 0.0225 \text{ m}^2 $$
$$ R_i^2 = (0.100)^2 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ R_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2(3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 1.000 \times 10^{-3})}{3(0.0225 - 0.0100)} $$

$$ R_{\text{mean}} = \frac{2 \times 2.375 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 0.0125} = \frac{4.750 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0375} = 0.1267 \text{ m} $$

**Final torque:**

$$ T = \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot R_{\text{mean}} \cdot n $$

$$ T = 0.3 \times 5000 \times 0.1267 \times 2 $$

$$ T = 380 \text{ N·m} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the symptoms and possible faults in a manual transmission gearbox. Describe any six common faults with their root causes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A manual transmission gearbox is a mechanical device that provides multiple gear ratios between the engine and drive wheels. Common symptoms and faults are:

| # | Symptom | Possible Fault |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grinding noise when shifting gears | Worn synchronizer rings (synchro rings), worn dog clutch teeth, incorrect gear oil grade |
| 2 | Gear pops out of mesh under load | Worn shift fork groove, weak detent spring, stretched selector mechanism |
| 3 | Difficulty engaging reverse gear | Worn reverse idler gear, damaged synchronizer for reverse |
| 4 | Whining or humming noise that varies with vehicle speed | Worn input/output shaft bearings, low or incorrect gear oil |
| 5 | Oil leak from gearbox housing | Damaged input/output shaft oil seals, cracked housing, loose cover bolts |
| 6 | Restricted/stiff gear lever movement | Worn shift linkage bushes, damaged cables, bent selector rod |
| 7 | Milky/frothy gear oil | Water ingress through failed seals, condensation |

**Diagnostic sequence:** Visual inspection → Oil check → Road test → Bench disassembly (if required).

**(b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an automatic transmission system. Discuss any four symptoms of automatic transmission failure with their probable causes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of Automatic Transmission:**

An automatic transmission consists of the following main subsystems:

1. **Torque Converter** — Hydraulic coupling replacing the manual clutch. Has three elements: impeller (driven by engine), turbine (drives gearbox), stator (redirects fluid for torque multiplication).
2. **Lock-Up Clutch** — Mechanically locks converter at highway speeds for efficiency.
3. **Planetary Gear Sets** — Provide multiple gear ratios through sun gears, planet gears, and ring gears.
4. **Hydraulic System** — Includes ATF pump, pressure regulator, valve body, and shift solenoids.
5. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU/TCU)** — Monitors speed, load, throttle, and temperature to command optimal shifts.

**Working:** Engine drives impeller → fluid coupling drives turbine → planetary gear sets select ratio based on hydraulic pressure → power transmitted to output shaft → ECU commands solenoid-operated valves for smooth shifts.

**Four Symptoms of AT Failure:**

| # | Symptom | Probable Cause |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Delayed engagement when shifting from P to D or R | Low ATF level, worn front clutch pack, faulty pressure regulator valve |
| 2 | Harsh or jerky shifts | Sticky valve body, worn accumulator springs, faulty shift solenoid |
| 3 | Torque converter shudder during light acceleration at 50-70 km/h | Worn lock-up clutch lining, contaminated ATF |
| 4 | Transmission overheating (warning light ON) | Failed transmission cooler, low ATF, blocked cooler lines, heavy towing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "AT failure = automatic clutch failure." This is **incorrect**. An AT has **no friction clutch** in the conventional sense — it uses a **torque converter** and **multiple wet clutch packs** inside the gearbox. Conflating these in your answer will lose 2-3 marks immediately.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3**

- ✅ A **symptom** is what the driver observes; a **fault** is the root mechanical cause — always distinguish.
- ✅ Clutch **slip** = friction insufficient; Clutch **drag** = friction not released.
- ✅ Mean radius for uniform pressure theory: $R_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$.
- ✅ Number of friction surfaces $n = 2$ for single plate, $n = 4$ for double plate clutch.
- ✅ Manual transmission **grinding** is almost always a **synchronizer ring** problem.
- ✅ Gear **pop-out** is a **detent spring** or **shift fork** issue — not a synchronizer problem.
- ✅ AT **delayed engagement** = first suspect is **ATF level**, then front clutch pack.
- ✅ AT **harsh shifts** = valve body / accumulator problem; **slipping** = worn clutch pack.
- ✅ Torque converter **stall ratio** = 1.8 to 2.5 for passenger cars.
- ✅ ATF critical temperature = **130°C** — above this, oxidation destroys clutch packs.
- ✅ Drive shaft must operate at less than **80% of critical speed** to avoid destructive whirling.
- ✅ Critical speed formula uses constant **9.55** (converts rad/s to RPM).
- ✅ U-joint maximum operating angle = **7°**; phasing difference ≤ **1°**.
- ✅ CV joint failure signature = **clicking on turns** + **grease on wheel arch**.
- ✅ Propeller shaft vibration at highway speed = imbalance → measure **run-out** (limit **0.5 mm**).
- ✅ Always write the **diagnosis sequence**: Symptom → Test → Fault → Remedy.
- ✅ In numerical problems, **state units clearly** (N·m, RPM, m/s²) — missing units lose marks.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Symptoms and Possible Faults in Clutch, Manual Transmission, Automatic Transmission and Drive Shafts**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Cooling System: Overheating Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Cooling System: Overheating Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Automotive Cooling System** is an integrated thermodynamic subsystem of an internal combustion (IC) engine designed to regulate and maintain the cylinder head, cylinder block, and lubricant temperatures within prescribed thermal limits, ensuring volumetric efficiency, optimal combustion, structural integrity, and emissions compliance. **Overheating** is defined as the abnormal condition where the engine operating temperature exceeds the manufacturer-specified upper threshold (typically **90°C to 105°C** for water-cooled petrol engines and **85°C to 95°C** for diesel engines), resulting from a thermal imbalance between heat generation in the combustion chambers and the heat rejection capability of the cooling circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> *Overheating is a SYMPTOM, not a fault itself.* It is the visible thermal manifestation of any one (or a combination) of: mechanical breakdown, fluid degradation, electrical failure, or thermodynamic imbalance in the heat-rejection loop.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Engine as a Human Body"

Think of the engine as a marathon runner and the cooling system as the runner's **sweating and breathing mechanism**:

- **Engine block** = Runner's muscles generating heat.
- **Coolant (water + glycol)** = Sweat (absorbs heat through evaporation/contact).
- **Radiator** = Skin surface where sweat evaporates and cools the body.
- **Water pump** = Heart, circulating the sweat (coolant).
- **Thermostat** = Brain's hypothalamus — decides when to open skin pores (let coolant flow to radiator) or shut them (retain heat for warm-up).
- **Fan** = A handheld fan accelerating evaporation.
- **Overheating** = The runner is in a closed, hot room with no air — body temperature rises dangerously.

If *any* part of this loop fails — blocked pores (clogged radiator), stopped heart (failed pump), or absent fan — the runner collapses. **Servicing, Repair, and Overhauling (SRO)** is the equivalent of giving the runner clean skin, a healthy heart, and a working fan.

### 1.3 Standard Thermal Metrics & Reference Thresholds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Temperature Bands (Industry-Standard KTU Values)**
>
> | Engine Type | Normal Op. Temp | Warning Threshold | Overheating (Red-Line) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Petrol (Water-Cooled) | 85°C – 95°C | 100°C | **> 105°C** |
> | Diesel (Water-Cooled) | 80°C – 90°C | 95°C | **> 100°C** |
> | Air-Cooled (2-stroke) | 120°C – 180°C | 200°C | **> 220°C** |
> | Turbocharged (Petrol) | 90°C – 105°C | 110°C | **> 115°C** |

Heat Rejection Capacity (typical passenger car): **35 kW to 75 kW** at full load.
Coolant Specific Heat Capacity: **$C_p = 3.81 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$** (50/50 ethylene glycol-water mix).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsystem Architecture of an Automotive Cooling System

The cooling system is structured into **five functionally interlocked subsystems**. Each must be inspected during SRO:

1. **Heat Source Side (Engine Block)**
   - Combustion chamber (heat generation zone).
   - Water jacket (coolant passages cast into block & head).
   - Cylinder head gasket (seals coolant from combustion gases — critical SRO item).

2. **Circulation Loop (Primary Circuit)**
   - Water pump (centrifugal, belt/electric driven).
   - Thermostat (wax-pellet type, opens at ~82°C–88°C).
   - Bypass circuit (allows coolant circulation before thermostat opens).

3. **Heat Rejection Side (Secondary Circuit / Radiator Loop)**
   - Radiator core (tubes, fins, tanks).
   - Cooling fan (mechanical viscous clutch / electric PWM / electromagnetic).
   - Expansion tank / degassing bottle.
   - Pressure cap (typically **0.9 bar to 1.4 bar** rating).

4. **Fluid Management**
   - Coolant composition (ethylene glycol + corrosion inhibitors + water).
   - pH level (maintained between **7.0 and 9.0**).
   - Freeze/boil protection range.

5. **Monitoring & Control**
   - Temperature gauge / sender unit (NTC thermistor).
   - ECU-controlled electric fan relay logic.
   - Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS) feeding the Engine Control Unit (ECU).

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Principle of Heat Rejection

The engine generates heat from fuel combustion. Approximately:

- **30%** converted to useful mechanical work.
- **30% to 35%** rejected via exhaust gases.
- **30% to 35%** rejected through the cooling system.
- Remaining 5%–10% lost via radiation and friction.

The cooling system must continuously dissipate this 30%–35% fraction, or the cylinder walls reach temperatures that cause:

- Lubricant oil breakdown (oil coking at > **150°C**).
- Pre-ignition and detonation (petrol engines).
- Thermal distortion of head gasket.
- Piston scuffing and seizure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Engine Cooling Thermodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All temperatures in the following equations MUST be in Kelvin (K)** for standard SI compliance. Conversions: $K = °C + 273.15$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) Heat Generated in Engine:} \quad Q_{\text{gen}} &= \dot{m}_f \times \text{NCV} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}} \\
\text{(2) Heat to be Rejected:} \quad Q_{\text{rej}} &= Q_{\text{gen}} \times f_{\text{cool}} \\
\text{(3) Sensible Heat Carried by Coolant:} \quad Q_{\text{cool}} &= \dot{m}_c \times C_{p,c} \times \Delta T \\
\text{(4) Heat Dissipated by Radiator:} \quad Q_{\text{rad}} &= U \times A \times \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} \\
\text{(5) Log Mean Temp Difference:} \quad \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
\text{(6) Coolant Mass Flow Required:} \quad \dot{m}_c &= \frac{Q_{\text{rej}}}{C_{p,c} \times (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})} \\
\text{(7) Boiling Point Elevation (Pressure Cap):} \quad T_{\text{boil}} &\approx 100°C + (\text{Pressure in bar}) \times 15°C \\
\text{(8) Fan Power (Air-side):} \quad P_{\text{fan}} &= \dot{V}_{\text{air}} \times \Delta P_{\text{static}} \\
\text{(9) Pump Power:} \quad P_{\text{pump}} &= \frac{\dot{V}_c \times \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{pump}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbol Glossary (units strictly SI):**

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\dot{m}_f$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| $C_{p,c}$ | Specific heat of coolant | $\approx$ 3.81 kJ/kg·K |
| $\dot{m}_c$ | Coolant mass flow rate | kg/s |
| $T_{\text{in}}, T_{\text{out}}$ | Coolant inlet/outlet temperature | K |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |
| $A$ | Radiator frontal/core surface area | m² |
| $\eta_{\text{pump}}$ | Pump mechanical efficiency | $\approx$ 0.60 – 0.75 |
| $\Delta P$ | Pressure differential across pump | Pa |
| $\dot{V}_{\text{air}}$ | Volumetric airflow through radiator | m³/s |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Radiator Sizing:** Formula (4) is used by Tata, Mahindra, Maruti engineers to size radiators for new vehicles, ensuring heat dissipation matches engine output even at **40°C ambient** with **AC ON** (which adds ~3–5 kW parasitic load).
- **Pressure Cap Selection:** Formula (7) shows why a **1.0 bar** cap elevates boiling point to ~**115°C**, preventing boil-over at altitude.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Using Formula (6), workshops compute if the existing pump is delivering enough coolant — if not, overheating is mechanically inevitable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix of Overheating Root Causes

A complete KTU-style SRO study of overheating requires mapping every symptom to its root cause. Below is the analytical diagnostic matrix:

| # | Subsystem | Symptom | Root Cause | Diagnostic Method | Repair Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coolant | Milky residue on oil cap | **Head gasket failure** | Compression + combustion leak test | Replace head gasket, skive head |
| 2 | Coolant | Low level, no visible leak | **Internal leak** (into oil or combustion) | Pressure test, exhaust gas bubbles in header tank | Overhaul head gasket |
| 3 | Radiator | External leak | Corrosion, hose clamp failure | Visual + pressure test | Repair/replace radiator |
| 4 | Radiator | High temp, AC off, idle | **Clogged core** (debris, scale) | Infrared thermometer at inlet vs outlet | Reverse flush or replace |
| 5 | Thermostat | Slow warm-up, then sudden rise | Stuck-open / stuck-closed thermostat | Feel upper/lower radiator hose temperature differential | Replace thermostat + gasket |
| 6 | Water Pump | Whining noise, leakage | **Bearing failure** or seal failure | Listen with stethoscope, shaft play check | Replace pump assembly |
| 7 | Fan | Temp climbs at idle | **Fan clutch failure** or burnt motor | Observe fan spin at hot idle | Replace fan clutch/motor |
| 8 | Cooling Fan | Temp climbs only at speed | Low coolant flow at high RPM (cavitation) | Check pump impeller | Replace pump |
| 9 | Pressure Cap | Boil-over, no overflow leak | **Cap pressure relief failure** | Cap tester gauge | Replace cap |
| 10 | Electrical | Gauge reads max immediately | Open CTS circuit / faulty gauge | Scan ECU codes, multimeter CTS | Replace sensor |
| 11 | Lubrication | Oil level overfull | Coolant ingressed into sump | Oil analysis, dipstick emulsion | Head gasket + oil change |
| 12 | Combustion | Detonation / pinging | Lean mixture, retarded timing | OBD scan, knock sensor data | Tune engine |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Coolant Mass Flow Required

**Problem Statement (KTU Board Style):**
A 4-cylinder petrol engine develops **75 kW** of brake power at rated RPM. The fuel consumption is **0.30 kg/kWh**, and **30%** of the total fuel energy must be rejected by the cooling system. The calorific value of fuel is **43 MJ/kg**, and the coolant (50:50 glycol-water) enters the engine at **75°C** and leaves at **95°C**. Determine:
(i) The total heat to be rejected per second.
(ii) The coolant mass flow rate required.

**Given:**
- Brake Power, $P_b = 75$ kW
- Specific Fuel Consumption, $\text{bsfc} = 0.30$ kg/kWh
- Calorific Value of fuel, $\text{NCV} = 43$ MJ/kg $= 43000$ kJ/kg
- Fraction rejected via cooling, $f_{\text{cool}} = 0.30$
- Coolant Specific Heat, $C_{p,c} = 3.81$ kJ/kg·K
- Coolant inlet temp, $T_{\text{in}} = 75°C = 348.15$ K
- Coolant outlet temp, $T_{\text{out}} = 95°C = 368.15$ K

---

**Step 1 — Compute the Fuel Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}_f$):**

Since bsfc is given per hour, convert time base to seconds.

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{P_b \times \text{bsfc}}{3600}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{75 \times 0.30}{3600}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{22.5}{3600}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = 0.00625 \text{ kg/s}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Total Heat Input Rate ($\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$):**

The total heat energy supplied by the fuel per second is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{NCV}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.00625 \times 43000
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 268.75 \text{ kW}
$$

This represents the total chemical energy being released per second.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Heat to be Rejected by the Cooling System ($\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}}$):**

Given that 30% of the input heat must be removed by the coolant:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = f_{\text{cool}} \times \dot{Q}_{\text{in}}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = 0.30 \times 268.75
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = 80.625 \text{ kW}}
$$

This is the thermal load that the cooling system must handle continuously.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Coolant Temperature Rise ($\Delta T$):**

Since both $T_{\text{in}}$ and $T_{\text{out}}$ are already in Kelvin via the +273.15 conversion, we can equivalently use °C difference (the conversion cancels out):

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 95 - 75
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 20 \text{ K (equivalent to 20°C)}}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Apply the Sensible Heat Equation to Find Coolant Mass Flow ($\dot{m}_c$):**

The fundamental energy balance for the coolant stream is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = \dot{m}_c \times C_{p,c} \times \Delta T
$$

Rearranging for $\dot{m}_c$:

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}}}{C_{p,c} \times \Delta T}
$$

Substituting the numerical values (keep $\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}}$ in kW = kJ/s):

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{80.625}{3.81 \times 20}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{80.625}{76.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_c \approx 1.058 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Interpretation & Engineering Verification:**

Converting to volumetric flow rate (assuming coolant density $\rho_c \approx 1.07$ kg/L):

$$
\dot{V}_c = \frac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{1.058}{1.07} \approx 0.989 \text{ L/s}
$$

This equals **~59.3 L/min**, which is realistic for a mid-size 4-cylinder engine (typical OEM specification: **50–80 L/min**). If the water pump delivers less than this, overheating at high load is mathematically guaranteed.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Pressure Cap Boiling Point

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the new boiling point of the coolant when the system is sealed with a **1.2 bar** pressure cap. The atmospheric boiling point is **100°C**.

Using the Clausius-Clapeyron approximation for water-glycol mixtures:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{boil}} \approx P_{\text{gauge}} \times 15 \text{ °C/bar}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{boil}} = 1.2 \times 15
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{boil}} = 18 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{boil, sealed}} = 100 + 18 = 118 \text{°C}}
$$

**Verification:** This is why pressure caps are mandatory. A faulty cap that releases at 0.5 bar reduces boiling point to ~107.5°C — very close to the overheating threshold of 105°C, causing boil-over exactly when overheating begins. This is a **vicious cycle trap** students must recognize.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Systematic Overheating Diagnostic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Overheating Reported] --> B[Verify Temperature Gauge Reading]
    B --> C{Coolant Level OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Pressure Test System for Leaks]
    D --> D1{Hose or Radiator Leak?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Repair Hose or Radiator Core]
    D1 -- No --> D3[Check Internal Leak into Oil or Combustion]
    D3 --> D4[Head Gasket Failure Suspected]
    D4 --> D5[Replace Gasket, Skive Head, Reassemble]
    C -- Yes --> E[Run Engine to Operating Temp]
    E --> F{Upper Hose Hot?}
    F -- No --> G[Thermostat Stuck Open or Closed]
    G --> G1[Replace Thermostat and Gasket]
    F -- Yes --> H[Feel Air Through Radiator]
    H --> I{Fan Spinning at Hot Idle?}
    I -- No --> J[Check Fan Motor or Clutch]
    J --> J1[Replace Fan Motor or Viscous Coupling]
    I -- Yes --> K[Measure Inlet vs Outlet Temp]
    K --> L{Temp Differential > 15C?}
```
