```mermaid
    L -- No --> M[Radiator Core Clogged]
    M --> M1[Reverse Flush or Replace Radiator]
    L -- Yes --> N[Pressure Test Radiator Cap]
    N --> O{Cap Holds Rated Pressure?}
    O -- No --> O1[Replace Pressure Cap]
    O -- Yes --> P[Check Water Pump Impeller]
    P --> Q{Pump Shaft Play or Noise?}
    Q -- Yes --> Q1[Replace Water Pump Assembly]
    Q -- No --> R[Scan ECU for CTS or Fan Relay Codes]
    R --> S[Perform Combustion and Timing Analysis]
    S --> T[Repair Identified Fault and Re-Test]
    T --> U[Overheating Resolved - Final Road Test]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Cooling System Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Block and Cylinder Head Heat Source] --> WP[Water Pump Centrifugal]
    WP --> TH[Thermostat Wax Element]
    TH -- Cold Closed --> BY[Bypass Loop Returns to Pump]
    TH -- Hot Open --> RAD[Radiator Core Tube and Fin Matrix]
    RAD --> FAN[Cooling Fan Airflow Inducer]
    FAN -- Hot Air Out --> AMB[Ambient Environment Heat Sink]
    RAD --> ET[Expansion Tank Degassing Bottle]
    ET --> RAD
    ENG --> CS[Combustion Space]
    CS -. Coolant Leak .-> HG[Head Gasket Seal]
    HG --> ENG
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] --> CTS[Coolant Temperature Sensor NTC]
    CTS --> ECU
    ECU --> FAN
    ECU -. Relay Logic .-> FAN
    CAP[Pressure Cap 0.9 to 1.4 Bar Rated] --> ET
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Standard Servicing and Overhauling Sequence (SRO Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Inspect Coolant Colour and pH] --> S2[Step 2 Drain Old Coolant into Container]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Pressure Test for Leaks at 1.5 Bar]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Remove and Bench Test Thermostat]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Inspect Water Pump Shaft Play and Seal]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Reverse Flush Radiator and Engine Block]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Clean Radiator Fins with Compressed Air]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Inspect All Hoses Clamps and Replace if Cracked]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Test Pressure Cap on Cap Tester]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Refill with New Coolant and Bleed Air]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Run Engine Warm and Recheck Levels]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Road Test and Confirm Stable Operating Temp]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description — Coolant Circulation at Different Engine States

**Cold Engine State:**
- Thermostat **CLOSED** → coolant circulates only in engine block via bypass loop.
- Radiator hoses stay cool to touch.
- Heat builds up rapidly inside the block (fast warm-up).

**Normal Operating State:**
- Thermostat **OPEN** (~85°C) → coolant flows through radiator.
- Fan activates (electric at ~95°C, mechanical via clutch at all times).
- Steady-state temperature stabilizes.

**Overheating / Failure State:**
- Thermostat **STUCK CLOSED** → coolant never reaches radiator → catastrophic heat soak.
- **OR** Pump impeller sheared → no circulation even with open thermostat.
- **OR** Cap pressure relief → coolant boils at low temp → vapour lock.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. List any three symptoms of engine overheating and explain what each symptom indicates about the cooling system fault.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Coolant boiling in the expansion tank with bubbling** — indicates either a faulty pressure cap (cannot hold rated pressure), low coolant level causing localized boiling, or combustion gases leaking into the coolant through a blown head gasket. (1 mark)

2. **Steam or sweet-smelling vapour from the bonnet area** — indicates a coolant leak at a hose joint, radiator seam, or water pump seal, combined with elevated operating temperature. The sweet smell is characteristic of ethylene glycol vaporization. (1 mark)

3. **Temperature gauge needle in the red zone with simultaneous heater blowing cold air** — indicates very low coolant circulation (failed water pump, fully clogged radiator core, or stuck-closed thermostat). With no hot coolant reaching the heater matrix, cabin heating also fails — a unique dual-symptom signature. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. What is the function of a thermostat in an engine cooling system? What happens if it gets stuck in the closed position?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function (2 marks):**
The thermostat is a temperature-controlled valve, typically a wax-pellet type, located at the engine coolant outlet. Its purpose is to:
- Restrict coolant flow to the radiator when the engine is cold, allowing rapid warm-up to operating temperature (improving fuel efficiency, reducing emissions, and protecting bearings during cold start).
- Fully open at approximately **82°C–88°C** to permit full radiator circulation and maintain steady-state operating temperature within the **85°C–95°C** band.

**If stuck closed (1 mark):**
The coolant cannot reach the radiator at all. Heat is continuously generated but never rejected. Within 5–15 minutes, the engine enters the **overheating red-line zone (>105°C)**, resulting in oil coking, detonation, head gasket failure, and possible piston seizure. This is a critical safety fault requiring immediate shutdown.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Equivalent]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **OR**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**

---

#### **QUESTION A — Overheating Diagnosis and Coolant Flow Analysis**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat flowchart the systematic procedure for diagnosing an overheating complaint in a water-cooled petrol engine. List the tools and instruments used at each stage.

**Model Answer:**

**Systematic Diagnostic Procedure (5 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Customer Information Gathering**
- Symptom: When does it overheat? (idle, highway, AC on)
- Recent service history
- Coolant last replaced?

**Stage 2 — Visual & Static Inspection**
- Coolant level & colour in expansion tank
- Hoses: bulges, cracks, soft spots, clamp tightness
- Radiator fins: bent fins, debris blockage
- Leaks at water pump weep hole
- Oil cap: white mayonnaise = head gasket leak

**Stage 3 — Pressure Test (Cooling System Tester)**
- Apply **1.5 bar** pressure with hand pump.
- Hold for 10 minutes — pressure drop indicates external leak.
- Listen for hissing at gasket surfaces.

**Stage 4 — Thermostat Bench Test**
- Suspend thermostat in water, heat on stove, observe opening temperature with thermometer.
- Replace if opens at wrong temp or fails to open fully.

**Stage 5 — Radiator Flow Check**
- With engine at operating temp, feel both hoses.
- Hot in, cooler out = OK.
- Both hot or both cold = flow restriction.

**Stage 6 — Fan Operation Verification**
- Electric fan: should kick in at ~95°C.
- Mechanical fan: should spin smoothly with viscous drag at hot idle.

**Stage 7 — Combustion Leak Test**
- Use exhaust gas analyzer fluid over header tank with engine cranking.
- Fluid colour change = combustion leak (head gasket).

**Tools Used (2 marks):**
- Cooling system pressure tester
- Infrared thermometer
- Refractometer (coolant concentration)
- Multimeter (CTS resistance)
- OBD-II scanner (ECU codes)
- Combustion leak tester fluid

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-cylinder SI engine develops **60 kW** at 3000 RPM. The fuel consumption is **0.28 kg/kWh**, calorific value **44 MJ/kg**, and **32%** of the fuel heat must be removed by the cooling system. The coolant enters at **70°C** and exits at **90°C**. Calculate: (i) Total heat to be rejected per second, (ii) Coolant mass flow rate (take $C_p = 3.81$ kJ/kg·K).

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $P_b = 60$ kW, $\text{bsfc} = 0.28$ kg/kWh, $\text{NCV} = 44$ MJ/kg, $f_{\text{cool}} = 0.32$, $T_{\text{in}} = 70°C$, $T_{\text{out}} = 90°C$, $C_p = 3.81$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Fuel mass flow rate:**
$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{60 \times 0.28}{3600} = \frac{16.8}{3600} = 0.004667 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total heat input rate:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.004667 \times 44000 = 205.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected by cooling:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{rej}} = 0.32 \times 205.33 = \boxed{65.71 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 4 — Coolant mass flow rate:**
$$
\Delta T = 90 - 70 = 20 \text{ K}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{65.71}{3.81 \times 20} = \frac{65.71}{76.2} = \boxed{0.8623 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — Cooling System Components Overhauling**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the overhauling procedure of an automotive radiator. Mention the inspection points, cleaning methods, and testing standards.

**Model Answer:**

**Overhauling Procedure (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Removal**
- Drain coolant, disconnect upper/lower hoses, remove mounting bolts.
- Detach electric fan shroud and coolant hoses from expansion tank.

**Step 2 — External Cleaning**
- Wash exterior with low-pressure water.
- Use soft brush (never wire brush) and approved radiator cleaner.

**Step 3 — Internal Reverse Flushing**
- Insert garden hose into lower outlet.
- Force water backwards through core to dislodge scale.
- For severe scaling, use chemical descaler (oxalic acid based, 5% concentration) for 30 minutes, then flush.

**Step 4 — Tube Inspection**
- Check for green/white corrosion marks, pitting.
- Inspect soldered joints for hairline cracks.

**Step 5 — Tank and Cap Inspection**
- Inspect plastic tanks for cracks.
- Test pressure cap on calibrated cap tester — must hold rated pressure ± 0.05 bar.

**Step 6 — Fan Shroud and Mounting**
- Inspect fan motor resistance (typical 0.5–5 Ω).
- Replace worn grommets.

**Testing Standards (2 marks):**
- Pressure test radiator at **1.5 bar** for 2 minutes — no drop allowed.
- Flow test: water should flow out of all tubes uniformly.
- Air flow test using shop fan to verify cooling fan delivers rated CFM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the common causes of overheating in modern fuel-injected engines. How is the ECU involved in cooling management?

**Model Answer:**

**Common Causes (4 marks):**
1. **Stuck thermostat** — most frequent failure; ECU cannot detect this directly.
2. **Failed electric cooling fan** — ECU commands fan ON at 95°C, but a burnt relay/motor prevents it.
3. **Faulty Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** — ECU may read incorrect low temp, delay fan activation, or command lean mixture that burns hotter.
4. **Water pump impeller erosion** — common in vehicles with poor coolant maintenance; plastic impellers shear.
5. **Air in cooling system** — improper bleeding after repair creates vapour pockets that block circulation.
6. **Restricted radiator** — internal scaling from degraded coolant.

**ECU's Role in Cooling Management (3 marks):**
- The ECU receives continuous input from the **CTS (NTC thermistor)** and the **ECT sensor**.
- At predefined thresholds (typically **95°C, 100°C, 105°C**), the ECU triggers fan relay activation.
- At >105°C, the ECU enters **limp-home mode**: reduces fuel injection quantity, retards ignition timing, and may disable AC compressor clutch to lower parasitic load.
- Some ECUs also adjust the variable-displacement water pump speed and electronic thermostat position.
- OBD-II codes P0217, P0218, P0219 are stored for cooling system faults, retrievable via scanner.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap (Loses 1–2 marks):** Many students use °C in formula (4) and (5) instead of Kelvin. The ΔT differences cancel out, but absolute temperatures used in efficiency expressions (Carnot-based reasoning) will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the 30% Rule (Loses 1 mark):** Examiners specifically ask "fraction of heat rejected via cooling" — students often wrongly apply 100% or 70%. Always re-read whether the problem gives brake power (mechanical output) or fuel energy input.
> 3. **Confusing Symptom with Cause (Loses 2 marks):** Writing "overheating = bad thermostat" as the final answer without listing differential diagnosis is treated as incomplete by KTU evaluators. Always give a diagnostic flowchart or matrix.
> 4. **Skip the Verification Step (Loses 1 mark):** KTU 2024 valuation key rewards numerical sanity checks (e.g., comparing your 59 L/min coolant flow against the OEM 50–80 L/min range). Omitting this loses the "engineering judgment" mark.
> 5. **Diagram Without Labels (Loses 1–2 marks):** A flowchart without arrows labeled with parameter names (temperature thresholds, pressures) earns only partial credit. Label every node.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Overheating Threshold:** Petrol > **105°C**, Diesel > **100°C** — these are the red-line values.
- **Three-Layer Diagnosis:** Always check **(1) Fluid** (level, condition, leaks), **(2) Flow** (pump, thermostat, blockage), **(3) Air-side** (fan, radiator, cap).
- **Pressure Cap Math:** A **1.0 bar** cap raises boiling point by ~**15°C** — non-negotiable for sealed cooling systems.
- **Head Gasket Signature:** Milky residue on oil cap + bubbles in expansion tank + sweet exhaust smell = definitive diagnosis.
- **Coolant Capacity:** Typical passenger car holds **5 to 8 litres** of coolant (50:50 ethylene glycol + distilled water).
- **First Service Rule:** Drain and refill coolant every **2 years or 40,000 km**, whichever comes first.
- **Fan Clutch Test (Mechanical Fan):** At hot idle, fan should slip slightly with effort; if it spins freely like a blade, the silicone fluid has leaked out — replace the clutch.
- **Bleeding the System:** After any cooling service, air pockets must be removed by running with the heater on full and the radiator cap off until no bubbles emerge.
- **CTS Resistance Rule:** NTC sensor resistance decreases as temperature rises — at 20°C it should read ~**2.5 kΩ**, at 80°C ~**300 Ω** (verify against OEM spec).
- **Formula Memory Anchor:** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ is the **master equation** — every cooling system problem reduces to identifying the correct $\dot{m}$ and $\Delta T$.
- **ECM Safety:** Modern ECUs will reduce engine power above 110°C to protect components — recognize this as a *protective symptom*, not a fault.
- **Avoid Tap Water:** Mineral content causes scale buildup; always use **distilled or deionized water** when mixing coolant.
- **Thermostat Opening Temp:** Always replace with the **same rated temperature** as OEM — fitting a lower-rated thermostat causes the ECU to remain in cold-start enrichment mode.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Checking, Regular Maintenance and Repairing of Cooling System Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Checking, Regular Maintenance and Repairing of Cooling System Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Cooling System** of an internal combustion engine is a closed-loop thermal management assembly designed to **dissipate excess heat** generated during combustion, maintain optimal engine operating temperature (typically **85 °C to 95 °C**), and prevent localized overheating, cavitation, and corrosion of wetted metallic components. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Preliminary Checking* refers to the systematic visual, aural, tactile, and instrument-based inspection carried out before starting scheduled maintenance. *Regular Maintenance* denotes the periodic service actions mandated by the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM), while *Repairing* covers the corrective restoration of worn, scaled, or failed cooling system components back to functional specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A liquid-cooled engine rejects roughly **30 % to 35 %** of the fuel's calorific energy through the coolant. If this heat is not managed, cylinder head temperatures may exceed **200 °C**, leading to detonation, warping, lubrication failure, and seizure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your engine as a **marathon runner** running in the middle of summer. Just as the runner sweats to release body heat, the engine uses a special "blood" (coolant + water mixture) that circulates through the body (engine block), carries away the heat, and dumps it at a "skin cooling station" (the radiator). The **water pump** is the heart pumping this blood; the **thermostat** is a thermostat-like valve that decides whether to let the blood pass through the cooling station or short-circuit back to the body; and the **radiator cap** is like a pressure-release valve on a pressure cooker — by raising pressure, it raises the boiling point, preventing the "blood" from boiling away.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Coolant Boiling Point (Pure Water at 1 atm):** **100 °C**
- **Coolant Boiling Point (50:50 Ethylene Glycol at 1.05 bar cap):** **~125 °C**
- **Typical Radiator Cap Pressure Rating:** **0.7 bar to 1.05 bar** (10 psi to 15 psi)
- **Engine Operating Thermostat Opening Temperature:** **80 °C to 90 °C**
- **Freezing Point of 50:50 EG-Water:** **−36 °C**
- **Specific Heat Capacity of Coolant (50:50):** **≈ 3.5 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific Heat Capacity of Pure Water:** **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- **Density of Coolant (50:50):** **≈ 1.07 g/cm³**

> [!NOTE]
> The cooling system is a **pressurized** liquid system, not an open one. Pressurization is the single most important reason why a passenger car engine can run hotter than water's atmospheric boiling point without the coolant boiling off.

### 1.4 Major Components of the Cooling System (KTU Module 3 Focus)

| S.No | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Radiator** | Heat exchanger that rejects coolant heat to atmosphere |
| 2 | **Water Pump** | Circulates coolant through engine and radiator |
| 3 | **Thermostat** | Temperature-responsive valve regulating coolant flow |
| 4 | **Cooling Fan** | Forces air across radiator core |
| 5 | **Radiator Cap (Pressure Cap)** | Maintains system pressure, controls boiling point |
| 6 | **Hoses (Upper & Lower)** | Flexible coolant conduits |
| 7 | **Expansion/Overflow Tank** | Accommodates coolant expansion and recovers overflow |
| 8 | **Heater Core** | Uses engine heat for cabin warming |
| 9 | **Temperature Gauge / Sender Unit** | Indicates coolant temperature to driver |
| 10 | **Coolant Temperature Switch (CTS)** | Sends signal to ECU/fan relay |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Pressurized Cooling System — Step-by-Step

1. **Combustion Event:** Fuel air mixture burns, producing peak cylinder gas temperatures in the range of **2000 °C to 2500 °C**.
2. **Heat Transfer to Coolant:** Approximately one-third of this heat is conducted through the cylinder walls into the water jackets surrounding the combustion chamber.
3. **Coolant Circulation:** The **centrifugal water pump**, belt-driven off the crankshaft, draws coolant from the radiator bottom tank and forces it through the engine block and cylinder head.
4. **Thermostat Regulation:** When cold, the **wax-pellet thermostat** remains closed, blocking flow to the radiator and forcing the coolant to recirculate internally (small bypass loop) so the engine warms up rapidly.
5. **Opening Point:** At the rated opening temperature (e.g., **82 °C**), the wax pellet expands, opening the main valve and allowing hot coolant into the radiator.
6. **Radiator Heat Rejection:** Air (forced by the fan or ram effect) passes through the radiator tubes; heat is transferred from coolant to air.
7. **Return Cycle:** Cooled, denser coolant sinks and returns via the lower hose to the water pump inlet.
8. **Pressure Regulation:** The radiator cap's pressure valve lifts at the calibrated pressure, venting excess coolant into the expansion tank. On cooling, a vacuum valve admits coolant back from the tank, preventing air ingress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bypass loop** is critical during warm-up. Without it, the cold coolant touching the hot cylinder head would create severe thermal shock and the engine would never reach operating temperature.

### 2.2 Why Pressurization Works — The Clausius-Clapeyron Principle

The saturation (boiling) temperature of a liquid rises with pressure. For water, an empirical approximation valid in the automotive pressure range is:

$$T_{b} \approx 100\,°C + \frac{\Delta P \times 2.7}{100}$$

where $\Delta P$ is the gauge pressure in kPa above atmospheric. Therefore, raising system pressure by **70 kPa (≈ 0.7 bar)** increases the boiling point by roughly **1.9 °C**; raising by **105 kPa (≈ 1.05 bar)** raises it by about **2.8 °C**. Combined with ethylene-glycol addition (which itself elevates boiling point), a 50:50 EG-water mixture under a 1.05 bar cap boils near **125 °C**, comfortably above normal operating temperatures.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|--------------------|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Heat Rejection Rate | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/s); $C_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K); $\Delta T$ = temperature drop across radiator (K) | Sizing radiator & verifying pump capacity |
| Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K); $A$ = surface area (m²) | Radiator effectiveness analysis |
| Boiling Point Elevation (pressure) | $T_{b} \approx 100 + 0.027 \cdot \Delta P$ | $\Delta P$ in kPa | Cap-pressure rating selection |
| Freezing Point Depression | $T_{f} \approx -k_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | $k_f$ = cryoscopic constant; $m$ = molality; $i$ = van't Hoff factor | Coolant concentration selection |
| Coolant Concentration | $\phi = \frac{V_{EG}}{V_{EG} + V_{W}} \times 100\,\%$ | $V_{EG}$ = EG volume; $V_{W}$ = water volume | Freeze/boil protection ratio |
| Pump Power | $P = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta P / \rho$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow; $\Delta P$ = pressure rise; $\rho$ = density | Power consumed by water pump |
| Heat Engine Efficiency (Carnot Reference) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$ | $T_c$ = coolant (sink) temp; $T_h$ = combustion (source) temp | Theoretical efficiency ceiling |

> [!WARNING]
> Do not use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown table cells. The cryoscopic constant $k_f$ for water is **1.86 K·kg/mol** — memorize it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cooling system diagnostics underpin warranty disputes, BS-VI emission compliance (overheating engines run richer and emit more CO/HC), and predictive maintenance algorithms in fleet management. Modern OBD-II systems continuously monitor coolant temperature sensor (CTS) plausibility; a stuck-closed thermostat will throw **P0128** (Coolant Thermostat), while a stuck-open one throws **P0125**. Mastery of the underlying thermal physics is therefore directly testable in KTU vivas and ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Heat to be Rejected by an Engine Cooling System

**Given Data (typical 1.5 L petrol engine):**
- Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f = 0.008 \, \text{kg/s}$ (≈ 4 L/100 km cruise)
- Lower Heating Value (LHV) of petrol: $LHV = 44 \, \text{MJ/kg}$
- Fraction of heat to coolant: $f_c = 0.30$ (i.e., 30 % of fuel energy)

**Step 1 — Energy input rate from fuel**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 0.008 \, \text{kg/s} \times 44\,000\,000 \, \text{J/kg}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 352\,000 \, \text{W} = 352 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Heat that must be rejected by coolant**

$$\dot{Q}_{coolant} = f_c \times \dot{Q}_{in}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{coolant} = 0.30 \times 352\,000 \, \text{W}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{coolant} = 105\,600 \, \text{W} \approx 105.6 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute required coolant mass flow rate**

Assume radiator cools coolant from **95 °C** at inlet to **85 °C** at outlet, so $\Delta T = 10 \, \text{K}$, with $C_p = 3.5 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$ for 50:50 coolant.

$$\dot{m}_{coolant} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{coolant}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

$$\dot{m}_{coolant} = \frac{105\,600}{3500 \times 10} = \frac{105\,600}{35\,000}$$

$$\dot{m}_{coolant} \approx 3.02 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow (for pump selection)**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{3.02}{1070} \approx 2.82 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{V} \approx 2.82 \, \text{L/s} \approx 170 \, \text{L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This figure (≈ 170 L/min) is realistic for a modern passenger car. A failing water pump impeller may deliver only 60–80 % of this, causing gradual overheating.

### 3.2 Derivation — Coolant Boiling Point at Elevated Pressure

We use the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron form for liquid-vapour equilibrium (water):

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

For water: $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.66 \, \text{kJ/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$, $P_1 = 101.3 \, \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 373.15 \, \text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Take $P_2 = 101.3 + 70 = 171.3 \, \text{kPa}$ (0.7 bar cap)**

$$\ln\left(\frac{171.3}{101.3}\right) = 0.525$$

$$0.525 = -\frac{40\,660}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right)$$

$$0.525 = -4888.86 \times \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.680 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.680 \times 10^{-3} = -\frac{0.525}{4888.86} = -1.074 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} - 1.074 \times 10^{-4} = 2.573 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2.573 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 388.7 \, \text{K} = 115.5 \, \text{°C}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**

$$\boxed{T_{b,\,0.7\,bar} \approx 115.5\,°C}$$

The empirical shortcut gave 101.9 °C; the rigorous Clausius-Clapeyron gives 115.5 °C. The empirical shortcut is biased low because it ignores the latent heat non-linearity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Faults vs. Diagnostic Cues

| Symptom | Probable Component | Diagnostic Cue | Rectification |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Engine overheats in traffic, normal on highway | **Stuck-closed thermostat** | Upper radiator hose cold; coolant not flowing | Replace thermostat |
| Engine takes very long to warm up; heater blows cold | **Stuck-open thermostat** | Lower hose always warm; fuel economy drops | Replace thermostat |
| Coolant loss with no visible leak | **Head gasket failure** | White exhaust smoke, bubbles in expansion tank, oily coolant | Pressure-test, replace gasket |
| Overheating at idle only | **Failed fan motor / relay / CTS** | Fan not spinning; cold air at speed | Test fan circuit, replace relay/CTS/motor |
| Coolant level drops, sweet smell, green puddle | **Hose leak** | Soft/swollen hose, clamp corrosion | Replace hose and clamps |
| Discoloured coolant (rust brown) | **Rust / corrosion / failed inhibitor** | Electrolysis, neglected coolant change | Flush, refill with fresh coolant |
| Coolant boils in expansion tank on hot day | **Cap pressure valve failed** | Cap fails pressure test | Replace cap |
| Whining noise, coolant leak at front of engine | **Water pump bearing failure** | Shaft play, seepage at weep hole | Replace water pump |
| Milky oil on dipstick | **Coolant-in-oil (head gasket)** | Combustion gas test positive | Engine top overhaul |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pressurized Liquid Cooling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Water Jackets\nHeat Source Zone] --> B[Thermostat Housing\nWax Pellet Valve]
    B -->|Below Opening Temp| C[Internal Bypass Loop]
    C --> A
    B -->|Above Opening Temp| D[Upper Radiator Hose]
    D --> E[Radiator Top Tank]
    E --> F[Radiator Core\nTube and Fin Matrix]
    F --> G[Fan Forced Airflow\nOr Ram Air]
    G --> H[Radiator Bottom Tank]
    H --> I[Water Pump\nCentrifugal Impeller]
    I --> A
    J[Radiator Pressure Cap\nPressure and Vacuum Valve] -->|Vents at Rated Pressure| K[Expansion Overflow Tank]
    K -->|Vacuum Refill on Cooling| J
    L[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> M[ECU and Gauge Cluster]
    N[Heater Core Tap Off] --> O[Cabin HVAC Heater]
```

### 4.2 Preliminary Check Inspection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Visual Inspection of Coolant Level] --> P2[Check Hoses for Cracks and Swelling]
    P2 --> P3[Inspect Belts for Tension and Cracking]
    P3 --> P4[Test Radiator Cap Pressure Rating]
    P4 --> P5[Check Fan Operation on Cold and Hot]
    P5 --> P6[Listen for Water Pump Whine or Bearing Noise]
    P6 --> P7[Smell for Sweet Coolant Odour Around Engine Bay]
    P7 --> P8[Start Engine and Observe Temperature Gauge]
    P8 --> P9{Operates Within 85 to 95 C?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[System Healthy Document and Release]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Proceed to Symptom Based Diagnostic Matrix]
```

### 4.3 Scheduled Maintenance Interval Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Daily Pre Drive Check] --> M2[Weekly Coolant Level Visual]
    M2 --> M3[Every 6 Months or 10 000 km Coolant Concentration Test]
    M3 --> M4[Annual Radiator Cap Pressure Test and Hose Inspection]
    M4 --> M5[Every 2 Years or 40 000 km Coolant Drain Flush Refill]
    M5 --> M6[Every 4 Years Thermostat and Water Pump Inspection]
    M6 --> M7[Every 5 to 6 Years Replace Radiator Hoses and Clamps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Block-level mermaid diagrams are preferred over physical drawings because mermaid cannot accurately render 3D mechanical components. The functional blocks above map all the necessary interactions and flows for KTU exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Why is the cooling system of a passenger car engine kept pressurized?*

**Model Answer:**
The cooling system is pressurized (typically **0.7 to 1.05 bar** above atmospheric) by the radiator cap to **raise the boiling point** of the coolant above the engine's normal operating temperature (≈ 95 °C). According to the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, increasing saturation pressure raises the boiling temperature, preventing localized boiling in the cylinder head water jackets. The pressurized cap also incorporates a vacuum valve that admits coolant back from the expansion tank during cool-down, preventing air ingress that would otherwise cause cavitation and corrosion.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any three preliminary checks performed on an engine cooling system before scheduling maintenance.*

**Model Answer:**
Three preliminary checks are:
1. **Visual coolant level inspection** in the expansion tank (cold mark verification).
2. **Radiator cap pressure test** using a cap tester to confirm the pressure-relief and vacuum valves operate at the calibrated rating.
3. **Hose and clamp inspection** for swelling, cracking, leaks, and proper tightness at all connection points.
*(Additional acceptable checks: belt tension, fan operation, water-pump bearing play, coolant smell and color.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the aid of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a pressurized liquid cooling system of a multi-cylinder petrol engine. Discuss the role of the thermostat and the bypass loop during engine warm-up.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 1.6 L petrol engine consumes fuel at 0.010 kg/s. The fuel LHV is 44 MJ/kg. The coolant receives 32 % of the total fuel energy. The 50:50 EG-water mixture ($C_p$ = 3.5 kJ/kg·K) enters the radiator at 95 °C and leaves at 80 °C. Compute the required coolant mass flow rate and the volumetric flow in L/min. Take $\rho$ = 1070 kg/m³.*

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

1. **Functional Block Diagram:** Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1**. Mark all major components — water pump, thermostat housing, bypass loop, upper hose, radiator core, fan, lower hose, pressure cap, expansion tank, CTS, and engine jackets.
2. **Cold State:** Thermostat wax pellet is solid; main valve is closed. Coolant circulates only in the internal bypass loop (engine jackets → bypass → water pump → engine jackets). This allows rapid warm-up.
3. **Opening Transition:** At ~82–90 °C, the wax pellet melts and expands, opening the main thermostat valve progressively. Coolant now begins flowing into the radiator for heat rejection.
4. **Hot State Operation:** Coolant flows engine → upper hose → radiator top tank → radiator core → bottom tank → water pump → engine. Fan engages (electric or viscous) at ~95–105 °C to augment ram air.
5. **Pressure Regulation:** Radiator cap rated at 0.9 bar lifts at threshold pressure to vent to expansion tank; vacuum valve admits coolant back on cool-down.
6. **Bypass Loop Necessity:** Prevents cold-coolant thermal shock on hot cylinder head; ensures uniform warm-up; protects gasket joints from differential expansion.

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV = 0.010 \times 44\,000\,000 = 440\,000 \, \text{W}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{coolant} = 0.32 \times 440\,000 = 140\,800 \, \text{W}$$

$$\Delta T = 95 - 80 = 15 \, \text{K}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{140\,800}{3500 \times 15} = \frac{140\,800}{52\,500} \approx 2.68 \, \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{2.68}{1070} \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \times 60\,000 \, \text{L/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V} \approx 150.4 \, \text{L/min}}$$

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the procedure for regular maintenance of a liquid cooling system. State the recommended intervals for: coolant replacement, hose replacement, thermostat inspection, and radiator cap pressure test.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss common cooling system faults, their symptoms, and the step-by-step diagnostic procedure to isolate each fault. Include at least five distinct faults in your answer.*

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Regular Maintenance Procedure (recommended OEM schedule):**
1. **Coolant Level Check (Weekly):** Inspect expansion tank with engine cold. Top up with premixed 50:50 EG-water to MAX line.
2. **Concentration Test (Every 6 Months or 10 000 km):** Use a hydrometer or refractometer to verify EG concentration ≈ 50 %; refractometer preferred as it is unaffected by additive package.
3. **Radiator Cap Pressure Test (Annually):** Using a hand pump cap tester, verify the pressure-relief valve opens at the rated value (e.g., 1.05 bar ± 0.05 bar) and the vacuum valve holds.
4. **Hose and Clamp Inspection (Annually):** Squeeze hoses; replace if soft, swollen, cracked, or oil-soaked. Torque clamps to OEM specification.
5. **Coolant Drain, Flush, and Refill (Every 2 Years or 40 000 km):** Open radiator drain cock, flush with distilled water until clear, refill with new coolant, bleed air pockets via bleeder screws.
6. **Thermostat Inspection (Every 4 Years):** Remove and test in water bath; full opening within 3 °C of rating; full lift ≥ 8 mm.
7. **Water Pump and Belt Service (Every 4 Years or 80 000 km):** Inspect weep hole for seepage; check bearing play; replace timing belt and water pump together if belt-driven.
8. **Hose Replacement (Every 5–6 Years):** Replace all upper, lower, bypass, and heater hoses regardless of appearance.

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

| Fault | Symptom | Diagnostic Step-by-Step |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Stuck-closed thermostat** | Engine overheats quickly in all conditions; heater blows hot | 1. Feel upper radiator hose with engine at operating temp 2. If hose is cold, thermostat is stuck closed 3. Remove and bench-test in water bath |
| **Failed water pump** | Whining noise, coolant leak at pump, slow warm-up or overheating | 1. Inspect weep hole 2. Check shaft end-play > 1 mm means replacement 3. Check impeller fins for erosion 4. Replace pump |
| **Blown head gasket** | White exhaust smoke, bubbles in expansion tank, milky oil | 1. Combustion leak test (block tester) — blue fluid turns yellow 2. Compression test — low cylinder(s) 3. Pressure-test cooling system — gauge drops |
| **Failed radiator cap** | Coolant loss, slow leaks, boiling in expansion tank | 1. Bench-test cap with hand pump tester 2. Replace if relief valve opens below or above rating |
| **Air in system** | Slow warm-up, fluctuating gauge, heater intermittent | 1. Check for low coolant 2. Bleed via bleeder screws 3. Inspect cap vacuum valve |
| **Clogged radiator** | Overheats at idle/low speed, OK at highway | 1. Infrared thermometer scan inlet vs outlet tubes 2. > 8 °C drop at idle confirms flow restriction 3. Reverse-flush or replace radiator |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Dec 2023 / May 2024 pattern):**
> 1. **No mention of bypass loop** while explaining thermostat operation → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing pressure cap and expansion tank function** — cap pressurizes; tank accommodates expansion. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping units in numericals** — every $Q$, $\dot{m}$, and $\dot{V}$ must carry units; unmarked numbers lose credit.
> 4. **Recommending "100 % antifreeze"** — pure EG freezes at −13 °C; 50:50 is the optimal concentration. Examiners flag this as a conceptual error.
> 5. **Ignoring the pressure cap's vacuum valve** during a question on cooling system pressure regulation → loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cooling system** is a **pressurized closed loop** designed to maintain engine block temperature in the **85–95 °C** band.
- **Pressure cap rating (0.7 to 1.05 bar)** elevates coolant boiling point by ~15–28 °C above 100 °C.
- **Thermostat** has two functions: rapid warm-up (bypass) and operating-temperature regulation (main valve).
- **Water pump** is centrifugal, belt-driven, and should be replaced along with the timing belt on interference engines.
- **Coolant mix** is **50:50 EG-water** for balanced freeze (−36 °C) and boil (+125 °C) protection; pure EG freezes at only −13 °C.
- **Heat rejection** is governed by $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ — a *failing water pump* reduces $\dot{m}$, directly reducing $Q$.
- **Diagnostic cues:** sweet smell = leak; white smoke = head gasket; rusty coolant = neglected inhibitor; cold upper hose + overheating = stuck thermostat.
- **Maintenance schedule:** weekly level check → 6-month concentration test → annual cap and hose inspection → 2-year full coolant change → 4-year pump and thermostat → 5–6-year hose replacement.
- **OEM-specific** intervals may vary; always consult the manufacturer's service manual before quoting numbers.
- **OBD-II codes** P0125 (coolant temp low) and P0128 (thermostat) are the most common DTCs to remember.
- **Never open the radiator cap on a hot engine** — the system is pressurized at ~1 bar and can scald. Wait until temperature drops below 50 °C, then wrap with a thick cloth and turn slowly to the first stop before releasing.
- **Use distilled water** for top-up to avoid mineral scale in the radiator core.
- **Refractometer** is preferred over hydrometer for testing coolant concentration because hydrometers are inaccurate when inhibitors are added.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leak Detection and Repair of Cooling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Leak Detection and Repair of Cooling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cooling System Leak Detection** is the systematic diagnostic procedure employed in automotive engineering to identify the source, location, and severity of unintended escape of coolant (typically a water-ethylene glycol mixture) from the pressurized liquid-cooling circuit of an internal combustion engine. The **Cooling System Repair** encompasses the subsequent rectification procedures that restore the system to its original sealed, pressurized, and thermally efficient operating condition.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEMUT635, the cooling system must operate at a **gauge pressure of 0.9 bar to 1.5 bar** (approximately **13 psi to 22 psi**) above atmospheric pressure to elevate the coolant boiling point and prevent cavitation in the water pump.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Leak detection is a direct application of the *Engine Auxiliary Systems* module, connecting mechanical sealing, fluid dynamics, and thermal management. The student must be capable of identifying both **external leaks** (visible) and **internal leaks** (combustion-gas ingestion, oil contamination).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Circulatory System

Think of a vehicle's cooling system as the **human circulatory system**. The radiator is the *skin* (releases heat through convection), the water pump is the *heart* (pumps the fluid), the hoses are the *veins and arteries* (transport the fluid), and the engine block is the *body's muscles* (generates heat during work). A leak in this "circulatory system" is like a wound — it must be located (diagnosed), assessed (severity), and sealed (repaired). If the coolant "blood" drops below a critical level, the engine "organ" suffers heat-stroke, leading to warping of the cylinder head, blown head gaskets, or even seizure of the pistons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Consequence:** A 10% coolant loss typically raises the engine's peak operating temperature by **15 °C to 25 °C**, dramatically accelerating wear on piston rings, valve seats, and cylinder liners. Studies by SAE indicate that **40% of all cooling system failures** originate from preventable hose and clamp degradation.

### 1.3 Classification of Cooling System Leaks

| Leak Type | Location | Visibility | Severity |
|-----------|----------|------------|----------|
| **External Leak** | Hoses, radiator, water pump, hoses, overflow tank | Visible (coolant pooling) | Low to High |
| **Internal Leak** | Head gasket, cylinder head cracks, engine block | Invisible (symptomatic) | Critical |
| **Permeation Leak** | Hose material porosity | Microscopic | Gradual |
| **Seal/Gasket Leak** | Thermostat housing, water pump seals | Visible or weeping | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of a Pressurized Cooling System

The cooling system exploits a fundamental thermodynamic principle: **the boiling point of a liquid rises with increasing pressure** (as governed by the Clausius-Clapeyron relation). A standard 50/50 ethylene glycol-water mixture boils at approximately **106 °C** at atmospheric pressure. When the pressure cap seals the system at **1.0 bar gauge**, the absolute pressure becomes **2.01325 bar**, raising the boiling point to approximately **120 °C to 125 °C**.

#### 2.1.1 The Three Functional Stages of Leak Detection

1. **Symptom Recognition Stage** — Identifying the *warning signs* (overheating gauge, sweet smell, white exhaust smoke, low coolant warning).
2. **Localization Stage** — Using diagnostic tools (pressure tester, UV dye, combustion leak tester) to pinpoint the leak.
3. **Quantification Stage** — Determining the rate of coolant loss to assess urgency.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula/Value | Units | Application |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| Gauge Pressure of Cap | $P_{g}$ | $P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | bar (g) | Pressure cap rating |
| Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs}$ | $P_{atm} + P_{g}$ | bar (a) | Boiling point calc |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **1.01325** | bar | Reference baseline |
| Coolant Freezing Point (50/50) | $T_f$ | **-36 °C** | °C | Antifreeze property |
| Coolant Boiling Point (50/50, 1 atm) | $T_b$ | **106 °C** | °C | Atmospheric condition |
| Boiling Point Elevation (approx.) | $\Delta T_b$ | $\approx 10 \times P_{g}$ | °C per bar | Empirical rule |
| Coolant Concentration Ratio | $C_{EG}$ | $\frac{V_{EG}}{V_{EG} + V_{w}} \times 100$ | % | Mixture verification |
| Heat Rejection Rate (approx.) | $Q_{rej}$ | $\approx 0.30 \times Q_{fuel}$ | kW | Energy balance |
| Coolant System Volume | $V_{sys}$ | **8 to 15** (typical) | Litres | Capacity check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Never operate the cooling system pressurization test above the **rated pressure cap value** (typically stamped on the cap, e.g., **1.0 bar / 14.7 psi**). Exceeding this value can rupture hoses, damage the radiator, or distort the cylinder head.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles taught in this topic are directly applied by **Automotive Service Technicians (AST)**, **Diagnostic Engineers**, and **Quality Assurance Inspectors** in OEM service centres. Mastery of cooling system diagnostics is also a prerequisite for advanced topics such as **hybrid vehicle thermal management**, **battery cooling systems in EVs**, and **heavy-duty commercial vehicle maintenance** — all of which are emerging KTU 2024 NEP-aligned skill areas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boiling Point Elevation under Pressure (Simplified)

We begin with the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** for liquid-vapour phase transition. For a small temperature range, latent heat of vaporization $L$ is assumed constant.

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{L}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = Initial pressure (atmospheric) = **1.01325 bar**
- $P_2$ = Final pressure (system pressure) = **$P_{atm} + P_g$**
- $T_1$ = Boiling point at $P_1$ = **373.15 K** (for pure water)
- $T_2$ = Boiling point at $P_2$ = to be found
- $L$ = Latent heat of vaporization of water = **40,656 J/mol**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

#### Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** Pressure cap rating $P_g$ = 1.0 bar (gauge), so $P_2$ = 2.01325 bar.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{2.01325}{1.01325}\right) &= \frac{40656}{8.314} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
\ln(1.9871) &= 4889.6 \cdot \left(0.0026798 - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
0.6865 &= 4889.6 \cdot \left(0.0026798 - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
\frac{0.6865}{4889.6} &= 0.0026798 - \frac{1}{T_2} \\
1.404 \times 10^{-4} &= 0.0026798 - \frac{1}{T_2} \\
\frac{1}{T_2} &= 0.0026798 - 0.0001404 \\
\frac{1}{T_2} &= 0.0025394 \\
T_2 &= 393.81 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting back to Celsius:

$$
T_2 = 393.81 - 273.15 = 120.66 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The pressurization raises the boiling point of pure water from **100 °C** to **120.66 °C** — a margin of **20.66 °C** of safety against localized boiling (cavitation) near hot spots like the exhaust port and spark plug region.

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Ethylene Glycol Concentration for Sub-Zero Operation

The freezing point depression of ethylene glycol in water follows a non-linear curve. The empirically accepted safe mixture for automotive use is **50% EG / 50% water** by volume, providing protection down to **-36 °C**.

Using the simplified colligative expression:

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_f$ = Freezing point depression (°C)
- $K_f$ = Cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**
- $m$ = Molality of solute (mol/kg)
- $i$ = Van 't Hoff factor (for non-ionic EG, $i = 1$)

**For 50% EG mixture (approx. molality calculation):**

- Density of EG = **1.113 g/cm³**, Molar mass = **62.07 g/mol**
- In 1 L of mixture: 500 mL EG = 556.5 g, moles = 556.5/62.07 = **8.966 mol**
- 500 mL water ≈ 500 g, so molality $m$ = 8.966/0.5 = **17.93 mol/kg**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_f &= 1.86 \times 17.93 \times 1 \\
\Delta T_f &= 33.35 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, freezing point ≈ 0 - 33.35 = **-33.35 °C** (matches the empirical -36 °C closely; deviation due to non-ideal behaviour and crystallization suppression by EG's hydrogen bonding).

### 3.3 Leak Rate Quantification Matrix

| Diagnostic Tool | Leak Rate Detectable | Detection Limit | Time Required |
|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Visual Inspection** | > 100 mL/hour | Large leaks only | 5 min |
| **Pressure Tester** | 10 to 100 mL/hour | 0.1 bar drop / 2 min | 15 min |
| **UV Dye + Blacklight** | 5 to 50 mL/hour | Microscopic drips | 30 min |
| **Combustion Leak Tester** | Combustion gases only | Trace infiltration | 10 min |
| **Smoke Machine** | 1 to 10 mL/hour | Hairline cracks | 20 min |
| **Chemical Test Strips** | Gasoline contamination | Hydrocarbons in coolant | 5 min |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cooling System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RADIATOR] --> B[UPPER RADIATOR HOSE]
    B --> C[THERMOSTAT HOUSING]
    C --> D[CYLINDER HEAD WATER JACKET]
    D --> E[ENGINE BLOCK WATER JACKET]
    E --> F[HEATER CORE SUPPLY HOSE]
    F --> G[HEATER CORE]
    G --> H[HEATER RETURN HOSE]
    H --> I[WATER PUMP INLET]
    I --> J[WATER PUMP]
    J --> K[LOWER RADIATOR HOSE]
    K --> A
    L[EXPANSION TANK] --> A
    A --> L
    M[PRESSURE CAP] --> A
    N[COOLING FAN ASSEMBLY] --> A
    subgraph detection
        P1[VISUAL INSPECTION]
        P2[PRESSURE TEST]
        P3[UV DYE TEST]
        P4[COMBUSTION LEAK TEST]
    end
```

### 4.2 Leak Detection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[COOLANT LEVEL DROP OBSERVED] --> B{IS LEAK VISIBLE?}
    B -- YES --> C[VISUAL INSPECTION OF COMPONENTS]
    B -- NO --> D[PRESSURE TEST COOLING SYSTEM]
    C --> E{LEAK SOURCE IDENTIFIED?}
    D --> F{PRESSURE HOLDS?}
    F -- NO --> C
    F -- YES --> G[PERFORM COMBUSTION LEAK TEST]
    G --> H{BLOCK TESTER FLUID CHANGES COLOUR?}
    H -- YES --> I[INTERNAL LEAK - HEAD GASKET OR CRACK]
    H -- NO --> J[PERFORM UV DYE TEST]
    J --> K[DRIVE VEHICLE 15-30 MIN]
    K --> L[INSPECT WITH UV BLACKLIGHT]
    L --> M[GLOWING TRACES LOCATE LEAK]
    I --> N[DISASSEMBLE AND REPAIR]
    M --> N
    E -- NO --> O[SMOKE TEST FAILED SEAL]
    O --> N
```

### 4.3 Cooling System Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[HEAT FROM ENGINE COMBUSTION] --> CORE[COOLANT ABSORPTION]
    CORE --> CIRC[CIRCULATION VIA WATER PUMP]
    CIRC --> REG[PRESSURE REGULATION BY CAP]
    REG --> RAD[HEAT DISSIPATION AT RADIATOR]
    RAD --> FAN[FORCED AIR BY COOLING FAN]
    FAN --> OUT[HEAT REJECTED TO ATMOSPHERE]
    OUT --> COOL[COOLED COOLANT RETURNS]
    COOL --> CORE
    LEAK[UNINTENDED COOLANT ESCAPE] --> DET[LEAK DETECTION MODULE]
    DET --> DIAG[DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS]
    DIAG --> FIX[REPAIR AND RESEAL]
    FIX --> CORE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the function of the pressure cap in an automobile cooling system. Why is it rated at a specific pressure value?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pressure cap seals the cooling system and maintains a **gauge pressure of 0.9 to 1.5 bar** above atmospheric. It performs three functions: (1) seals the radiator filler neck via a spring-loaded valve, (2) regulates system pressure to elevate the coolant's boiling point (preventing vapour lock), and (3) allows excess coolant to escape into the expansion tank when the system is hot, then draws it back when cool. The specific rating is chosen to balance thermal safety margin against hose/radiator structural limits.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any four methods of detecting leaks in an automotive cooling system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(1) **Visual inspection** for external pooling of coolant on the ground or component surfaces.
(2) **Pressure testing** using a hand pump and gauge to pressurize the cold system.
(3) **UV dye testing** — fluorescent dye added to coolant and traced with a blacklight.
(4) **Combustion leak (block) test** — detects exhaust gases infiltrating the coolant via a chemical colour change.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**
*(a) Explain with a neat sketch the step-by-step procedure for pressure testing a cooling system. (7 marks)*
*(b) Describe any three methods of detecting internal leaks in a cooling system. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Pressure Testing Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Preparation (1 mark):** Ensure the engine is completely cold. Open the bonnet and locate the pressure cap on the radiator or expansion tank. Visually inspect hoses, the radiator, water pump, and heater core for obvious signs of leakage before testing.

**Step 2 — Coolant Top-Up (1 mark):** Top up the coolant to the maximum marked level on the expansion tank. This ensures a valid pressure-hold test. Note the level for later comparison.

**Step 3 — Adapter Connection (1 mark):** Attach the cooling system pressure tester adapter to the radiator filler neck (or expansion tank) in place of the pressure cap. Connect the hand pump and gauge assembly. Ensure all clamps and hose connections are tight.

**Step 4 — Pressurization (1 mark):** Pump the tester handle to raise the system pressure to the **rated cap value** (typically 1.0 bar / 14.7 psi). Do not exceed the cap rating.

**Step 5 — Hold and Observe (2 marks):** Maintain the pressure for a minimum of 2 minutes. Observe the gauge for any pressure drop. A drop greater than **0.1 bar in 2 minutes** indicates a leak. Visually trace the source — radiators weep at seams, hoses bulge at weak points, water pumps leak at the weep hole.

**Step 6 — Repair Recommendation (1 mark):** Mark the leak location, depressurize the system by releasing the tester valve, and proceed to repair. Common fixes include hose clamp tightening, hose replacement, or radiator re-soldering.

**(b) Internal Leak Detection Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Combustion Leak Test (Block Test) — 3 marks:** A block tester is fitted over the radiator filler neck. The engine is cranked (without starting) or idled. Exhaust gases leaking past a damaged head gasket or cracked cylinder head bubble through the tester fluid, changing its colour from **blue to green/yellow**. This confirms combustion gas infiltration.

2. **Coolant Analysis for Hydrocarbons — 2 marks:** A sample of coolant is drawn from the radiator. A chemical test strip or **FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared) spectroscopy** is used to detect combustion byproducts (CO₂, unburnt hydrocarbons). Presence of these indicates a breach between the combustion chamber and water jacket.

3. **Exhaust Smoke and Coolant Behaviour Observation — 2 marks:** White, sweet-smelling exhaust (especially at idle) coupled with a constantly overflowing expansion tank and bubbling in the radiator are classic symptoms of head gasket failure. The technician can also check the oil filler cap for a **milky brown emulsion (mayonnaise-like)**, which is coolant mixing with engine oil.

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024]:**
*(a) Explain the common causes and locations of cooling system leaks in an automobile. (7 marks)*
*(b) Describe the repair procedure for (i) a leaking radiator and (ii) a leaking water pump. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Common Causes and Locations of Cooling System Leaks (7 marks):**

| Location | Cause | Symptom | Frequency |
|----------|-------|---------|-----------|
| **Hoses & Clamps** | Rubber degradation, heat cycling, loose clamps | Visible drip, bulging | **~40%** |
| **Radiator Core** | Corrosion, stone damage, solder joint failure | Coolant pooling, sweet smell | **~25%** |
| **Water Pump** | Seal wear, bearing failure, gasket blowout | Weep hole drip, whining noise | **~15%** |
| **Heater Core** | Internal corrosion, fitting failure | Foggy windscreen, wet footwell | **~10%** |
| **Head Gasket** | Overheat damage, bolt torque loss | White exhaust, bubbling radiator | **~5%** |
| **Other (expansion tank, cap)** | Cracks, seal failure | Slow drip, hard starting | **~5%** |

Causes include **electrochemical corrosion** (dissimilar metals in coolant), **thermal cycling fatigue**, **vibration-induced cracking**, and **improper coolant chemistry** (mixing IAT with OAT coolants forms gel that blocks passages and stresses seals).

**(b) Repair Procedures (7 marks):**

**(i) Leaking Radiator Repair — 3 marks:**

1. **Drain the coolant** by opening the radiator drain cock. Collect in a clean container for reuse if less than 2 years old.
2. **Disconnect the upper and lower hoses** and any transmission oil cooler lines (if integrated).
3. **Remove the radiator** by unbolting the top and bottom mounts and disconnecting the cooling fan shroud.
4. **Inspect the core** for the leak location. For minor leaks, apply a **two-part epoxy radiator repair paste** or use a **radiator stop-leak pellet** for emergency fixes.
5. For major damage, **replace the radiator** with a new OEM unit. Refill with the correct coolant mixture and **bleed air pockets** by running the engine with the heater on maximum and the cap off until steady coolant flow is observed.

**(ii) Leaking Water Pump Repair — 4 marks:**

1. **Drain the coolant** below the pump level. Remove the negative battery terminal for safety.
2. **Remove the drive belt(s)** — serpentine or V-belt — by releasing the tensioner.
3. **Unbolt the water pump** (typically 4 to 6 bolts). Note the bolt torque sequence for reassembly.
4. **Clean the mounting surface** thoroughly with a gasket scraper. Inspect for warping. Install a **new gasket or O-ring** (never reuse the old one).
5. **Install the new water pump**, torquing bolts in a **criss-cross pattern** to the manufacturer specification (typically **20-25 Nm**). Reinstall the belt and refill coolant. **Inspect the weep hole** post-repair — it should be dry after a test run.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the diagnostic sequence** — Examiners award marks for systematic methodology, not just naming tools. Always state the *order* of operations.
> 2. **Forgetting to bleed air** after repair — A common 1-mark deduction. Air pockets cause localized overheating and thermostat malfunction.
> 3. **Not stating torque values** — KTU expects numerical specifications for fasteners; vague phrases like "tighten securely" earn zero.
> 4. **Ignoring coolant disposal** — Environmental safety is a KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome. Coolant is toxic (ethylene glycol) and must be disposed through authorized recyclers.
> 5. **Mixing coolant types** — Combining IAT (green) with OAT (orange) forms a gel that blocks passages. Always flush before changing coolant type.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure Cap Rating:** Standard automotive caps are rated between **0.9 bar and 1.5 bar** (13 to 22 psi). Never test above this.
- **Coolant Boiling Point:** A 50/50 EG-water mixture boils at **~106 °C at 1 atm**, rising to **~120 °C** at 1.0 bar gauge.
- **Freezing Point:** 50/50 mixture protects down to **-36 °C**; 60/40 (more EG) down to **-50 °C** (but reduces heat transfer).
- **Top Leak Locations:** Hoses (40%) > Radiator (25%) > Water Pump (15%) > Heater Core (10%) > Head Gasket (5%).
- **Diagnostic Order:** Visual → Pressure Test → UV Dye → Combustion Leak Test → Disassembly.
- **Repair Sequence:** Drain → Disassemble → Clean → Replace Gasket/Seal → Reassemble with Torque Specs → Refill → Bleed Air.
- **Torque Convention:** Always use a **criss-cross (star) pattern** for circular gaskets (water pump, thermostat housing).
- **Coolant Safety:** Ethylene glycol is **toxic and sweet-tasting** — a known hazard to pets and children. Never store in unmarked containers.
- **KTU 2024 Focus:** Outcome-Based Education emphasizes *practical skill demonstration*. Be prepared for **workshop-based viva questions** on identifying radiator, water pump, and thermostat components on a live engine.
- **NEP 2020 Alignment:** This topic supports the course outcome of demonstrating *diagnostic competence in engine auxiliary systems*, mapping to **Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)** cognitive domains.
- **Pressure Testing Time:** Minimum hold time is **2 minutes** for an effective test; less time may miss slow leaks.
- **Weep Hole Significance:** A small hole beneath the water pump bearing is a deliberate design feature — a leak here signals **seal failure** and warrants immediate pump replacement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Lubrication System: General Lubrication Flow in Automotives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Lubrication System: General Lubrication Flow in Automotives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lubrication System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A lubrication system in an automobile is an integrated network of mechanical and hydraulic components designed to deliver clean, pressurized lubricating oil at the correct viscosity, flow rate, and pressure to all moving parts of an engine and drivetrain, with the primary objectives of **friction reduction, wear prevention, heat dissipation, sealing, contamination control, and corrosion protection**, while simultaneously returning the used oil to a reservoir for filtration and recirculation.

The **General Lubrication Flow** refers to the standardized, sequential path that engine oil follows from the sump to the bearings and back — a closed-loop hydraulic circuit that is functionally analogous to the human circulatory system.

## 1.2 The "Why" — Functions of an Automotive Lubrication System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Six Primary Functions of Lubricant in an Engine:**
> 1. **Friction Reduction:** Forms a hydrodynamic/mixed/Boundary film between sliding surfaces (e.g., crankshaft journal vs. bearing shell).
> 2. **Wear Reduction:** Prevents metal-to-metal contact (asperity contact) under boundary lubrication conditions.
> 3. **Cooling:** Removes ~25–40% of combustion heat in an IC engine (the rest goes to coolant and exhaust).
> 4. **Sealing:** Oil film in piston ring-cylinder liner interface prevents blow-by of combustion gases.
> 5. **Cleaning:** Detergent additives suspend soot, carbon, and sludge particles.
> 6. **Corrosion Prevention:** Anti-corrosion additives neutralize acids from combustion blow-by and fuel sulphur.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Engine as a Human Body

Think of the engine's lubrication system exactly like the **human circulatory system**:

| Human Circulatory System | Engine Lubrication System |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Heart** (pumps blood) | **Oil Pump** (pressurizes oil) |
| **Arteries** (carry oxygenated blood) | **Oil Galleries** (drillings in block/head) |
| **Capillaries** (deliver to cells) | **Bearings, Camshaft, Tappets** (lubricated parts) |
| **Veins** (return blood) | **Drain-back holes & oil return passages** |
| **Lungs** (filter/oxygenate) | **Oil Filter** (removes contaminants) |
| **Blood** | **Engine Oil (SAE 10W-30, 15W-40 etc.)** |
| **Stomach/Kidneys** (clean blood) | **Centrifugal Filter / Oil Cooler** |
| **Body temperature (37 °C)** | **Operating oil temperature: 80–110 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metrics (must memorize):** Normal oil pressure in a passenger car engine: **1.5 – 4.0 bar** (≈ 22–58 psi). Oil temperature window: **80 °C to 110 °C**. Oil capacity of a typical 4-cylinder petrol engine: **3.5 – 5.0 litres**. Typical oil change interval: **5,000 – 10,000 km** (or 6 months).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Lubrication Systems in Automobiles

The General Lubrication Flow in automobiles is delivered through **one of three architectures** (or a hybrid). The flow path differs in each.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Three Architectural Types:**

### Type 1 — Splash Lubrication System
- Used in **small, low-speed engines** (e.g., vintage engines, small 2-strokes, scooter engines).
- **Mechanism:** A dipper or scoop attached to the connecting rod big-end **slaps the oil surface** in the sump, throwing droplets onto cylinder walls and timing gears.
- **No oil pump** is used; reliant on engine rotation.
- **Limitation:** Cannot reliably deliver oil to bearings under high load or at low RPM.

### Type 2 — Pressure Lubrication System (Forced-Feed)
- Used in **all modern multi-cylinder engines** (4-stroke, > 1000 cc).
- **Mechanism:** A positive-displacement pump **forces oil under pressure** through drilled galleries to all bearings, camshaft, and valve gear.
- **Components:** Sump → Strainer → Pump → Filter → Galleries → Bearings → Drain-back.

### Type 3 — Combination (Splash + Pressure) System — **Most Common in Practice**
- Pressure-fed to the **crankshaft main bearings, big-end bearings, and camshaft bearings**.
- Splash lubrication of the **cylinder walls** via oil flung from the big-end and from spray jets directed at the pistons.
- **This is the standard for all modern petrol/diesel automotive engines** and the focus of KTU Module 3.

## 2.2 Component-by-Component Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Oil Sump (Oil Pan)
- Acts as the **reservoir**. Capacity typically **3.5 – 8.0 L** depending on engine.
- Houses the **oil strainer** (a coarse mesh screen) at the pump inlet.
- Capacity is designed for **≈ 1 minute of oil circulation reserve** at maximum flow.

### 2.2.2 Oil Pump
Three types in practice:

| Pump Type | Mechanism | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **External Gear Pump** | Two identical meshing spur gears trap and carry oil. | Most common in modern cars. |
| **Internal Gear (Gerotor) Pump** | An inner rotor (lobes) and outer rotor (internal teeth) create crescent-shaped chambers. | Higher efficiency, used in many modern engines. |
| **Vane / Plunger Pump** | Sliding vanes or reciprocating plungers. | Older designs, some commercial vehicles. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump Discharge Equation (Gear Pump):**
> $$\large Q_{theoretical} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L \cdot N$$
> where $D_o$ = outer gear tip diameter, $D_i$ = inner gear root diameter, $L$ = gear face width, $N$ = pump shaft speed (rev/s). Volumetric efficiency of a healthy pump: **≥ 90%**.

### 2.2.3 Oil Strainer
- Coarse wire mesh (typically **60–80 mesh**) bolted to the pump inlet inside the sump.
- Traps large debris (gaskets, chips, rags) — first line of defence.

### 2.2.4 Oil Filter
- **Full-flow type** in most modern vehicles — **100% of the pumped oil passes through it**.
- Filter media: pleated paper with **β-ratio ≥ 75** at **20 µm** (per ISO 4548).
- Contains a **bypass valve** that opens at ≈ **1.0 – 1.5 bar** pressure differential, allowing unfiltered oil to reach the engine if the filter is clogged (prevents oil starvation).

### 2.2.5 Oil Galleries
- **Drilled passages in the cylinder block and head** carrying oil to the main bearings, camshaft, tappets, and valve gear.
- Diameter typically **6 – 10 mm** for main bearing feeds.

### 2.2.6 Pressure Relief Valve
- Located in the pump body or filter housing. **Spring-loaded valve** set to open at **≈ 4.0 – 5.0 bar**.
- Protects the system from over-pressurization (cold oil, blocked passages).

### 2.2.7 Oil Pressure Indicator / Sending Unit
- Sends signal to the **dashboard warning lamp** (typically activates below **0.3 – 0.7 bar** at idle).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 ESE / Module Tests**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Theoretical pump flow** | $Q_t = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) L N$ | m³/s | Sizing oil pump displacement |
| 2 | **Volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Diagnoses pump wear |
| 3 | **Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop** (in galleries) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pa | Pressure loss through oil gallery |
| 4 | **Shear stress in oil film** | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Pa | Hydrodynamic film strength |
| 5 | **Oil consumption rate** | $SOC = \dfrac{V_{consumed}}{V_{refilled} \times \text{km}} \times 10^4$ | L/1000 km | Servicing benchmark |
| 6 | **Reynolds number for oil flow** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Determines laminar vs. turbulent flow in galleries |
| 7 | **Sommerfeld number** | $So = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Bearing lubrication regime |
| 8 | **Stribeck equation** (μN/P) | $f = k \cdot (So)^n$ | — | Coefficient of friction in bearings |
| 9 | **Pump power** | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta_{mech}}$ | W | Power consumed by oil pump |
| 10 | **Oil temp-viscosity (Walther eqn.)** | $\log(\log(\nu+0.7)) = A - B \log T$ | mm²/s | Predicting viscosity at operating temp |

> **Constants to Memorize:** Standard atmospheric pressure **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar**. SAE 20W-50 oil at 100 °C: dynamic viscosity **μ ≈ 0.0094 Pa·s**. Density of engine oil ≈ **880 kg/m³** at 15 °C. Oil flow rate in a typical 4-cyl engine at 3000 RPM: **20 – 30 L/min**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The lubrication system directly determines **engine durability, fuel efficiency, and emissions compliance**. A poorly maintained lubrication system accounts for:
- **~70% of all engine wear** (per Shell Lubricants field studies).
- A **1% reduction in mechanical friction** (via optimized oil) saves **~0.5% fuel**.
- The EU **ACEA oil specifications** (A3/B4, C2/C3) tie directly to emissions standards — modern low-SAPS (Sulphated Ash, Phosphorus, Sulphur) oils protect DPFs and catalytic converters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in an Oil Gallery (Hagen-Poiseuille)

The **general lubrication flow** through a cylindrical drilling (oil gallery) can be modelled as fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid.

> **Starting Premise — Newton's Law of Viscosity:**
> For a thin cylindrical shell of radius $r$ and length $L$, the shear stress on the fluid is:
> $$\large \tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dr}$$

> **Step 1 — Force balance on the cylindrical fluid element:**
> Pressure force pushing the fluid forward minus pressure force at the other end equals viscous shear force on the cylindrical surface:
> $$\large \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau \cdot (2\pi r L)$$
> *(Negative sign because velocity decreases as $r$ increases — parabolic profile.)*

> **Step 2 — Substitute Newton's law of viscosity:**
> $$\large \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = - \mu \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

> **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from $r = 0$ to $r = R$:**
> At $r = 0$ (centre), velocity is maximum $v_{max}$. At $r = R$ (wall), velocity is zero (no-slip).
> $$\large dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$
> $$\large \int_{v_{max}}^{0} dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} r \, dr$$

> **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**
> $$\large -v_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$
> $$\large v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

> **Step 5 — General velocity profile (parabolic):**
> $$\large v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

> **Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate (integrate velocity over the cross-section):**
> $$\large Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$
> $$\large Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$$

> **Final Result — Hagen-Poiseuille Equation:**
> $$\large \boxed{\Delta P = \frac{8\mu L Q}{\pi R^4} = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}}$$

> **Engineering Insight — Why D⁴ in the denominator?**
> Doubling the gallery diameter **reduces pressure drop by 16×**. This is why engine designers use large-diameter main oil galleries (8–10 mm) wherever possible, and why **a single restricted passage** (e.g., a blocked feed to a camshaft bearing) can starve an entire engine.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Discharge of an External Gear Pump

> **Step 1 — Geometric volume of one tooth space:**
> A gear tooth meshing region displaces a volume equal to the area between the gear teeth and the housing wall, swept twice per revolution (two gears, two meshing events).
> $$\large V_{one\ tooth} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L$$

> **Step 2 — Number of teeth that pass the discharge port per revolution:**
> For a gear with $N_t$ teeth, each rotation delivers $N_t$ volumes:
> $$\large Q_{theory} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L \cdot N_t \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$
> *(in m³/s, with $N$ in rpm).*

> **Step 3 — Simplification (most textbook convention):**
> Using the standard displacement per revolution of the pump chamber:
> $$\large V_d = \frac{\pi}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) L \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/rev)}$$
> $$\large Q_{theory} = V_d \cdot N \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s, with } N \text{ in rev/s)}$$

> **Step 4 — Actual discharge (account for leakage):**
> $$\large Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{theory}$$
> Typical $\eta_v$ for a worn pump in need of overhaul: **60 – 75%**. A healthy pump: **> 90%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Symptom → Root Cause → Action

This is a **valuation-friendly matrix** commonly tested in KTU practical/ESE questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubrication System Troubleshooting Matrix (KTU Module 3 PLO Mapping)**

| # | Symptom (Customer Complaint) | Probable Root Cause | Diagnostic Action | Repair / Service |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Low oil pressure warning at idle** | Worn pump, thin oil (wrong grade), worn bearings | Mechanical gauge test; oil sample analysis | Pump rebuild, bearing replacement, use correct SAE grade |
| 2 | **High oil consumption (> 1 L/1000 km)** | Worn piston rings, valve stem seals, PCV fault | Compression test, leak-down test | Engine top-overhaul |
| 3 | **Oil warning light flickering at low RPM** | Low oil level, restricted pick-up, weak pump | Dipstick check, oil pressure test at 850 rpm | Top up, clean strainer, pump service |
| 4 | **Milky oil on dipstick** | Coolant leak into oil (head gasket failure) | Pressure test cooling system, combustion leak test | Replace head gasket, skim head if warped |
| 5 | **Engine knocking / rattling at start** | Oil drain-back valve fault, low oil level, wrong viscosity | Oil pressure test cold & hot | Replace drain-back check valves, correct oil grade |
| 6 | **Oil leak at sump gasket** | Crushed/hardened gasket, warped sump, loose bolts | Dye-trace test, visual inspection | Re-flange sump, new gasket, torque to spec (≈ 12–15 Nm) |
| 7 | **Black, sludgy oil** | Infrequent oil changes, short trips, PCV fault | Oil sample analysis, drain inspection | Full flush, new oil + filter, replace PCV |
| 8 | **Oil in air filter housing** | Turbo seal failure, excessive crankcase pressure | Inspect turbo seal, blow-by test | Turbo rebuild/replacement |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** An engine oil gallery has a diameter $D = 8\text{ mm}$, length $L = 200\text{ mm}$. Oil flow rate $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.012\text{ Pa·s}$. Calculate the pressure drop.

> **Solution (using Hagen-Poiseuille):**
> $$\large \Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$
> $$\large \Delta P = \frac{128 \times 0.012 \times 0.200 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.008)^4}$$
> $$\large \text{Numerator} = 128 \times 0.012 \times 0.200 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 4.608 \times 10^{-6}$$
> $$\large \text{Denominator} = \pi \times (0.008)^4 = \pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-9} = 1.287 \times 10^{-8}$$
> $$\large \Delta P = \frac{4.608 \times 10^{-6}}{1.287 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 358 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.00358 \text{ bar}$$

> **Interpretation:** A single clean oil gallery of 8 mm causes negligible pressure drop. Therefore, in healthy engines, **most of the pressure loss occurs at bearings and restrictors** — this is why bearing clearances are the critical design parameter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — General Lubrication Flow in Automobiles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OIL SUMP Reservoir 3.5 to 8 L] --> B[OIL STRAINER Coarse mesh 60 to 80 mesh]
    B --> C[OIL PUMP Gear or Gerotor type]
    C --> D[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE Set at 4.0 to 5.0 bar]
    D --> E[FULL FLOW OIL FILTER Pleated paper 20 micron]
    E --> F[OIL COOLER Optional in heavy duty engines]
    F --> G[MAIN OIL GALLERY 8 to 10 mm diameter]
    G --> H1[CRANKSHAFT MAIN BEARINGS Pressure fed]
    G --> H2[BIG END CONNECTING ROD BEARINGS]
    G --> H3[CAMSHAFT BEARINGS]
    G --> H4[HYDRAULIC TAPPETS LIFTERS]
    G --> H5[TIMING CHAIN TENSIONER]
    G --> H6[SPRAY JETS for PISTON COOLING]
    H1 --> I1[OIL RETURN to SUMP via drain holes]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I2[OIL RETURN via head drain back]
    H4 --> I2
    H5 --> I1
    H6 --> I3[OIL MIST for CYLINDER WALL lubrication]
    I1 --> A
    I2 --> A
    I3 --> A
    J[OIL PRESSURE SENDING UNIT] --> K[DASHBOARD WARNING LAMP at 0.3 to 0.7 bar]
    G --> J
    L[OIL LEVEL DIPSTICK] --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Combination Lubrication System (Most Common Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SUMP_REGION[OIL SUMP REGION]
        SUMPOIL[Engine Oil in Sump]
        STR[Strainer Mesh]
    end
    subgraph PUMP_REGION[OIL PUMP AND CONTROL REGION]
        PUMP[Positive Displacement Pump]
        RELIEFV[Relief Valve opens at 4.0 bar]
        REGULV[Pressure Regulator]
    end
    subgraph FILTER_REGION[FILTER AND COOLER REGION]
        FILT[Full Flow Filter]
        BYPASS[Bypass Valve opens at 1.0 to 1.5 bar delta P]
        COOL[Oil Cooler optional]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE_REGION[ENGINE BEARINGS AND GEAR REGION]
        MAINBRG[Main Bearings 1 to 5]
        BIGEND[Big End Bearings]
        CAMBRG[Camshaft Bearings]
        TAP[Hydraulic Tappets]
        SPRAYJET[Piston Cooling Jets]
    end
    SUMPOIL --> STR
    STR --> PUMP
    PUMP --> RELIEFV
    RELIEFV --> FILT
    FILT --> BYPASS
    BYPASS --> COOL
    COOL --> MAINBRG
    MAINBRG --> BIGEND
    BIGEND --> SPRAYJET
    MAINBRG --> CAMBRG
    CAMBRG --> TAP
    BIGEND --> SUMPOIL
    TAP --> SUMPOIL
    SPRAYJET --> SUMPOIL
    REGULV --> PUMP
```

## 4.3 Servicing, Repair, and Overhauling — Flow of Workshop Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Complaint] --> B[Initial Inspection Dipstick Visual Leak Check]
    B --> C{Diagnosis}
    C -->|Routine| D[Routine Servicing Oil and Filter Change]
    C -->|Specific Fault| E[Component Level Repair e.g. Pump]
    C -->|Major Failure| F[Complete Overhaul]
    D --> D1[Drain old oil and warm engine]
    D1 --> D2[Remove old filter]
    D2 --> D3[Install new filter and refill with correct grade]
    D3 --> D4[Run engine check for leaks and pressure]
    E --> E1[Remove faulty component]
    E1 --> E2[Inspect and measure]
    E2 --> E3[Replace worn parts or rebuild]
    E3 --> E4[Reinstall and torque to spec]
```
