> **KTU Insight:** The system transfers heat *from a low-temperature reservoir (cabin)* to a *high-temperature reservoir (ambient)* — which is only possible because **external work (W) is supplied by the compressor**.

## 2.2 The Heating Loop (Heater Core)

The heater core is a small radiator that uses **engine coolant (typically 50:50 ethylene glycol-water mix)** as the heat source. Hot coolant circulates through the heater core, and a separate blower (or the same blower via a mode door) pushes cabin air across it. A **heater control valve** regulates coolant flow.

## 2.3 Ventilation & Air Distribution

- **Recirculation Mode (RECIRC):** Cabin air is reused — faster cooling, but reduces freshness.
- **Fresh Air Mode:** Outside air enters via cowl vents.
- **Mode Doors (Actuated by vacuum, electric, or servo motors):** Direct airflow to face, feet, defrost, or bi-level outlets.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula, threshold, and operational parameter a KTU student must memorize for this module.**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Form | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coefficient of Performance (Heating) | $COP_H = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{in}}$ | **3.0 – 4.0** (typical) | Heat pump efficiency comparison |
| 2 | Coefficient of Performance (Cooling) | $COP_C = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$ | **2.0 – 3.0** (typical) | A/C efficiency check |
| 3 | Refrigeration Effect (per kg) | $RE = h_g - h_f$ | kJ/kg | Evaporator heat absorption |
| 4 | Compressor Work (per kg) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Compressor input energy |
| 5 | Heat Rejection (per kg) | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Condenser heat dumping |
| 6 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{discharge}}{P_{suction}}$ | **6 to 10** (typical) | Compressor stress indicator |
| 7 | Volume Flow Rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{suction}}$ | m³/s | Compressor displacement sizing |
| 8 | Saturation Pressure (R-134a at 0°C) | $P_{sat}$ | **2.92 bar (≈ 42.4 psi)** | Low-side baseline |
| 9 | Saturation Pressure (R-134a at 50°C) | $P_{sat}$ | **13.18 bar (≈ 191 psi)** | High-side baseline |
| 10 | Micron Vacuum Test Threshold | $P_{vac} < 500\ \mu m\ Hg$ for 30 min | **≤ 500 microns** | Evacuation quality check |
| 11 | PAG Oil Charge | $V_{oil} = V_{displ} \times 0.85$ | ml | Compressor re-oiling |
| 12 | Refrigerant Charge (R-134a, typical sedan) | $m_{ref}$ | **500 – 900 g** | System recharging |
| 13 | Temperature Split (Center Vent vs Ambient) | $\Delta T = T_{amb} - T_{vent}$ | **≥ 11°C (20°F)** | A/C performance test |
| 14 | Psychrometric Enthalpy | $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$ | kJ/kg dry air | Dehumidification analysis |
| 15 | Humidity Ratio (basic) | $\omega = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg water/kg dry air | Cabin moisture load |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above equations are not merely academic — they are used in:
- **Automotive OEM design** (Mahindra, Tata, Maruti) for compressor displacement and condenser sizing.
- **Garage diagnostic routines** (MACS — Mobile Air Conditioning Society worldwide diagnostic charts).
- **HSE/F-gas regulations** (leak detection thresholds, refrigerant recovery efficiency).
- **EV thermal management** (heat pump systems where the same compressor heats the cabin using waste heat from the drive inverter).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coefficient of Performance (COP) of an Ideal Vapor Compression Cycle

The **COP of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of *desired cooling effect* to *required work input*. We derive this from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the cyclic steady-state system.

### Step 1: Define the System Boundary
We draw an imaginary envelope around the **compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator**. After one complete cycle, the working fluid (refrigerant) returns to its initial state. Therefore, the **net change in internal energy (ΔU) over one cycle is zero**.

$$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

### Step 2: Apply the First Law to a Cyclic Process
The First Law states:

$$\oint dU = \oint (\delta Q - \delta W)$$

Since the integral of $dU$ over a closed cycle is zero, we obtain:

$$0 = \oint \delta Q - \oint \delta W$$

Rearranging:

$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$

Where:
- $Q_L$ = heat absorbed by the evaporator (low-temperature reservoir) = desired cooling effect
- $Q_H$ = heat rejected by the condenser (high-temperature reservoir) = waste heat
- $W_{net,in}$ = net work input to the compressor

### Step 3: Write the COP Expression
By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{cooling} &= \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} \\[6pt]
COP_C &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} \\[6pt]
COP_C &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Refrigerant Enthalpies
Using the standard P-h diagram notations (state 1 = compressor inlet, state 2 = compressor outlet, state 3 = condenser exit, state 4 = evaporator inlet):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(enthalpy absorbed at evaporator)} \\[4pt]
Q_H &= h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{(enthalpy rejected at condenser)} \\[4pt]
W_{c} &= h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(compressor work)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into the COP Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_C &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\[6pt]
COP_C &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)} \\[6pt]
COP_C &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A higher $COP_C$ means the system delivers more cooling per unit of compressor work. Modern variable-displacement scroll compressors achieve **COP values 15–25% higher** than fixed-orifice systems because the expansion losses $(h_4 - h_3)$ are minimized.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A vehicle A/C system uses R-134a. The compressor inlet enthalpy is $h_1 = 395\ \text{kJ/kg}$, the compressor outlet enthalpy is $h_2 = 430\ \text{kJ/kg}$, the condenser exit enthalpy is $h_3 = 245\ \text{kJ/kg}$, and the evaporator inlet enthalpy is $h_4 = 245\ \text{kJ/kg}$. The refrigerant mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 0.05\ \text{kg/s}$. Calculate: (a) Compressor work input (kW), (b) Cooling capacity (kW), (c) COP.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compressor Work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 430 - 395 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 35\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compressor Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_c &= \dot{m} \times w_c \\[4pt]
\dot{W}_c &= 0.05 \times 35 \\[4pt]
\dot{W}_c &= 1.75\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cooling Capacity (Refrigeration Effect):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_L &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt]
q_L &= 395 - 245 \\[4pt]
q_L &= 150\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_L &= \dot{m} \times q_L \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_L &= 0.05 \times 150 \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_L &= 7.5\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_C &= \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{150}{35} \\[4pt]
COP_C &= 4.286
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** COP > 1 confirms that the system is moving more heat than the work input. This is consistent with the Second Law and is **normal** for refrigeration cycles. The figure of 4.286 is on the **higher side** (typical real-world values are 2.0–3.0), suggesting this is an ideal-cycle numerical problem.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component Failure → Symptom → Root Cause

| Component | Likely Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Diagnostic Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressor** | No cooling; noisy clutch | Worn swash plate, leak, seized bearing | Clutch engagement test; amp draw |
| **Condenser** | High-side pressure too high | Blocked fins, internal restriction | Pressure gauge; IR thermometer |
| **Expansion Valve** | Frost on evaporator; low cooling | Stuck open/closed, sensing bulb leak | Feel line temperature; gauge reading |
| **Evaporator** | Sweet smell; misty windshield | Microbial growth, leak | UV dye; cabin air filter check |
| **Receiver-Drier** | High-side normal, low-side low | Moisture saturation, blockage | Sight glass bubbles; replace every 4 yrs |
| **Blower Motor** | Weak/no airflow | Brush wear, resistor failure | Voltage drop test |
| **Heater Core** | Coolant smell, fogging | Internal corrosion leak | Pressure test cooling system |
| **Hoses/Seals** | Gradual refrigerant loss | O-ring degradation | Electronic leak detector |
| **Thermostat (HVAC)** | Erratic cabin temp | Failed sensor | OBD-II scan |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Refrigeration Cycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor\nSuction Side\nLow Pressure Vapor] -->|Compression\nW_in supplied| B[Compressor\nDischarge Side\nHigh Pressure Hot Vapor]
    B -->|Heat Rejection\nto Ambient| C[Condenser\nHigh Pressure Liquid]
    C -->|Liquid Line| D[Receiver Drier\nFilter and Moisture Trap]
    D -->|High Pressure Liquid| E[Expansion Valve\nOrifice Tube]
    E -->|Pressure Drop\nJoule Thomson| F[Evaporator\nLow Pressure Cold Mix]
    F -->|Heat Absorption\nfrom Cabin Air| G[Cabin Blower\nDistributes Cool Air]
    F -->|Low Pressure Vapor\nreturns| A
    G -->|Recirculated or\nExhausted| H[Cabin Return Air]
    H -->|Filtered| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The compressor acts as the driver; refrigerant circulates in a **closed loop** A → B → C → D → E → F → A. The cabin air loop is a **secondary open loop** that exchanges heat with the evaporator and is exhausted or recirculated.

## 4.2 HVAC Air Handling Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Intake
        A1[Fresh Air Inlet\nfrom Cowl] --> A2[Air Filter\nCabin Filter]
        A3[Recirculation Door] --> A2
        A2 --> A4[Blower Motor\nand Fan]
    end
    A4 --> B1[Evaporator Core\nRefrigerant Cooling]
    B1 --> B2[Heater Core\nEngine Coolant Heating]
    B2 --> C1[Mode Door Assembly\nFace/Foot/Defrost]
    C1 --> D1[Center Vents]
    C1 --> D2[Foot Vents]
    C1 --> D3[Defrost Vents]
    C1 --> D4[Side Vents]
    subgraph Control
        E1[Control Head] --> E2[Actuators\nVacuum or Electric]
        E2 --> B1
        E2 --> B2
        E2 --> C1
        E1 --> E3[Sensors\nSunload and InCar]
    end
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Refrigerant_Loop[Refrigerant Loop]
        R1[Compressor] -->|Discharge| R2[Condenser]
        R2 -->|Liquid| R3[Receiver Drier]
        R3 -->|Filtered Liquid| R4[Expansion Valve]
        R4 -->|Atomized Mix| R5[Evaporator]
        R5 -->|Suction Vapor| R1
    end
    subgraph Air_Loop[Air Distribution Loop]
        A1[Blower] --> A2[Evaporator Coil]
        A2 --> A3[Heater Coil]
        A3 --> A4[Mode Doors]
        A4 --> A5[Cabin Outlets]
    end
    subgraph Coolant_Loop[Engine Coolant Loop]
        K1[Engine Block] -->|Hot Coolant| K2[Heater Control Valve]
        K2 --> K3[Heater Core]
        K3 -->|Cooled Coolant| K1
    end
    R5 -.Heat Exchanger.-> A2
    K3 -.Heat Exchanger.-> A3
```

## 4.4 Servicing Procedure — Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Customer Complaint\nand Symptom Log] --> S2[Visual Inspection\nHoses Belts Fins]
    S2 --> S3[Connect Manifold Gauges\nRead High and Low Side]
    S3 --> S4{Diagnosis}
    S4 -->|No Refrigerant| S5[Leak Detection\nUV Dye or Electronic Sniffer]
    S4 -->|Low Refrigerant| S6[Top Up and Recheck]
    S4 -->|No Pressure| S7[Compressor Failure Test]
    S4 -->|High Pressure| S8[Condenser or Fan Fault]
    S5 --> S9[Recover Refrigerant\nper F-gas Norms]
    S9 --> S10[Repair Replace Component]
    S10 --> S11[Evacuate System\n500 Microns 30 Min]
    S11 --> S12[Vacuum Hold Test]
    S12 -->|Pass| S13[Recharge R134a\nby Weight]
    S12 -->|Fail| S10
    S13 --> S14[Leak Test Soap Solution]
    S14 --> S15[Performance Test\nTemperature Split]
    S15 --> S16[Document and Return Vehicle]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a refrigeration cycle in an exam, **always include the state labels (1, 2, 3, 4)** and the working fluid (R-134a). The arrows must follow a **clockwise convention** if drawn on a P-h diagram, and a **logical fluid path** on a schematic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**List and explain the four main components of a vehicle refrigeration cycle. State the working fluid commonly used.**

**Model Answer:**

The four main components of a vehicle **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** are:

1. **Compressor** — Pumps low-pressure refrigerant vapor from the evaporator and compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature vapor. Driven by the engine via a serpentine belt and electromagnetic clutch.
2. **Condenser** — Rejects heat from the high-pressure vapor to the ambient air, condensing it to a high-pressure liquid. Located at the front of the vehicle, ahead of the radiator.
3. **Expansion Valve (or Orifice Tube)** — A metering device that suddenly drops the pressure of the liquid refrigerant, causing it to atomize and cool sharply.
4. **Evaporator** — Located inside the HVAC housing, where the low-pressure refrigerant absorbs heat from the cabin air, producing the cooling effect.

**Working fluid:** Modern vehicles use **R-134a (Tetrafluoroethane)**. Newer vehicles (post-2017) are transitioning to **R-1234yf** due to lower Global Warming Potential (GWP < 1 vs. 1430 for R-134a).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**What is the function of a receiver-drier in an automotive A/C system? Why must it be replaced whenever the system is opened?**

**Model Answer:**

The **receiver-drier** is a storage reservoir located between the condenser and the expansion valve. It performs three critical functions:

1. **Storage:** Holds excess refrigerant to ensure a steady liquid supply to the expansion valve.
2. **Filtration:** Contains a desiccant (typically molecular sieve — silica gel or zeolite) that absorbs **moisture** from the refrigerant.
3. **Filtration of contaminants:** Traps dirt, metal particles, and debris.

**Why replace when opened:** The desiccant becomes **saturated with moisture** after exposure to atmosphere. If the receiver-drier is not replaced, the absorbed moisture reacts with refrigerant to form **hydrochloric acid and sludgy deposits**, which corrode the compressor internals and the expansion valve. The rule of thumb is: **"Any time the sealed system is opened for more than 15 minutes, replace the receiver-drier."**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in Part B questions for:
> - **Omitting the P-h diagram** (carries 3–4 marks in thermodynamic explanations).
> - **Confusing high-side and low-side** pressure values (high-side = condenser side, low-side = evaporator side).
> - **Skipping the evacuation step** in servicing procedures — examiners will *deduct 2 marks* if evacuation is not mentioned before recharging.
> - **Writing "compressor oil = engine oil"** — this is a fatal error; always specify **PAG oil (Polyalkylene Glycol)** for R-134a systems and **PVE oil (Polyvinyl Ether)** for R-1234yf systems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Module 4 Set A]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic (block diagram), explain the working of an automotive air-conditioning system. Label the state points and indicate pressure and temperature conditions at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle A/C system is found to have **no cooling output**. Diagnose the possible causes systematically, and describe the step-by-step procedure to restore proper functioning using proper service equipment. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — System Schematic and Working:**

The automotive A/C system operates on the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle** using **R-134a** as the refrigerant.

**Working (State-wise description):**

- **State 1 (Compressor Inlet):** Low-pressure (~30 psi), low-temperature (~5°C) **saturated vapor** enters the compressor from the evaporator outlet.
- **State 2 (Compressor Outlet / Condenser Inlet):** The compressor raises the pressure to **~200 psi** and temperature to **~70–80°C**, producing **superheated vapor**.
- **State 3 (Condenser Outlet / Receiver-Drier Inlet):** Air flowing across the condenser (via ram air + electric fan) removes heat. Refrigerant condenses to a **high-pressure sub-cooled liquid** at ~50°C.
- **State 4 (Expansion Valve Inlet / Outlet):** Liquid passes through the **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)**. The sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of a portion of the refrigerant, cooling the remaining liquid-vapor mixture to **~0°C to 5°C**.
- **State 5 (Evaporator Outlet → Compressor Inlet):** The cold mixture absorbs heat from the cabin air blown across the evaporator coil. Refrigerant fully vaporizes and returns to the compressor, completing the cycle.

**Auxiliary Systems Include:**
- Receiver-drier (moisture removal)
- Sight glass (charge verification)
- Thermostatic switch (clutch cycling)
- Pressure cut-off switches (safety)

---

**Part (b) — Diagnosis of "No Cooling" and Restoration Procedure:**

| Step | Action | Tool/Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Verify complaint** — start engine, set A/C to MAX COOL, RECIRC, blower HIGH. | Visual / physical |
| 2 | **Inspect drive belt and compressor clutch engagement.** | Visual / manual |
| 3 | **Connect manifold gauges.** Read high and low side. | Manifold gauge set |
| 4 | **Interpret pressures:** | — |
| | • Both low: Undercharge / leak | — |
| | • Both high: Overcharge / condenser blockage | — |
| | • Low low, high high: Restriction (TXV or drier) | — |
| | • Low high, high low: Compressor failure (no pumping) | — |
| 5 | **Leak detection** using **electronic sniffer** or **UV dye** if pressure is low. | Leak detector |
| 6 | **Recover refrigerant** using a recovery machine (per F-gas norms). | Recovery unit |
| 7 | **Replace failed component** (e.g., compressor, condenser, expansion valve, receiver-drier). | Hand tools |
| 8 | **Replace receiver-drier** and **add PAG oil** to the new compressor (per OEM spec). | Oil measure |
| 9 | **Evacuate the system** to **500 microns** for **30 minutes** minimum. | Vacuum pump |
| 10 | **Vacuum hold test** — isolate pump; gauge must hold at < 500 microns for 10 min (checks for moisture/air ingress and gross leaks). | Manifold gauges |
| 11 | **Recharge with R-134a by weight** (e.g., 750 g) per OEM spec. | Charging scale |
| 12 | **Leak test** with soap solution or electronic sniffer at all connections. | Sniffer |
| 13 | **Performance test** — measure center vent temperature; ΔT should be **≥ 11°C** from ambient. | Thermometer |
| 14 | **Document** — fill service log, attach sticker under hood. | Manual |

> **Conclusion:** Diagnosis is based on pressure gauge readings; restoration follows the **RRRRR** sequence — **Recover → Repair → Replace drier → Re-evacuate → Recharge**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Module 4 Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the function of the **heater core** and **blower motor** in the HVAC system. How is cabin temperature regulated in an **automatic climate control system**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **overhauling procedure of an A/C compressor**. What tools and safety precautions are required? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Heater Core, Blower Motor, and ACC:**

**Heater Core:**
- A small radiator made of copper or aluminum tubes with fins.
- **Hot engine coolant** (at 80–95°C) flows through it continuously whenever the engine is warm.
- A **heater control valve** (vacuum, electric, or thermostatic) regulates the flow.
- Cabin air blown across the heater core absorbs heat — providing the **heating function**.

**Blower Motor:**
- A 12 V DC motor (or brushless in modern cars) with a centrifugal fan.
- Speeds are controlled by a **resistor pack** (for fixed speeds) or a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) module** for infinite variability.
- Located typically in the HVAC housing, immediately before the evaporator.

**Automatic Climate Control (ACC) Working:**

ACC uses a **closed-loop feedback control system**:

1. **Sensors** measure:
   - In-car temperature (cabin sensor)
   - Outside (ambient) temperature
   - Sunload (solar radiation sensor on the dashboard)
   - Evaporator outlet temperature (to prevent icing)
2. The **HVAC ECU** compares the actual cabin temperature to the **set point** entered by the driver.
3. The ECU calculates the *error* and actuates:
   - **Mode doors** (face/feet/defrost)
   - **Blower speed**
   - **A/C compressor clutch** (engage/disengage)
   - **Heater control valve**
   - **Recirculation door** (fresh vs. recirculated)
   - **Air blend door** (mixing hot and cold air for precise temperature)
4. The cycle repeats every few hundred milliseconds — maintaining the cabin within **± 0.5°C** of the setpoint in premium systems.

---

**Part (b) — Compressor Overhauling Procedure:**

**Tools Required:**
- **A/C manifold gauge set** (R-134a compatible)
- **Refrigerant recovery machine**
- **Vacuum pump** (capable of 50–100 microns)
- **PAG oil** (correct viscosity grade — e.g., PAG 46 or PAG 100, per OEM)
- **Refrigerant charging scale** or cylinder
- **Snap-ring pliers, bearing puller, swash plate remover**
- **Leak detector / UV dye lamp**
- **Clean lint-free rags, nitrogen kit** for pressure testing
- **OEM service manual** (torque specs)

**Safety Precautions:**
1. Wear **safety goggles and gloves** — R-134a can cause frostbite on contact.
2. **Recover refrigerant** before disconnecting any line — never vent to atmosphere (illegal under F-gas regulations).
3. Work in a **well-ventilated area** — R-134a is heavier than air and can displace oxygen.
4. Do **not** smoke or use open flames — refrigerant can decompose to **phosgene gas** (toxic).
5. Use **only specified PAG oil** — mixing with mineral oil causes sludging.
6. **Cap all open lines** immediately to prevent moisture ingress.

**Overhauling Steps:**

1. **Recover refrigerant** from the system.
2. **Disconnect suction and discharge hoses**; plug openings.
3. **Unbolt compressor** from bracket; remove drive belt.
4. **Mark all components** for reassembly orientation.
5. **Drain old PAG oil** and measure volume.
6. **Disassemble** — remove clutch hub, bearing, front head, swash plate, pistons, cylinder block, rear head, shaft seal.
7. **Inspect** for wear:
   - Bearing roughness (rotate by hand)
   - Swash plate scoring
   - Piston bore wear
   - Reed valves and shaft seal damage
8. **Replace** all seals, O-rings, and the **clutch bearing** (always).
9. **Clean** all parts with approved solvent; **never** use water-based cleaners.
10. **Reassemble** with **new PAG oil**, following OEM torque sequences.
11. **Leak test** the compressor with dry nitrogen at OEM-specified pressure.
12. **Reinstall** on the engine; evacuate the entire system; recharge.

> **Note:** Many OEMs (e.g., Toyota, Honda) recommend **compressor replacement rather than overhaul** because the cost of new internals + labour often exceeds a remanufactured unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The four core components of an A/C system are: **Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator**.
- ✅ The refrigeration cycle is a **Vapor Compression Cycle** — heat is moved from low-T to high-T using external work.
- ✅ **R-134a** is the standard refrigerant; **R-1234yf** is the new low-GWP replacement.
- ✅ The **compressor** is the heart; the **expansion valve** is the throttle; the **evaporator** is where cooling happens.
- ✅ **High-side pressure** (condenser side): 150–250 psi; **Low-side pressure** (evaporator side): 25–45 psi.
- ✅ **Evacuation must reach 500 microns for 30 minutes** before recharging.
- ✅ **Always replace the receiver-drier** whenever the system is opened.
- ✅ **PAG oil** for R-134a; **PVE oil** for R-1234yf — *never* mix them.
- ✅ **The heater core uses engine coolant**, not refrigerant, to warm the cabin.
- ✅ **ACC (Automatic Climate Control)** uses sensors + ECU + actuators in a **closed feedback loop**.
- ✅ Diagnostic procedure: **Read pressures → Identify fault → Recover → Repair → Evacuate → Recharge → Test**.
- ✅ **ΔT test:** center vent air must be at least **11°C cooler** than ambient for adequate A/C performance.
- ✅ Common failures: **compressor clutch, receiver-drier saturation, expansion valve sticking, condenser blockage, refrigerant leaks**.
- ✅ **Never vent refrigerant** — recover using F-gas certified equipment.
- ✅ Always **leak test** with electronic sniffer or UV dye; pressure-test with **dry nitrogen** (never compressed air — explosion risk with oil vapor).
- ✅ **Overhauling the compressor** requires clean-room conditions, OEM-specified oil, and full seal replacement.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Recover, Repair, Replace Drier, Re-evacuate, Recharge — in that order."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symptoms and Possible Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Symptoms and Possible Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Symptom-Fault Analysis** in vehicular engineering is defined as the systematic diagnostic methodology employed to identify, classify, and isolate automotive system malfunctions through the observation of observable deviations (symptoms) from specified operational baselines, followed by the establishment of causal relationships between these symptoms and their underlying mechanical, electrical, thermal, or hydraulic root causes (faults).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for PEMUT635, this topic forms the diagnostic backbone of vehicle maintenance engineering. A *symptom* is the externally perceivable manifestation of a fault—such as unusual noise, vibration, smoke emission, fluid leakage, or performance degradation—while a *fault* is the internal functional defect or component failure responsible for producing that symptom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):** Vehicle troubleshooting relies on the principle that every fault generates a unique set of symptoms. The maintenance engineer must establish a *causal chain*: **Root Cause → Component Degradation → Observable Symptom → Customer Complaint**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human body experiencing illness**. When you visit a doctor:
- The doctor observes your **symptoms** (fever, cough, fatigue)
- Through diagnostic tests, the doctor identifies the **fault** (viral infection, bacterial pneumonia, etc.)
- The treatment is prescribed based on the **root cause**, not just symptom suppression

**Vehicles behave identically.** A rattling noise is a "fever"; the loose heat shield is the "infection." Suppressing the noise (symptom) without tightening the heat shield (fault) leads to recurring problems and component cascade failures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five-Sense Diagnostic Principle:** A skilled technician uses all five senses—hearing (abnormal sounds), sight (leaks, smoke, color changes), smell (burning odors), touch (vibrations, excessive heat), and even taste (coolant glycol detection)—to map symptoms to faults.

### 1.3 Standardized Diagnostic Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics govern symptom evaluation:

- **Noise Frequency Range:** Audible mechanical faults typically manifest in **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz** with diagnostic significance concentrated between **100 Hz to 8,000 Hz**.
- **Engine Operating Temperature Baseline:** Standard coolant operating temperature is **85°C to 95°C**; deviation beyond **±10°C** indicates cooling system faults.
- **Oil Pressure Baseline:** At idle, minimum oil pressure is **1.0 bar (15 psi)**; at 3,000 RPM, **3.0 to 4.5 bar (45 to 65 psi)**.
- **Battery Voltage Baseline:** **12.6V** (fully charged), **13.7–14.7V** (running/charging).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid — "S.L.A.M." Diagnostic Framework:** Every symptom falls into one of four universal categories:
> - **S** — Sound (Knock, Rattle, Whine, Squeal)
> - **L** — Leakage (Oil, Coolant, Fuel, Brake Fluid)
> - **A** — Appearance (Smoke color, Discoloration, Wear patterns)
> - **M** — Malfunction (Loss of power, Hard starting, Poor mileage)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Symptom Classification

Vehicle symptoms can be mathematically and structurally classified into four primary domains. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to demonstrate mastery of this taxonomy in Part A questions (3-mark category).

#### 2.1.1 Acoustic Symptoms (Noise-Based Faults)

Noise symptoms are characterized by their **frequency**, **amplitude**, **timing** (continuous vs. intermittent), and **RPM correlation**.

| Symptom | Possible Fault | Diagnostic Trigger |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Metallic knock at low RPM | Worn crankshaft bearings | Pressure test |
| High-pitched squeal during braking | Worn brake pads | Visual inspection |
| Rattling on rough roads | Loose exhaust heat shield | Tapping test |
| Whining that increases with vehicle speed | Worn wheel bearing | Road test |
| Clicking on turns | CV joint failure | Boot inspection |
| Hissing from engine bay | Vacuum leak or coolant leak | Soap solution test |

#### 2.1.2 Visual Symptoms (Smoke & Fluid Leaks)

The color and behavior of smoke from the exhaust is a critical diagnostic indicator.

| Smoke Color | Composition | Possible Fault |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| **White (thick, sweet smell)** | Coolant vapor | Head gasket failure, cracked block |
| **Blue/Gray** | Engine oil burning | Worn piston rings, valve seals |
| **Black** | Excess fuel | Rich mixture, clogged air filter, faulty injector |
| **White (thin, disappears quickly)** | Water vapor | Normal cold-start condensation |

#### 2.1.3 Performance Symptoms

Performance symptoms manifest as deviations from baseline operational parameters. These are typically quantified using:

- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — increase indicates combustion inefficiency
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_th)** — decrease indicates mechanical losses
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** — decrease indicates intake/exhaust restrictions

#### 2.1.4 Sensory-Olfactory Symptoms (Smell-Based)

Unusual odors are immediate indicators of fault categories:
- **Burning oil smell:** Oil leak on hot exhaust manifold
- **Sweet syrup smell:** Coolant leak (ethylene glycol)
- **Rotten egg smell:** Catalytic converter failure (hydrogen sulfide)
- **Burning rubber smell:** Belt slippage or hose contact
- **Burning insulation smell:** Electrical short circuit

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Engine Compression Ratio | $\varepsilon = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$ | Dimensionless | Loss of compression symptom |
| 2 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | % | Power loss diagnosis |
| 3 | BSFC | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh | Mileage degradation |
| 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | % | Combustion fault analysis |
| 5 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $IMEP = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_s}$ | bar/kPa | Cylinder pressure imbalance |
| 6 | Cooling System Pressure Cap Rating | $P_{cap} = P_{atm} + \Delta P$ | bar | Overheating symptom analysis |
| 7 | Charging System Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I \times R_{circuit}$ | Volts | Electrical symptom tracing |
| 8 | Wheel Speed Sensor Frequency | $f = \frac{N \times n_{teeth}}{60}$ | Hz | ABS fault code interpretation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction Note:** In all KTU 2024 Scheme answer sheets, the vertical bar `\vert` is used in tables to avoid markdown rendering conflicts. Students writing physical exams should use a single vertical line or "given that" notation.

### 2.3 Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Logic Flow

The engineering reasoning behind symptom-to-fault mapping follows a structured **5W2H-Plus** diagnostic protocol:

1. **What** symptom is observed? (Categorize into S.L.A.M.)
2. **When** does it occur? (Cold start, warm engine, cruising, braking)
3. **Where** is it perceived? (Engine bay, undercarriage, cabin, steering)
4. **Under what load** does it appear? (Idle, partial throttle, WOT)
5. **How often** does it recur? (Continuous, intermittent, RPM-dependent)
6. **Trend Analysis** — Is it worsening over time?
7. **Correlation Check** — Multiple symptoms from same system?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This systematic methodology is not limited to automotive applications. The same diagnostic framework is deployed in **aerospace maintenance (FMEA protocols)**, **industrial machinery**, **power generation turbine maintenance**, and **medical device troubleshooting**, making this a transferable engineering competency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Compression Pressure Loss Symptom

When a customer reports **"loss of power, hard starting, and rough idle"**, the diagnostic pathway involves compression pressure analysis. The following derivation establishes the quantitative threshold for fault declaration.

#### Given Conditions:
- 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine
- Bore $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.09 \text{ m}$
- Number of cylinders $n = 4$
- Measured compression pressure $P_{m} = 8 \text{ bar}$
- Standard (healthy) compression pressure $P_{std} = 11 \text{ bar}$ to $13 \text{ bar}$

#### Step 1: Calculate the Swept Volume of One Cylinder

The swept volume $V_s$ (also called displacement volume) is the volume displaced by one piston during one stroke.

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08)^2 \times 0.09
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.09
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{3.14159}{4} \times 0.000576
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.000576
$$

$$
V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Converting to cubic centimeters:

$$
V_s = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ per cylinder}
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the Total Engine Displacement

$$
V_{total} = n \times V_s = 4 \times 452.4
$$

$$
V_{total} = 1809.6 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 1.81 \text{ L}
$$

This represents a typical small-displacement passenger car engine.

#### Step 3: Calculate the Pressure Loss Percentage

The deviation from baseline is the diagnostic indicator. The healthy range baseline is $P_{std,min} = 11 \text{ bar}$ and $P_{std,max} = 13 \text{ bar}$.

We use the minimum baseline as the conservative reference:

$$
\% \text{ Pressure Loss} = \frac{P_{std,min} - P_m}{P_{std,min}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Pressure Loss} = \frac{11 - 8}{11} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Pressure Loss} = \frac{3}{11} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Pressure Loss} = 27.27\%
$$

#### Step 4: Diagnostic Fault Classification

$$
\text{If } \% \text{ Loss} > 25\% \implies \text{Major Mechanical Fault Confirmed}
$$

$$
\text{If } \% \text{ Loss} \in [10\%, 25\%] \implies \text{Moderate Fault — Ring/Valve Suspect}
$$

$$
\text{If } \% \text{ Loss} < 10\% \implies \text{Acceptable — Within Wear Tolerance}
$$

Since our calculated loss is **27.27%**, this confirms a **major mechanical fault** — likely candidates are:
- Worn piston rings (most common cause at 27%+ loss)
- Burnt/exhaust valve
- Head gasket failure
- Cracked cylinder head

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Logic:** A single low cylinder on a multi-cylinder engine is typically a valve/head gasket issue. If all cylinders are uniformly low, the issue is generalized wear (rings, cylinder bore taper).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Symptom-to-Fault Mapping

| Observed Symptom | Primary Suspect Components | Diagnostic Test | Tools Required |
|------------------|---------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Hard cold start, smooth when warm | Choke / Enrichment system | Cold start fuel pressure test | Fuel pressure gauge |
| Engine surges at constant throttle | Vacuum leak | Smoke machine / propane enrichment | Vacuum gauge, smoke machine |
| Overheats in traffic, normal on highway | Radiator fan / thermostat | Temperature gun, fan operation test | Infrared thermometer |
| Steering wheel vibration at 80-100 km/h | Wheel balance / tire wear | Road force variation test | Tire balancer |
| Brake pedal travels to floor | Master cylinder / air in lines | Brake bleeder test | Brake bleeder kit |
| Battery dies overnight | Parasitic draw | Amperage draw test | Multimeter, clamp meter |
| Transmission slips on hills | Worn clutch bands / low fluid | Stall test, fluid inspection | Tachometer, dipstick |
| AC not cooling | Low refrigerant / compressor | Pressure gauge reading | Manifold gauge set |
| Excessive fuel consumption | O2 sensor / tire pressure / clogged filter | Long-term fuel trim scan | OBD-II scanner |
| Engine misfire under load | Ignition coil / spark plug / fuel injector | Cylinder balance test | Scan tool, oscilloscope |

### 3.3 Derivation: Battery Parasitic Drain Symptom

A common electrical symptom is "battery dead in the morning." The diagnostic threshold is:

$$
I_{parasitic, max} = 0.05 \times C_{battery}
$$

Where $C_{battery}$ is the battery capacity in Ampere-hours (Ah). For a typical $45 \text{ Ah}$ battery:

$$
I_{parasitic, max} = 0.05 \times 45
$$

$$
I_{parasitic, max} = 2.25 \text{ A}
$$

Wait — this is the discharge rate. The standard accepted parasitic draw is **20 to 50 milliamps (mA)**, allowing memory retention for ECU, clock, and alarm systems. If the measured draw exceeds **50 mA**, a circuit is staying active when it should be off.

$$
I_{measured} > 0.050 \text{ A} \implies \text{Parasitic Fault Confirmed}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Isolating the Faulty Circuit:** When parasitic draw exceeds 50 mA, technicians pull fuses one at a time while monitoring current. When the current drops, the last fuse removed identifies the faulty circuit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Symptom-to-Fault Diagnostic Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the systematic decision-making process a maintenance engineer follows when a customer reports a vehicle symptom. This block-level architecture mapping demonstrates the **S.L.A.M. framework** decision logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CUSTOMER COMPLAINT RECEIVED] --> B[OBSERVABLE SYMPTOM IDENTIFIED]
    B --> C{SYMPTOM CATEGORY}
    C -->|Acoustic| D[NOISE-BASED FAULTS]
    C -->|Visual| E[LEAKS AND SMOKE]
    C -->|Performance| F[POWER OR EFFICIENCY LOSS]
    C -->|Olfactory| G[BURNING OR CHEMICAL ODORS]
    D --> D1{Continuous or Intermittent}
    D1 -->|Continuous| D2[Check engine internals]
    D1 -->|Intermittent| D3[Check loose components]
    D2 --> D2A[Piston/Valve/Bearing]
    D3 --> D3A[Heat shield/Bracket/Trim]
    E --> E1{Fluid Type}
    E1 -->|Oil| E2[Engine gaskets and seals]
    E1 -->|Coolant| E3[Radiator hoses and head gasket]
    E1 -->|Fuel| E4[Fuel lines and injector O-rings]
    F --> F1{Under Load or Idle}
    F1 -->|Under Load| F2[Ignition and fuel delivery]
    F1 -->|At Idle| F3[Vacuum leaks and idle control]
    G --> G1{Sweet or Acrid}
    G1 -->|Sweet| G2[Coolant leak probable]
    G1 -->|Acrid| G3[Electrical short probable]
    D2A --> H[DIAGNOSTIC TEST]
    D3A --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    E4 --> H
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[FAULT ISOLATED]
    I --> J[REPAIR ACTION DOCUMENTED]
    J --> K[POST-REPAIR VERIFICATION]
```

### 4.2 Brake System Symptom Architecture

The brake system presents a clear case-study for symptom correlation. The block diagram below maps the major brake components to their characteristic symptoms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[DRIVER INPUT]
        P1[Brake Pedal]
    end
    subgraph HYDRAULIC[HYDRAULIC SYSTEM]
        P1 --> MC[Master Cylinder]
        MC --> BL[Brake Lines]
        BL --> WC[Wheel Cylinders OR Calipers]
    end
    subgraph FRICTION[FRICTION SYSTEM]
        WC --> PAD[Brake Pads]
        WC --> ROTOR[Brake Disc OR Drum]
    end
    MC -.Symptom.-> S1[Soft Pedal Travel]
    BL -.Symptom.-> S2[Fluid Leak Under Car]
    WC -.Symptom.-> S3[Uneven Braking Pull]
    PAD -.Symptom.-> S4[Squeal and Grinding Noise]
    ROTOR -.Symptom.-> S5[Steering Wheel Vibration]
    ROTOR -.Symptom.-> S6[Pedal Pulsation]
    S1 --> DIAG[Brake Bleed and Master Cylinder Test]
    S2 --> DIAG
    S3 --> DIAG
    S4 --> DIAG
    S5 --> DIAG
    S6 --> DIAG
    DIAG --> FIX[Repair and Bed-In]
```

### 4.3 Engine Performance Symptom Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Performance Symptom] --> B{Power Loss Confirmed}
    B -->|Yes| C[Perform Compression Test]
    B -->|No| D[Perform Vacuum Test]
    C --> C1{All Cylinders Low}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Rings and Bore Wear]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Individual Valve or Gasket]
    D --> D1{Steady or Fluctuating Vacuum}
    D1 -->|Steady Low| D2[Timing Retard or Restricted Exhaust]
    D1 -->|Fluctuating| D3[Valve Issue or Head Gasket]
    C2 --> E[Mechanical Overhaul]
    C3 --> E
    D2 --> F[Ignition and Exhaust Service]
    D3 --> F
    E --> G[Post-Repair Validation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Road Test and Customer Sign-off]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node identifiers in the above diagrams use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `D1`, `S5`, `C2`) to avoid conflicts with Mermaid reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, and `graph`. All node labels are presented as clean uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure rendering compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define "Symptom" and "Fault" in vehicle maintenance. Give two examples each. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

A **symptom** is an externally observable deviation from normal vehicle operation that alerts the driver or technician to a potential problem. A **fault** is the underlying mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic defect that causes the symptom.

**Examples of Symptoms:**
1. Blue smoke from the exhaust
2. Squealing noise during braking

**Examples of Faults:**
1. Worn piston rings allowing oil to enter the combustion chamber
2. Worn brake pad friction material contacting the rotor

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common student error is using "symptom" and "fault" interchangeably. The symptom is the *visible effect*; the fault is the *root cause*. Always distinguish between them.

---

#### Question 2: List four types of exhaust smoke and the fault each indicates. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

| Smoke Color | Indicates |
|-------------|-----------|
| **White (thick, sweet smell)** | Coolant burning — head gasket failure |
| **Blue/Gray** | Oil burning — worn rings or valve seals |
| **Black** | Rich fuel mixture — clogged air filter or faulty sensor |
| **White (thin, disappears)** | Normal condensation — no fault |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Below are two complete alternative questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Part B Choice 1**

**(a) Describe the systematic procedure for diagnosing an engine that shows loss of power and excessive fuel consumption. List at least five possible faults with their corresponding symptoms. [7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Customer Complaint (1 mark)**
Connect the vehicle to an OBD-II scanner and read Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs). Cross-check live data: short-term fuel trim, long-term fuel trim, MAF sensor reading, O2 sensor voltage, and coolant temperature.

**Step 2 — Visual Inspection (1 mark)**
Inspect air filter condition, vacuum hoses for cracks/leaks, spark plugs for fouling (black = rich, white = lean, oily = ring/valve issue), and exhaust system for restrictions.

**Step 3 — Performance Tests (2 marks)**
- **Compression test:** Should be within 10% across all cylinders
- **Vacuum test:** Steady 17–21 in-Hg at idle; fluctuating indicates valve issue
- **Fuel pressure test:** Should match manufacturer specification
- **Ignition scope test:** Verify proper secondary waveform

**Step 4 — Five Possible Faults and Their Symptoms (3 marks)**

| # | Fault | Symptom |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | Clogged air filter | Black smoke, sluggish acceleration |
| 2 | Faulty MAF sensor | Hesitation, surging, poor mileage |
| 3 | Leaking fuel injector | Strong fuel smell, black smoke, fouled plugs |
| 4 | Worn O2 sensor | Poor mileage, failed emission test, rough idle |
| 5 | Ignition timing off | Knocking, loss of power, overheating |

**Conclusion:** Begin with the most likely cause based on OBD codes, then move systematically through fuel, air, ignition, and mechanical systems.

---

**(b) Explain the symptoms of a faulty cooling system. How would you differentiate between a thermostat fault and a water pump fault? [7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Cooling System Symptoms (3 marks):**
1. Temperature gauge reads above normal (overheating)
2. Coolant reservoir bubbling or overflowing
3. Sweet smell of coolant from the engine bay
4. White steam from under the hood
5. Heater blowing cold air
6. Visible coolant puddles under the car

**Thermostat Fault vs. Water Pump Fault — Differentiation (4 marks):**

| Test / Symptom | Thermostat Fault | Water Pump Fault |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Heat pattern | Engine warms slowly or not at all (stuck open) OR overheats quickly with no heat from heater (stuck closed) | Engine overheats, but heater still produces hot air |
| Coolant flow | Coolant level stable; radiator hose may be cold | Coolant may be pushed out of reservoir; hoses may be hot but radiator remains cool |
| Timing | Fault appears within minutes of starting | Fault develops progressively, often with RPM correlation |
| Mechanical check | Thermostat removed and placed in hot water — should open at rated temperature | Water pump shaft play; impeller erosion visible upon removal |
| Heater core | Stays cold when thermostat stuck closed | May still be hot (coolant circulates) |

**Diagnostic Decision Rule:**
- If upper radiator hose is **hot** and lower hose is **cold** → Thermostat stuck closed
- If both hoses are **hot** but engine still overheats → Water pump impeller slipping/eroded
- If engine takes unusually long to warm up → Thermostat stuck open

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Part B Choice 2**

**(a) A customer reports a "knocking noise from the engine that increases with load." List five possible causes, the diagnostic test for each, and the corrective action. [7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

| # | Possible Cause | Diagnostic Test | Corrective Action |
|---|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Worn crankshaft bearings | Oil pressure test + mechanical stethoscope on block | Engine overhaul or bearing replacement |
| 2 | Low oil pressure | Oil pressure gauge reading at idle and 3000 RPM | Replace oil pump, check for blockages |
| 3 | Detonation / pre-ignition | Ignition timing check, listen for ping under load | Retard timing, check for carbon deposits, verify fuel octane |
| 4 | Worn piston / connecting rod | Compression test + borescope inspection | Piston and ring replacement |
| 5 | Loose drive belt / pulley | Visual inspection + belt tension test | Tighten or replace belt and tensioner |

**Diagnostic Sequence:** Begin with the cheapest, non-invasive test (visual + listening), then proceed to instrumented tests (oil pressure, compression), and finally intrusive tests (borescope, disassembly).

---

**(b) Describe with examples how smoke color, fluid leaks, and unusual smells are used as diagnostic symptoms in vehicle troubleshooting. [7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Smoke Color (3 marks):**
Smoke from the exhaust is a direct indicator of combustion chamber conditions:
- **Black smoke** = Excess fuel. Example: A clogged air filter restricts airflow, causing the ECU to over-fuel. Diagnostic: replace filter, monitor fuel trim.
- **Blue smoke** = Oil burning. Example: Worn valve stem seals allow oil to seep into the combustion chamber during deceleration. Diagnostic: compression test + borescope.
- **White smoke (sweet smell)** = Coolant burning. Example: Head gasket breach between water jacket and combustion chamber. Diagnostic: pressure-test cooling system, check for bubbles in expansion tank.

**2. Fluid Leaks (2 marks):**
- **Puddle location, color, and consistency** pinpoint the source. Oil (brown/black, oily) → engine gaskets. Coolant (green/orange/pink, slippery) → hoses, radiator. Brake fluid (clear to amber) → master cylinder, brake lines, calipers. Power steering fluid (red/amber) → steering rack, pump seals.

**3. Unusual Smells (2 marks):**
- **Burning oil** → Oil contacting hot exhaust manifold.
- **Sweet smell** → Coolant leak (ethylene glycol odor).
- **Rotten eggs** → Catalytic converter failure (hydrogen sulfide from sulfur in fuel).
- **Burning plastic** → Electrical insulation melting from short circuit.

**Conclusion:** Combining all three sensory inputs (visual smoke, fluid identification, olfactory detection) creates a multi-modal diagnostic signature that narrows the fault to a specific subsystem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing symptom and fault** — Students frequently lose 1-2 marks by describing a fault as a symptom. Always define both clearly.
> 2. **Missing the "why"** — A list without explanation scores partial marks. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires the *reasoning* behind each diagnosis, not just a list of causes.
> 3. **Ignoring operating conditions** — A "knocking noise" alone is insufficient. The KTU rubric awards marks for specifying *when* it occurs (cold start, under load, on turns).
> 4. **No diagnostic test mentioned** — Merely naming faults without specifying how to confirm them loses 30–50% marks on the relevant sub-question.
> 5. **Incorrect color identification of smoke** — White vs. blue is a frequently misidentified pair. Memorize the color-fault correlation thoroughly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE Preparation:**

- ✅ **Symptom** = Observable effect (noise, smoke, leak, smell, performance drop). **Fault** = Root cause of the symptom.
- ✅ **S.L.A.M. Framework** = Sound, Leakage, Appearance, Malfunction — the four universal categories of vehicle symptoms.
- ✅ **Smoke Color Decoding** = **White (thick/sweet) = Coolant; Blue/Gray = Oil; Black = Excess Fuel; Thin White (cold) = Normal.**
- ✅ **Five-Sense Diagnosis** = Hearing, Sight, Smell, Touch, and Tool-Assisted Testing form the complete diagnostic toolkit.
- ✅ **Compression Loss Threshold** = If pressure loss > **25%**, major mechanical fault confirmed (rings, valves, head gasket).
- ✅ **Parasitic Drain Limit** = Maximum acceptable battery draw is **50 mA**; anything above indicates a stuck relay, light, or module.
- ✅ **Cooling System Faults** = Upper hose hot + lower hose cold = **Thermostat stuck closed**; both hoses hot + overheating = **Water pump failure**.
- ✅ **Brake Pedal Travel** = Pedal to floor = **Master cylinder failure** OR **air in lines** OR **caliper seal leak**.
- ✅ **Diagnostic Sequence Rule** = Always begin with **cheapest, least invasive test** (visual, OBD scan) and progress to **instrumented** then **intrusive** tests.
- ✅ **OBD-II Scan First** = Modern vehicles encode faults as DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes). Always read codes before manual diagnosis.
- ✅ **Coolant Temperature Baseline** = Normal operating range is **85°C to 95°C**; deviation > **±10°C** signals cooling system fault.
- ✅ **Oil Pressure Baseline** = Idle: **≥ 1.0 bar (15 psi)**; 3,000 RPM: **3.0–4.5 bar (45–65 psi)**.
- ✅ **Mermaid Diagram Rule** (for graphical answers) = Use **alphanumeric node IDs** prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** When a symptom is given in the exam, always structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **Symptom Identification** — What category does it fall into?
> 2. **Possible Faults** — List 3-5 probable causes.
> 3. **Diagnostic Confirmation** — State the test that confirms each fault.
>
> This **3-layer structure** aligns perfectly with the KTU 2024 Scheme's **course outcome (CO4)** for *Vehicle Maintenance and Troubleshooting* and consistently scores 80%+ marks in board evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing in HVAC Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Servicing in HVAC Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Servicing in HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) Systems** refers to the systematic, scheduled, and corrective maintenance procedures performed on the thermal management subsystem of an automobile to ensure optimal cabin comfort, refrigerant integrity, airflow performance, and component longevity. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for the course PEMUT635, this encompasses *preventive maintenance*, *refrigerant handling*, *component cleaning*, *leak diagnosis*, and *performance verification* of the closed-loop vapor-compression refrigeration cycle integrated with the engine's cooling system for heating duties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT635 – Module 4):** Servicing of HVAC systems is treated as a **practical, workshop-oriented** topic. Students must demonstrate competence in identifying refrigerant types (**R-134a** and **R-1234yf**), using manifold gauges, performing evacuation, and following safe recovery procedures in accordance with **SAE J2788** and **SAE J2843** standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body. When you have a fever, your circulatory system fails to regulate temperature, and you feel miserable. A vehicle's HVAC is the **"circulatory system of thermal comfort"** — it pumps refrigerant (the "blood") through arteries (hoses), filters it (receiver-drier), cools it at the **radiator-like condenser** (lungs expelling heat), and finally delivers chilled air to the cabin (the body). 

If the refrigerant is **low**, the cabin never gets cold — like anemia causing fatigue.
If the **cabin filter is clogged**, airflow chokes — like asthma restricting breathing.
If the **compressor fails**, the entire cycle stops — like a heart attack.

**Servicing** is the doctor's check-up: measuring pressures, replacing filters, topping up refrigerant, and ensuring every "organ" of the HVAC system works in harmony.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics for HVAC Servicing:**
> - Standard ambient test condition: **35 °C (95 °F)** with **50 % relative humidity**
> - Center vent air temperature target: **3 °C to 8 °C** (after 5 minutes at full cooling)
> - High-side pressure (R-134a): **1.4 to 2.5 MPa** at 35 °C ambient
> - Low-side pressure (R-134a): **0.15 to 0.30 MPa** at 35 °C ambient
> - High-side pressure (R-1234yf): **1.3 to 2.3 MPa** at 35 °C ambient

## 1.3 Scope of HVAC Servicing in an Automobile

The automotive HVAC system is a **dual-function** assembly:

| Function | Subsystem | Heat Source/Sink |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| Cooling (A/C) | Vapor-compression refrigeration cycle | Engine-driven compressor |
| Heating | Engine coolant circulation through heater core | Waste engine heat (coolant at **85–95 °C**) |
| Ventilation | Blower + ductwork + cabin filter | Fresh/recirculated ambient air |
| Defrosting | Blend door routing | Heated air to windshield |

> [!TIP]
> **Why study HVAC servicing under PEMUT635?**
> Modern vehicles allocate **~12 % of warranty claims** to HVAC-related faults. Improper servicing can introduce air/moisture into the system, leading to compressor seizure within weeks. The KTU board examiner expects students to understand **both the science and the workshop safety discipline**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vapor-Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Foundation for Servicing

Servicing decisions are rooted in the thermodynamics of the refrigeration cycle. The four fundamental processes are:

**Step 1 — Compression (Compressor):** Low-pressure refrigerant vapor is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor.
- Purpose: Raise enthalpy so heat can be rejected to ambient air.

**Step 2 — Condensation (Condenser):** Hot vapor rejects heat to ambient air, condensing into high-pressure liquid.
- Purpose: Reject cabin heat + compressor work to atmosphere.

**Step 3 — Expansion (TXV or Fixed Orifice Tube):** High-pressure liquid drops sharply in pressure through the metering device.
- Purpose: Flash evaporation cools the refrigerant to near-saturation.

**Step 4 — Evaporation (Evaporator):** Cold, low-pressure refrigerant absorbs cabin heat and evaporates.
- Purpose: Cool the cabin air blown across the evaporator fins.

## 2.2 Why Each Servicing Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the "How"

| Step | What | Why | KTU Exam Angle |
|------|------|-----|----------------|
| Recovery | Extract refrigerant using SAE J2788 machine | Prevents ozone depletion; legal compliance | Mention GWP of R-134a = **1430** |
| Recycling | Filter, dry, and clean refrigerant | Removes oil, moisture, acids | Moisture > **50 ppm** damages compressor |
| Evacuation | Pull 29 in-Hg vacuum for 30 min | Removes non-condensables (air) and moisture | Air in system raises head pressure |
| Charging | Add exact mass (e.g., 600 ± 25 g) | Overcharge floods compressor; undercharge starves evaporator | Charge by weight, not by gauge alone |
| Leak check | Electronic sniffer or dye | Identify source of refrigerant loss | Dye = fluorescent **PAG oil + dye** |

## 2.3 Component-Wise Servicing Breakdown

### A. Compressor Servicing
- Check clutch engagement (voltage **12.5 ± 0.5 V**)
- Inspect shaft seal for oil staining
- Measure **clutch air gap**: typically **0.4–0.8 mm**
- Replace desiccant if compressor replacement was due to internal failure

### B. Condenser & Evaporator
- Clean exterior fins with **low-pressure water + coil cleaner** (never high-pressure)
- Inspect for oil streaks (leak indicator)
- Check airflow with **anemometer**: target ≥ **4 m/s** at cabin outlet

### C. Receiver-Drier / Accumulator
- Replace every **3–4 years** or with every compressor replacement
- Desiccant capacity: typically **8–15 grams** of molecular sieve (3Å type)

### D. Expansion Device
- **TXV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve):** Adjustable superheat target **4–8 °C**
- **OT (Orifice Tube):** Fixed; replace if system opened

### E. Cabin Air Filter
- Replace every **15,000–25,000 km**
- HEPA-grade filters improve PM2.5 capture to **≥ 95 %**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **high-yield** for KTU ESE. Commit them to memory.

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \quad \text{(Boyle's Law – low-side tank)}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{V_{disp} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_1}{60} \quad \text{[kg/s]}
$$

$$
COP_{cooling} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}
$$

$$
COP_{heating} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}
$$

$$
\text{Superheat} = T_{suction-line} - T_{sat}(P_{low-side})
$$

$$
\text{Subcooling} = T_{sat}(P_{high-side}) - T_{liquid-line}
$$

$$
T_{dewpoint} \approx T_{dry-bulb} - \frac{(100 - RH)}{5} \quad \text{[°C, approximate]}
$$

### Key Servicing Constants Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (R-134a) | Typical Value (R-1234yf) | Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|------|
| Global Warming Potential | GWP | **1430** | **4** | dimensionless |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | $T_b$ | **-26.3** | **-29.4** | °C |
| Critical temperature | $T_{cr}$ | **101.1** | **94.7** | °C |
| Evaporator target temp | $T_{evap}$ | **0 to 5** | **0 to 5** | °C |
| Condenser target temp | $T_{cond}$ | **50 to 65** | **50 to 65** | °C |
| Compressor oil | — | **PAG 46 / PAG 100** | **PAG 46 YF** | — |
| Suction superheat target | $\Delta T_{sh}$ | **4 to 8** | **4 to 8** | K |
| Liquid subcool target | $\Delta T_{sc}$ | **3 to 8** | **3 to 8** | K |
| Standard charge tolerance | — | **± 25 g** | **± 20 g** | grams |
| Moisture limit (dry system) | — | **< 50** | **< 50** | ppm |
| Non-condensable limit | — | **< 2 %** | **< 2 %** | by volume |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Superheat values reveal TXV misadjustment or low charge; subcooling reveals condenser efficiency or overcharge. A KTU examiner loves a question where the student must **diagnose a fault from two pressure readings + two temperature readings**.

## 2.5 Subcooling — The Servicing Engineer's Secret Weapon

$$
\Delta T_{sc} = T_{sat}(P_{high-side}) - T_{liquid-line-outlet}
$$

- $\Delta T_{sc} = 3$ to $8$ K → **System properly charged**
- $\Delta T_{sc} > 12$ K → **Overcharged** OR restricted condenser airflow
- $\Delta T_{sc} < 2$ K → **Undercharged** OR receiver-drier restriction

## 2.6 Superheat — The TXV Diagnostic

$$
\Delta T_{sh} = T_{suction-line-at-TXV-bulb} - T_{sat}(P_{low-side})
$$

- $\Delta T_{sh} = 4$ to $8$ K → **TXV operating correctly**
- $\Delta T_{sh} > 12$ K → **Low charge** OR restricted metering orifice
- $\Delta T_{sh} < 2$ K → **Flooded evaporator** (liquid entering compressor → danger)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate Through the Compressor

We begin with the fundamental volumetric displacement relation. Let $V_{disp}$ be the compressor displacement per revolution (in $m^3$/rev), $N$ be the rotational speed in **RPM**, and $\eta_{vol}$ the volumetric efficiency.

**Step 1: Theoretical displaced volume per minute**

$$
V_{disp, \text{min}} = V_{disp} \times N \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]
$$

**Step 2: Effective (actual) displaced volume per minute** (accounting for volumetric losses such as re-expansion and leakage)

$$
V_{actual} = V_{disp} \times N \times \eta_{vol} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]
$$

**Step 3: Convert to per-second basis**

$$
V_{actual} = \frac{V_{disp} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol}}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by suction density** $\rho_1$ (kg/$m^3$) to convert volume to mass

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{V_{disp} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_1}{60} \quad [\text{kg/s}]
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Volume of refrigerant drawn per second $\times$ density of refrigerant at suction conditions = mass flow rate of refrigerant. This is the central equation governing charge behavior — if $\dot{m}$ is too low (undercharge), the evaporator cannot absorb enough cabin heat.

**Numerical Sample:**
Given a swash-plate compressor with $V_{disp} = 160$ cc/rev, $N = 1800$ RPM at idle with compressor engaged, $\eta_{vol} = 0.78$, and $\rho_1 = 4.5$ kg/$m^3$ (R-134a at suction):

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{160 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1800 \cdot 0.78 \cdot 4.5}{60}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{160 \times 1800 \times 0.78 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{60}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{1.01088}{60} = 0.01685 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate cooling capacity** assuming $h_2 - h_1 = 150$ kJ/kg (typical evaporator enthalpy rise for R-134a):

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = 0.01685 \times 150 = 2.527 \text{ kW}
$$

Converting to BTU/hr (since automotive A/C is often quoted in BTU/hr):

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = 2.527 \times 3412.14 = 8623 \text{ BTU/hr}
$$

> **Servicing implication:** A typical sedan requires **~ 8000–12000 BTU/hr** cooling capacity. If the calculated $\dot{Q}_{evap}$ falls below 6000 BTU/hr, the system is **underperforming** and requires diagnosis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Subcooling-Based Charge Verification

For a sealed system, the **subcooling value tells us about charge level**. We will derive the relationship.

**Step 1: Saturation temperature** at the high-side pressure:

Read $P_{high}$ from the manifold. Suppose $P_{high} = 1.6$ MPa. From R-134a saturation tables, $T_{sat}(1.6 \text{ MPa}) \approx 53.4$ °C.

**Step 2: Measure liquid-line temperature** at the receiver-drier outlet. Suppose $T_{liq} = 47$ °C.

**Step 3: Calculate subcooling**

$$
\Delta T_{sc} = 53.4 - 47 = 6.4 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4: Compare against specification** (3–8 K target). Since 6.4 K lies within range, **charge is correct**.

**Step 5: Cross-check with superheat** at TXV bulb. Suppose $P_{low} = 0.22$ MPa → $T_{sat}(0.22) \approx -3.5$ °C. Bulb reads $T_{bulb} = 3$ °C.

$$
\Delta T_{sh} = 3 - (-3.5) = 6.5 \text{ K}
$$

This is within the **4–8 K** window. System is **healthy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use both?** Subcooling diagnoses condenser-side issues (overcharge, condenser airflow, receiver-drier restriction). Superheat diagnoses evaporator/TXV-side issues (low charge, faulty TXV, restricted orifice). Together, they pinpoint the fault.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fault Diagnosis from Pressure-Temperature Data

| $P_{high}$ | $P_{low}$ | $\Delta T_{sh}$ | $\Delta T_{sc}$ | Likely Fault | Service Action |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|--------------|----------------|
| High | High | Low | High | **Overcharge** | Recover & recharge by mass |
| Low | Low | High | Low | **Undercharge** | Leak test → recharge |
| High | Normal | Low | High | **Condenser airflow restriction** | Clean condenser fins |
| Normal | Low | High | Normal | **Mild undercharge + TXV overfeeding** | Recharge & verify TXV |
| High | High | Normal | Normal | **Non-condensables (air in system)** | Recover, evacuate, recharge |
| Normal | Normal | High | Normal | **Restricted orifice tube / TXV stuck closed** | Replace metering device |
| Normal | Low | Low | Normal | **Restricted liquid line / filter drier** | Replace receiver-drier |
| High | Low | High | Low | **Compressor mechanical wear** | Replace compressor |

## 3.4 Heat Rejection at the Condenser

The condenser must reject both **cabin heat** and **compressor work**:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{(compressor inlet to outlet enthalpy rise)}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** If the condenser cannot reject this total heat (e.g., clogged with debris), the high-side pressure rises, raising compressor discharge temperature above **130 °C** and accelerating oil breakdown. PAG oil begins to degrade above **175 °C**, forming acids that corrode the system.

## 3.5 Moisture-Induced Failure: The Acid Formation Mechanism

$$
\text{Refrigerant} + H_2O \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Hydrofluoric Acid (HF)} + \text{HCl}
$$

> This is why evacuation below **500 microns (29.92 in-Hg)** is mandatory before charging. A typical 30-minute evacuation pulls moisture below **50 ppm** — the safe threshold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle HVAC Refrigeration Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **functional flow** of refrigerant, air, and control signals within a typical automotive HVAC system. This is the canonical reference diagram expected in KTU board answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    comp1["COMPRESSOR\nEngine driven, swash plate"]
    cond1["CONDENSER\nFront of radiator"]
    rxd1["RECEIVER DRIER\nDesiccant filter"]
    txv1["EXPANSION VALVE\nTXV or Orifice Tube"]
    evap1["EVAPORATOR\nBehind dashboard"]
    blow1["BLOWER MOTOR\nCabin air movement"]
    cab1["CABIN\nPassenger compartment"]
    hcore1["HEATER CORE\nUses engine coolant"]
    blend1["BLEND DOOR\nMixes hot and cold air"]
    amb1["AMBIENT AIR\nOutside atmosphere"]
    ctrl1["HVAC CONTROL MODULE\nDriver inputs"]
    clutch1["MAGNETIC CLUTCH\nEngages compressor"]

    comp1 -->|high pressure hot vapor| cond1
    cond1 -->|high pressure liquid| rxd1
    rxd1 -->|filtered liquid| txv1
    txv1 -->|low pressure mixture| evap1
    evap1 -->|low pressure vapor| comp1
    amb1 --> blow1
    blow1 --> blend1
    hcore1 --> blend1
    evap1 --> blend1
    blend1 --> cab1
    ctrl1 -->|electrical signal| clutch1
    clutch1 -->|mechanical engagement| comp1
    ctrl1 -->|vacuum or electric| blend1
    ctrl1 -->|PWM signal| blow1
```

## 4.2 Servicing Procedure Flowchart — Industry Standard (SAE J2788 / J2843)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["STEP 1: INSPECT\nVisual check, cabin filter, belt"]
    stepB["STEP 2: CONNECT MANIFOLD\nHigh side and low side valves"]
    stepC["STEP 3: READ STATIC PRESSURES\nEngine OFF, ambient temp logged"]
    stepD["STEP 4: RECOVER REFRIGERANT\nUse SAE J2788 machine"]
    stepE["STEP 5: LEAK TEST\nElectronic sniffer or UV dye"]
    stepF["STEP 6: REPAIR\nReplace faulty components"]
    stepG["STEP 7: EVACUATE\nVacuum pump, 500 microns, 30 min"]
    stepH["STEP 8: CHARGE BY WEIGHT\nExact grams per VIN spec"]
    stepI["STEP 9: PERFORMANCE TEST\nCenter vent temperature"]
    stepJ["STEP 10: DOCUMENT\nService tag, customer invoice"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ
```

## 4.3 Component Cross-Section Reference Diagram (Text-Based Schematic)

```
                  ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                  │      VEHICLE HVAC SYSTEM      │
                  └───────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────┐   1   ┌─────────┐  2   ┌──────────┐
  │COMPRESSOR├──────►│CONDENSER├─────►│RECEIVER  │
  │  (eng.)  │ HP    │  (front) │  HL │ DRIER    │
  └────▲─────┘ vapor└─────────┘ liq └────┬─────┘
       │                                  │ HL
       │ 4                                ▼
       │ LP                       ┌──────────────┐
       │ vapor                    │EXPANSION VALVE│
       │                          │   TXV / OT    │
       │                          └──────┬───────┘
       │                                 │ LP mix
       │                                 ▼
       │                          ┌──────────────┐
       └──────────────────────────┤  EVAPORATOR  │
                                  │ (behind dash)│
                                  └──────────────┘

Legend: HP = High Pressure, LP = Low Pressure, HL = High Pressure Liquid
Numbers 1, 2, 4 denote the four thermodynamic processes.
```

## 4.4 Diagnostic Decision Tree for Common HVAC Faults

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["CUSTOMER COMPLAINT:\nAir conditioning not cold"]
    q1{"Center vent\ntemp above 15 C?"}
    q2{"Compressor\nclutch engaging?"}
    q3{"High side\nabove 2.5 MPa?"}
    q4{"Low side\nbelow 0.1 MPa?"}
    q5{"Visible oil\nstreaks?"}
    r1["Check blower motor,\ncabin filter, blend door"]
    r2["Check clutch gap,\nfuse, refrigerant pressure switch"]
    r3["Clean condenser,\ncheck electric fan"]
    r4["Leak test entire system,\nrepair and recharge"]
    r5["Identify leak source,\nrepair, evacuate, recharge"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- No --> r1
    q1 -- Yes --> q2
    q2 -- No --> r2
    q2 -- Yes --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> r3
    q3 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> q5
    q4 -- No --> r1
    q5 -- Yes --> r5
    q5 -- No --> r4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q: List any three safety precautions to be observed while servicing an automotive air conditioning system.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Always wear **safety goggles and insulated gloves** when handling refrigerant, as R-134a vaporizes at -26 °C and can cause frostbite on skin contact.
2. **Never vent refrigerant to atmosphere.** Recover using an SAE J2788-certified recovery machine to comply with environmental regulations (Montreal Protocol).
3. Ensure the **engine is OFF** and the system has cooled before disconnecting any hose. The high-side may contain liquid at high pressure and temperature.
4. Use **dedicated PAG oil** for R-134a systems; ester oil for R-1234yf. Mixing oils causes compressor failure.
5. Keep the system sealed against moisture; open fittings expose desiccant to ambient humidity, requiring re-evacuation.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between R-134a and R-1234yf refrigerants in two points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | R-134a | R-1234yf |
|-----------|--------|----------|
| Global Warming Potential | **1430** | **4** |
| Boiling point | -26.3 °C | -29.4 °C |
| Compatibility | Legacy vehicles | Post-2014 vehicles (EU) |
| Compressor oil | PAG 46 / PAG 100 | PAG 46 YF (with additives) |

R-1234yf is the **environmentally preferred** successor owing to its dramatically lower GWP, but it is mildly flammable (ASHRAE A2L classification) and requires **specialist servicing equipment**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for recovering, evacuating, and recharging an automotive air conditioning system. Mention the equipment used and the safety standards followed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Recovery — Using SAE J2788 Recovery Machine**

The vehicle is brought to the workshop, the engine is started and the A/C is run for 10 minutes to circulate oil, then switched off. The manifold gauges are connected to the high-side and low-side service ports. The recovery machine hoses are connected, and the **recovery cycle is initiated**. The machine pulls refrigerant from both sides into its internal cylinder, filtering out oil and contaminants. The cylinder is weighed to confirm extracted mass matches factory charge (within 10 % tolerance).

**(ii) Leak Testing**

Before evacuation, the system must be leak-tested. Either:
- **Electronic sniffer**: Detects refrigerant concentration > 14 g/year leakage.
- **Fluorescent dye injection**: PAG oil + UV dye is added; leaks glow under UV light.

Common leak points: shaft seal, hose crimps, condenser pinhole, evaporator O-rings.

**(iii) Evacuation — Using Vacuum Pump**

A two-stage rotary vacuum pump is connected. The system is pulled down to **500 microns (29.92 in-Hg)**. Hold the vacuum for **30 minutes minimum**. This removes:
- **Non-condensables (air)** — raise head pressure and reduce efficiency
- **Moisture** — prevents HF acid formation

**Rule of thumb:** A system that loses vacuum (pressure rises) within 10 minutes has moisture or a leak.

**(iv) Charging — By Mass**

The exact refrigerant mass per the **Vehicle Identification Number (VIN)** specification is loaded into the charging cylinder. With the engine off and cylinder inverted, charge is introduced through the **low-side port**. Liquid charging through the low-side is preferred for R-134a. The system is run, and final top-up may be done vapor-side while monitoring low-side pressure to avoid slugging the compressor.

**(v) Standards & Equipment Summary**

| Equipment | Standard |
|-----------|----------|
| Recovery/recycling machine | **SAE J2788** |
| Refrigerant purity | **SAE J2099** |
| Service hoses | **SAE J2196** |
| Leak detection | **SAE J2791** (sniffer), **SAE J2297** (dye) |

**(b)** A technician reports that a vehicle's A/C system shows: $P_{high} = 2.1$ MPa, $P_{low} = 0.18$ MPa, $T_{ambient} = 35$ °C, suction-line temperature = 7 °C, liquid-line temperature = 49 °C, with cabin vent air at 14 °C. Diagnose the fault and prescribe service action. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Look up saturation temperatures**
- $P_{high} = 2.1$ MPa → $T_{sat}(high) \approx 63.0$ °C (R-134a)
- $P_{low} = 0.18$ MPa → $T_{sat}(low) \approx -9.0$ °C (R-134a)

**Step 2: Calculate subcooling**
$$
\Delta T_{sc} = 63.0 - 49 = 14 \text{ K}
$$
**Interpretation:** Subcooling above 12 K suggests **condenser airflow restriction** OR **overcharge**.

**Step 3: Calculate superheat**
$$
\Delta T_{sh} = 7 - (-9.0) = 16 \text{ K}
$$
**Interpretation:** Superheat above 12 K indicates **undercharge or restricted metering**.

**Step 4: Cross-reference against analytical matrix**

Both superheat AND subcooling are abnormal — high subcooling rules out simple undercharge (undercharge gives low subcooling). The combination points to **restricted metering device with partially restricted condenser airflow**. Cabin vent at 14 °C is too warm.

**Step 5: Prescribed service action**
1. Inspect condenser fins for blockage (leaves, bugs, debris). Clean with low-pressure water.
2. Verify electric cooling fan operation (both fans should run with A/C on).
3. Replace expansion valve (likely partially restricted).
4. Replace receiver-drier (desiccant likely saturated).
5. Recover refrigerant, evacuate to 500 microns for 30 min, recharge by mass to OEM spec.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often state "undercharge" simply because $P_{low}$ is low. But low-side pressure alone is insufficient. You MUST compute superheat AND subcooling before concluding. Half the marks are awarded for the **correct diagnostic reasoning**, not just identifying one symptom.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the components of an automotive HVAC system and the role of each in cabin comfort. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The automotive HVAC system consists of the following major components:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Compressor** | Pump that circulates refrigerant; driven by engine via serpentine belt (or electric in hybrids) |
| **Condenser** | Heat exchanger mounted in front of radiator; rejects heat to ambient air |
| **Receiver-Drier** | Storage vessel with desiccant; removes moisture and filters contaminants |
| **Expansion Valve (TXV/OT)** | Metering device; drops pressure to enable evaporation |
| **Evaporator** | Heat exchanger inside HVAC housing; absorbs cabin heat |
| **Blower Motor** | Forces air through evaporator and heater core |
| **Heater Core** | Uses engine coolant heat to warm cabin |
| **Blend Door Actuator** | Mixes cold and hot air to achieve desired temperature |
| **Cabin Air Filter** | Removes dust, pollen, PM2.5 from incoming air |
| **HVAC Control Module** | Receives driver inputs and commands actuators |
| **Magnetic Clutch** | Engages/disengages compressor as needed |

The block diagram (Section 4.1 above) shows the functional flow:
