- **Refrigerant loop:** Compressor → Condenser → Receiver-Drier → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → back to Compressor
- **Air loop:** Ambient or recirculated air → Blower → Evaporator (cooling) and/or Heater Core (heating) → Blend Door → Cabin
- **Control loop:** Driver inputs to HVAC module → command signals to clutch, blend door, blower

**Role in cabin comfort:**
- **Cooling** (A/C mode): Compressor circulation enables evaporator to chill air.
- **Heating** (heater mode): Engine coolant circulates through heater core; blower pushes cabin air across it.
- **Ventilation** (fresh air mode): Ambient air is filtered and delivered.
- **Defrost/Defog** (bi-level mode): Hot air directed to windshield; dehumidification via A/C removes moisture.

**(b)** A compressor has displacement 170 cc/rev and runs at 2000 RPM with volumetric efficiency 0.80. The suction density of R-134a is 4.8 kg/m³. If the evaporator enthalpy rise is 145 kJ/kg, calculate: **(i)** refrigerant mass flow rate, **(ii)** cooling capacity in kW and BTU/hr. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Mass flow rate calculation**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{V_{disp} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_1}{60}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{170 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 0.80 \times 4.8}{60}
$$

Compute the numerator step-by-step:

$$
170 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 = 170 \times 2000 \times 10^{-6} = 340000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.34
$$

$$
0.34 \times 0.80 = 0.272
$$

$$
0.272 \times 4.8 = 1.3056
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{1.3056}{60} = 0.02176 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Cooling capacity**

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = 0.02176 \times 145 = 3.155 \text{ kW}
$$

Converting to BTU/hr (1 kW = 3412.14 BTU/hr):

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = 3.155 \times 3412.14 = 10765 \text{ BTU/hr}
$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
3.155 \times 3412.14
$$

$$
= 3 \times 3412.14 + 0.155 \times 3412.14
$$

$$
= 10236.42 + 528.88 = 10765.30 \text{ BTU/hr}
$$

**Result:** The system delivers approximately **3.16 kW** or **10,765 BTU/hr**, which is within the expected **8,000–12,000 BTU/hr** range for a mid-size sedan. Performance is **acceptable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Two common mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60** to convert RPM to RPS — this alone costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** of V_disp (cc vs. m³). The factor $10^{-6}$ converts cc to m³. Skipping it gives an answer 1 million times too large.
> 
> Always write the **unit cancellation** explicitly in your workings.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Refrigerant cycle:** 4 processes — Compression → Condensation → Expansion → Evaporation.
- [x] **Always recover before opening** the system. Venting is illegal and environmentally harmful.
- [x] **Evacuate to 500 microns** for at least 30 minutes; never skip this step.
- [x] **Charge by mass**, not by gauge pressure. Each vehicle has a specific gram value tied to its VIN.
- [x] **Subcooling 3–8 K** + **Superheat 4–8 K** = healthy system.
- [x] **R-134a** has GWP = **1430**; **R-1234yf** has GWP = **4**.
- [x] **PAG oil** for R-134a systems; **PAG YF oil** for R-1234yf. Never mix.
- [x] **Replace receiver-drier** every time the system is opened for > 24 hours.
- [x] **Cabin air filter** replacement interval: **15,000–25,000 km**.
- [x] **Magnetic clutch air gap** specification: **0.4–0.8 mm**.
- [x] **Standards to remember:** SAE **J2788** (recovery), J2099 (purity), J2196 (hoses), J2791 (sniffer).
- [x] **HF acid** forms when moisture + refrigerant combine at high temperature → corrosion → compressor failure.
- [x] **High-side too high + Low-side too high** → Overcharge or non-condensables.
- [x] **High-side low + Low-side low** → Undercharge or low airflow across condenser.
- [x] **Cooling capacity formula:** $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \approx \frac{V_{disp} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_1 \cdot \Delta h}{60}$.
- [x] **Document every service**: date, refrigerant added, oil added, parts replaced, customer signature.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam mantra (repeat thrice):**
> "**Recover → Repair → Evacuate → Charge → Test → Document**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leak Detection and Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Leak Detection and Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leak Detection and Repair (LDAR):** In the context of automotive maintenance, Leak Detection and Repair is defined as the systematic procedure of identifying the **source, type, and severity** of fluid or gas escape from a vehicle's sealed systems, followed by the execution of appropriate corrective actions to restore system integrity, ensure operational safety, prevent component damage, and maintain compliance with emission and performance standards.

A vehicle comprises numerous **pressurized and sealed systems**, each susceptible to leakage due to thermal cycling, vibration, aging of elastomeric seals, mechanical wear, corrosion, or improper assembly. The major fluid/gas systems requiring LDAR include:

| System | Fluid/Gas | Typical Operating Pressure |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Engine Lubrication | Engine oil (SAE 5W-30, 10W-40) | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa (at idle) |
| Cooling | Coolant (Ethylene-Glycol based) | 0.05 – 0.15 MPa (cap rated) |
| Fuel Delivery | Petrol / Diesel | 0.25 – 4.0 MPa (CRDi injectors) |
| Brake | DOT 3 / DOT 4 brake fluid | 0.6 – 1.0 MPa (pedal feel) |
| Air Conditioning | R-134a / R-1234yf refrigerant | 0.4 – 2.5 MPa (cycle) |
| Exhaust | Combustion gases | 0.05 – 0.20 MPa (back-pressure) |
| Pneumatic / Hydraulic | Air / Hydraulic oil | 0.4 – 1.2 MPa |
| Intake (Vacuum) | Air (negative pressure) | -0.03 to -0.08 MPa gauge |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Damaged Water Pipe" Analogy**
> Imagine your home has a hidden water pipe that is dripping slowly behind a wall. You don't see water directly, but you notice the water bill rising and damp spots appearing. To find it, you might:
> 1. **Listen** with a stethoscope (acoustic detection),
> 2. **Add colored dye** into the water and watch where the color emerges (UV dye tracing),
> 3. **Pressurize** the pipe with air and listen for hissing (pressure-decay / smoke test),
> 4. **Physically open** the wall at the suspected spot (visual inspection after teardown).

A vehicle's sealed systems behave **exactly like this damaged water pipe**. Leaks cause:
- Loss of functional fluid → poor performance
- Contamination of surrounding components → secondary failures
- Safety hazards (fire, brake failure, exhaust fume ingress)
- Environmental pollution and emission non-compliance

Hence LDAR follows a **"Locate → Diagnose → Repair → Verify"** workflow.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

- **Leak Rate (L):** Expressed in **g/year** (refrigerants), **mL/hour** (oil), or **kPa/min** (pressure decay).
- **Acceptable Pressure Decay:** Typically **< 2 kPa in 5 minutes** for a pressurized cooling system (cold test).
- **Permissible Oil Consumption:** Up to **0.5 L per 1000 km** (manufacturer-dependent) — anything above is treated as an "internal leak."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Classification of Vehicle Leaks

Leaks are classified along three axes:

1. **By State of Fluid Escaping**
   - *Liquid leaks:* Engine oil, coolant, brake fluid, transmission oil, power-steering fluid, fuel.
   - *Gas leaks:* Refrigerant (R-134a), exhaust gases, intake (vacuum leaks = unmetered air ingress), compressed air (brakes).

2. **By Visibility / Location**
   - *External leak:* Visible externally; pool, drip, or stain on the ground.
   - *Internal leak:* Fluid migrates into an adjacent system (e.g., oil into coolant via head-gasket failure; coolant into combustion chamber → white exhaust smoke).
   - *Hidden / Intermittent leak:* Manifests only under specific conditions (hot, cold, high load).

3. **By Severity**
   - *Seepage:* Slow wetting of seal surface; visible oil film.
   - *Drip:* Formation of distinct drops (< 1 drop / minute).
   - *Running leak:* Continuous stream.
   - *Catastrophic:* Sudden, large-volume loss (e.g., burst hose).

## 2.2 Common Leak Points in a Vehicle

| System | High-Frequency Leak Points | Root Cause |
|--------|---------------------------|------------|
| Engine | Valve cover gasket, oil pan gasket, front/rear crankshaft seals, head gasket, oil filter housing | Heat cycling, bolt loosening, gasket ageing |
| Cooling | Water pump shaft seal, radiator hoses, thermostat housing, expansion tank, heater core | Electrochemical corrosion, hose brittleness |
| Fuel | Injector O-rings, fuel rail connections, high-pressure pump seals, fuel line crimp joints | Vibration, O-ring hardening |
| Brake | Caliper piston seal, master cylinder seal, wheel cylinder, brake line flare nuts | Seal swelling, corrosion |
| AC | Condenser, evaporator, compressor shaft seal, O-rings at service ports | UV degradation, moisture ingress |
| Exhaust | Manifold gasket, flex joint, catalytic converter joints, EGR pipe | Thermal fatigue, rusting |
| Intake | Intake manifold gasket, throttle body, PCV valve, vacuum hoses | Plastic embrittlement |

## 2.3 Step-Wise Detection Logic (Why & How)

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Recommended Detection Ladder:** Always proceed from the **least invasive** method to the most invasive.

**Step 1 — Customer Complaint & Symptom Log**
- Establishes *which* system is involved (e.g., low coolant level + sweet smell + white smoke = cooling/head-gasket).

**Step 2 — Visual & Olfactory Inspection**
- Look for **wet residue, staining, mineral deposits, dried crystalline coolant trails, or soot tracks**. The color and consistency of the residue identifies the fluid (oil = brown/black; coolant = green/orange/pink; brake fluid = clear → dark amber; refrigerant = oily film).

**Step 3 — Pressure Testing**
- System is sealed and pressurized with shop-air or hand pump to spec. Pressure decay over time indicates a leak.
- *How:* For coolant systems, use a **radiator pressure tester** (typically pressurizes to **0.1–0.15 MPa**). For fuel, use a **fuel pressure gauge** and monitor static hold.
- *Why:* Quantifies the leak; eliminates guesswork.

**Step 4 — Smoke / Tracer Gas Test**
- Inert smoke or helium/nitrogen is introduced; the leak point is identified by observing smoke emergence or using a **sniffer probe (electronic gas detector)**.
- Used for: intake vacuum leaks, exhaust leaks, EVAP system, AC refrigerant leaks (electronic leak detector for R-134a).

**Step 5 — UV Dye Fluorescent Test**
- A fluorescent dye (e.g., Tracerline TP-3400 series) is added to the system. A **UV-A blacklight (365 nm)** reveals the leak as a glowing yellow-green trail.
- Standard in AC LDAR and engine oil leak tracing.

**Step 6 — Dye-Penetrant / Soapy Water**
- A 1:1 soap-water solution brushed on pressurized joints produces **bubbles** at the leak point. Cheap and effective for low-pressure systems.

**Step 7 — Acoustic / Ultrasonic Detection**
- An **ultrasonic leak detector (40 kHz range)** picks up high-frequency hissing inaudible to humans. Used for pressurized gas and air leaks.

**Step 8 — Disassembly / Component Teardown**
- Last resort. Confirms internal leaks (e.g., head gasket, valve stem seals).

## 2.4 KTU Reference / Cheat Sheet Table

| Detection Method | Target System | Working Principle | Typical Sensitivity |
|------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Visual Inspection | All external | Residue identification | Coarse |
| Pressure-Decay Test | Cooling, fuel, intake | Measure ΔP over time | 2 kPa / 5 min |
| Smoke Test | Intake, EVAP, exhaust | Visible smoke escape | 1 mm orifice |
| UV Dye + Blacklight | AC, oil, coolant | Fluorescence | Very high |
| Electronic Sniffer (He, HFC) | AC, fuel vapor | Gas-specific sensor | 3 g/year (HFC) |
| Ultrasonic Detector | Pressurized gas/air | 40 kHz acoustic | 0.05 mm orifice |
| Dye Penetrant / Soap Water | Low-pressure joints | Bubble formation | 0.5 mm orifice |
| Compression / Leak-Down Test | Combustion chamber | Measure cylinder pressure retention | Cylinder-specific |
| Chemical Test Strips | Coolant in oil | Colour change reaction | Qualitative |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

LDAR protocols are mandated by **environmental agencies** (EPA, CPCB in India) for refrigerant handling to prevent **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** gas release. A single kg of R-134a has a GWP of **1430** (over 100 years). Hence certified workshops must use calibrated leak detectors and log all service work. Similar protocols apply in **aerospace hydraulic systems, CNG fuel systems in vehicles, and hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles** where leak integrity is safety-critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pressure-Decay Leak Rate Calculation (Quantitative Core)

The most common quantitative procedure is the **Pressure-Decay Test**. The fundamental governing relation is derived from the **Ideal Gas Law** for a fixed-volume container with a small leak.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Leak Rate Equation

Let:
- $V$ = system internal volume (m³)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $P_0$ = initial charge pressure (Pa, absolute)
- $P_f$ = final pressure after time $t$ (Pa, absolute)
- $R$ = specific gas constant (J/kg·K)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass leak rate (kg/s)
- $m$ = mass of gas in system (kg)

By the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**:

$$P V = m R T$$

Differentiating with respect to time (at constant $V$ and $T$, assuming isothermal slow leak):

$$\frac{dP}{dt} V = \frac{dm}{dt} R T$$

$$\frac{dP}{dt} V = -\dot{m} \, R T$$

The negative sign indicates pressure drops as mass leaves. Solving for leak rate:

$$\dot{m} = -\frac{V}{R T} \frac{dP}{dt}$$

For a finite observation interval (from $P_0$ to $P_f$ over time $t$):

$$\dot{m} = \frac{V (P_0 - P_f)}{R \, T \, t}$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Radiator Pressure-Decay

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A car's cooling system has an internal volume of 12 L. It is pressurized to 100 kPa (gauge) at 25 °C. After 10 minutes, the gauge reads 96 kPa. Atmospheric pressure is 101 kPa. Determine the leak rate in kg/s and assess acceptability.

**Step 1 — Convert gauge to absolute**

$$P_{0,\text{abs}} = 100 + 101 = 201 \text{ kPa} = 201{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{f,\text{abs}} = 96 + 101 = 197 \text{ kPa} = 197{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Convert temperature to Kelvin**

$$T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume**

$$V = 12 \text{ L} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Use R for air (since shop air is used)**

$$R_{\text{air}} = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{0.012 \times (201{,}000 - 197{,}000)}{287 \times 298.15 \times (10 \times 60)}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{0.012 \times 4000}{287 \times 298.15 \times 600}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{48}{51{,}345{,}420}$$

$$\dot{m} = 9.35 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to more practical units**

$$\dot{m} = 9.35 \times 10^{-7} \times 3600 = 3.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/h} = 3.37 \text{ g/h}$$

**Step 7 — Verdict**

A decay of 4 kPa in 10 minutes is **acceptable** for a shop-air pressurized cooling system (industry threshold is typically ≤ 2 kPa in 5 minutes; here it is 2 kPa in 5 minutes, borderline-pass).

## 3.2 Refrigerant Leak Rate Estimation (g/year)

For AC systems, the **AHRI Standard 700** defines permissible leak rate as a percentage of charge per year:

$$\text{Leak Rate} \left[\frac{\%}{\text{year}}\right] = \frac{\Delta m}{m_{\text{charge}}} \times \frac{365 \times 24}{t_{\text{elapsed}}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta m$ is the measured mass loss, $m_{\text{charge}}$ is the full refrigerant charge, and $t_{\text{elapsed}}$ is the observation time in hours.

> **Example:** An AC system holds 600 g of R-134a. After 30 days, 18 g has been lost.
> $$\text{Leak Rate} = \frac{18}{600} \times \frac{8760}{720} \times 100 = 3.66\%/\text{year}$$
> Industry target: ≤ 1.5% per year. **Action required — repair.**

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Which Test for Which Symptom?)

| Symptom | Suspected System | First-Choice Test | Second-Choice Test | Likely Repair |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Oil stain under engine at front | Front crank seal / oil pan | Visual + UV dye | Pressure test with smoke | Replace seal/gasket |
| Coolant level drops, no external drip | Head gasket / heater core | Combustion-leak test (chemical block tester) | Pressure test cap off | Head gasket / heater core |
| White sweet exhaust smoke | Coolant in combustion | Block test (chemical) | Compression + leak-down | Head gasket |
| Hard cold-start, rough idle | Intake vacuum leak | Smoke test | Propane enrichment (legacy) | Replace gasket / hose |
| AC not cooling, oily film | Refrigerant leak | Electronic sniffer | UV dye + blacklight | O-ring / condenser |
| Soft brake pedal, no warning | Brake fluid leak | Visual + pressure test | None — repair immediately | Caliper / line |
| Diesel knock, power loss | Injector O-ring | Visual + return-line quantity test | Pressure test | Copper washer + O-ring |
| Soot at exhaust joint | Exhaust leak | Visual + soap water | Smoke test | Donut gasket / clamp |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three methods used for detecting leaks in an automotive air-conditioning (AC) system. Mention the working principle of one method.**

**Model Answer:**
1. *UV Dye Fluorescent Test* — A fluorescent dye is added to the refrigerant. A UV-A blacklight (365 nm) reveals the leak as a yellow-green glow.
2. *Electronic Refrigerant Leak Detector (Sniffer)* — Uses a heated sensor (diode or infrared) that responds specifically to HFC refrigerants like R-134a.
3. *Soapy Water Test* — A 1:1 soap-water solution applied to joints produces bubbles at the leak point under system pressure.
4. *Nitrogen Pressure Test* — The system is charged with dry nitrogen at the operating pressure and observed for pressure decay.
5. *Bubble Immersion Test* — The component is submerged in a water bath while internally pressurized; escaping gas forms visible bubbles.

*(Principle of UV Dye Test explained above.)* **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an external and an internal engine oil leak with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | External Leak | Internal Leak |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Definition | Oil escapes the engine to the outside environment | Oil migrates into an adjacent system (coolant, fuel, combustion) |
| Detection | Visible drip, stain, oil smell | Symptomatic — oil in coolant, blue exhaust smoke, high consumption |
| Example | Valve cover gasket failure | Oil passing into coolant through a faulty head gasket |
| Urgency | Moderate | High — can lead to catastrophic failure |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**a)** Classify the various types of leaks encountered in an automobile. With a neat sketch describe the construction and working of a **ultrasonic leak detector**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain in detail the **Pressure-Decay Test** procedure for an automotive cooling system. Derive the leak-rate equation from the ideal gas law and assess the acceptability of a system that drops from 110 kPa (gauge) to 102 kPa (gauge) in 5 minutes. Assume $V = 10$ L, $T = 30 °C$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101$ kPa, and $R = 287$ J/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Classification of Leaks:**

1. *By Fluid State:* Liquid (oil, coolant, fuel, brake fluid); Gas (refrigerant, exhaust, intake air, compressed air).
2. *By Visibility:* External (visible), Internal (cross-system migration), Hidden/Intermittent.
3. *By Severity:* Seepage, Drip, Running, Catastrophic.
4. *By Origin:* Gasket/seal failure, hose/line rupture, threaded joint loosening, component crack, corrosion perforation.

**Ultrasonic Leak Detector — Construction & Working:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Leak Source] -->|40 kHz Ultrasound| B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Preamplifier]
    C --> D[Band-Pass Filter 38 to 42 kHz]
    D --> E[Amplifier with AGC]
    E --> F[Demodulator Heterodyne]
    F --> G[Audio Amplifier]
    G --> H[Headphone or Speaker]
    F --> I[Bar Graph LED Display]
    E --> J[Castor or Parabolic Concentrator]
    J --> B
```

*Construction:* A **piezoelectric transducer** housed in a probe, focused by a **parabolic concentrator** ("shotgun" attachment) for direction finding; a **castor attachment** (rubber cone) for pinpointing small orifices. A heterodyning circuit shifts the ultrasonic signal into the audible range.

*Working:* Pressurized gas escaping a small orifice generates broadband noise with a strong component at 38–42 kHz. The transducer converts this to an electrical signal, the **heterodyne mixer** beats it down to 200–1000 Hz, and the operator listens via headphones while scanning the area. The **bar-graph LED** gives an objective indication of intensity for logging. Sensitivity: ≈ 0.05 mm orifice at 0.2 MPa from 5 m distance.

**(7 marks awarded: 3 for classification, 4 for ultrasonic detector)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Procedure for Pressure-Decay Test of Cooling System:**

1. Ensure the engine is cool. Open the expansion tank cap.
2. Connect a **radiator pressure tester** to the filler neck adapter.
3. Pump the hand-pump to bring system to the **radiator cap rated pressure** (typically 100–150 kPa gauge).
4. Close the pump valve and start the timer.
5. Monitor the gauge for 5 to 10 minutes.
6. Record initial pressure $P_0$ and final pressure $P_f$.
7. Visually inspect all joints, hoses, and the radiator surface for seepage.

**Step 2 — Convert to Absolute Pressures**

$$P_0 = 110 + 101 = 211 \text{ kPa} = 211{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_f = 102 + 101 = 203 \text{ kPa} = 203{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$$

$$V = 10 \text{ L} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3$$

$$t = 5 \text{ min} = 300 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Leak-Rate Equation**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{V (P_0 - P_f)}{R \, T \, t}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{0.010 \times (211{,}000 - 203{,}000)}{287 \times 303.15 \times 300}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{0.010 \times 8000}{26{,}104{,}215}$$

$$\dot{m} = 3.065 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} \approx 11.0 \text{ g/h}$$

**Step 4 — Acceptability Assessment**

Pressure drop = $211 - 203 = 8 \text{ kPa}$ in 5 minutes.

Industry threshold for a cooling system pressure-decay test is **≤ 2 kPa in 5 minutes**.

**Result:** The system is **NOT acceptable**. The leak is significant and must be located and repaired.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 procedure, 3 derivation/numbers, 2 verdict)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**a)** Explain the **UV Dye Fluorescent Method** of leak detection. Mention the steps involved in the procedure and list two systems where it is most commonly used. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **smoke test** for detecting intake manifold vacuum leaks in a petrol engine. Compare the smoke test with the **propane enrichment method** (legacy). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Principle:**
A fluorescent dye (e.g., **Tracerline TP-3400**, Spectroline TP-390) is chemically compatible with the host fluid and remains suspended. When illuminated by a **UV-A source at 365 nm**, the dye fluoresces yellow-green, providing a visual marker at the leak point.

**Procedure:**

1. Identify the system to be tested (e.g., AC, engine oil, coolant).
2. Inject the **pre-measured dye dose** through a service port, filler cap, or via the oil filler.
3. Run the system for **15–20 minutes** (or one full duty cycle) to circulate the dye.
4. Switch off and allow a 5-minute settling period.
5. Scan the engine bay, underbody, and component joints with a **UV-A blacklight (365 nm)** in a darkened area.
6. The leak appears as a **bright yellow-green glow**; trace it upstream to the source.
7. Mark the source, repair, then **flush and recharge** the system as required.
8. Verify by re-scanning with the UV light — no glow should be visible.

**Commonly Used In:**
- Air-conditioning refrigerant circuits (R-134a, R-1234yf).
- Engine lubricating systems.
- Power-steering systems.
- Diesel fuel return-line leak tracing.

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Smoke Test — Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smoke Machine 12V] --> B[Air Pump or Shop Air 0.05 MPa]
    B --> C[Mineral Oil Vaporiser]
    C --> D[Output Hose to Intake]
    D --> E[Vacuum Port Throttle Body]
    E --> F[Intake Manifold]
    F --> G[Engine Cylinders and Seals]
    G -.Leak Path 1.- H1[Brake Booster Hose]
    G -.Leak Path 2.- H2[Vacuum Hose to MAP Sensor]
    G -.Leak Path 3.- H3[Intake Gasket Edge]
    G -.Leak Path 4.- H4[Crankcase Vent PCV]
    H1 --> I[Visible White Smoke]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

**Procedure:**
1. Block the air inlet at the air filter housing or throttle body.
2. Connect the smoke machine outlet to a vacuum port (often the brake booster line).
3. Activate the machine; it generates dense **white mineral-oil smoke** that fills the intake tract under low pressure.
4. The smoke escapes from any leak point — gaskets, hoses, throttle body, PCV line, EGR passages.
5. Inspect all accessible joints; mark the leak.
6. Repair, then re-test for confirmation.

**Comparison: Smoke Test vs. Propane Enrichment (Legacy Method):**

| Parameter | Smoke Test | Propane Enrichment |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Principle | Visualise smoke escaping at leak | Enrich air-fuel mix; RPM rises at leak |
| Equipment | Smoke machine + UV lamp (optional) | Propane cylinder + hose + regulator |
| Safety | Very safe (non-flammable mineral oil vapour) | Flammable — workshop safety hazard |
| Leakage at small orifices | Excellent — visible wisp | May not be detected |
| Localisation precision | High — exact point visible | Low — only general region |
| Modern usage | Industry standard (workshops, OEMs) | Largely obsolete |
| Cost | Moderate | Low |

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing gauge vs. absolute pressure** in pressure-decay calculations — always convert (add atmospheric) before substituting in $PV = mRT$.
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (isothermal, ideal gas, fixed volume). Examiners award a mark for the assumption list.
> 3. **Writing "vacuum leak" as a "gas leak"** — they are conceptually different; vacuum = ingress of unmetered air from outside, not loss of internal gas.
> 4. **In UV dye questions**, students often forget the **post-repair flushing step** — losing one mark.
> 5. **In smoke test questions**, students forget to **seal the air intake** to build pressure differential — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing valves with "end"** as a node label in Mermaid diagrams — KTU smart-scanners flag this as an error. Use descriptive node IDs (e.g., `step1`, `valveA`).
> 7. **Missing the "Why"** — KTU expects one-line rationale after every step in a 14-mark answer (e.g., "we convert to absolute pressure *because* the ideal gas law requires absolute values").
> 8. **Skipping the units** in the final leak-rate result — examiners may deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Leak Detection and Repair**

- **Core Principle:** LDAR follows a **Locate → Diagnose → Repair → Verify** cycle. Always choose the **least invasive** detection method first.

- **Leak Classification Axes:** State (liquid/gas), visibility (external/internal/hidden), severity (seepage/drip/running/catastrophic), origin (gasket/hose/threading/crack/corrosion).

- **High-Frequency Leak Points:** Valve-cover gasket, oil-pan gasket, water-pump seal, head gasket, brake caliper seal, AC O-rings, exhaust manifold gasket, intake manifold gasket.

- **Detection Method Selection (Memory Aid "V-P-S-U-D-S-A"):**
  1. **V**isual
  2. **P**ressure-decay
  3. **S**moke
  4. **U**V-dye
  5. **D**ye-penetrant / soapy water
  6. **S**niffer (electronic, ultrasonic)
  7. **A**coustic/teardown

- **Key Formula (must memorise):**
  $$\dot{m} = \frac{V (P_0 - P_f)}{R \, T \, t}$$

- **Key Constants:** $R_{\text{air}} = 287$ J/kg·K; $R_{\text{R-134a}} \approx 96$ J/kg·K; $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$.

- **Acceptable Decay Thresholds:**
  - Cooling system: ≤ 2 kPa in 5 minutes.
  - AC refrigerant leak: ≤ 1.5 %/year (AHRI 700).
  - Engine oil consumption: ≤ 0.5 L / 1000 km.

- **Repair Hierarchy:** Retorque → Reseal (thread sealant) → Replace gasket/seal → Replace hose → Replace component.

- **Post-Repair Verification:** Always re-run the same test that detected the leak; document results for warranty/compliance.

- **Safety Notes (frequently asked in viva):** Always recover refrigerant before opening an AC system; never smoke-test a fuel tank (explosion risk); use eye protection during pressure testing.

- **GWP Awareness:** R-134a has a GWP of **1430**; R-1234yf ≈ **4** — workshops must comply with F-Gas regulation (India: Ozone Depleting Substances Regulation, 2000 & amendments).
